3hk7nO_q0a8https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=3hk7nO_q0a8597 | Federal Budget 2026: Are YOU a Winner or Loser? (Property Edition)The Property Couch Podcast

Description
🚨 The Federal Budget 2026 has landed… and for this special reaction episode, Bryce is back on the couch to unpack the biggest property tax shake-ups Australia has seen in decades.
 
Together Ben and Bryce are breaking down the sweeping changes announced around negative gearing, capital gains tax, rental supply and more. 

The big questions:
• Will this actually improve affordability?
• Could rents rise even further?
• Which markets are most exposed?
• And what should investors do now?
 
Listen now 👉 https://thepropertycouch.com.au/ep597-federal-budget-2026 

*TIMESTAMPS*
0:00 – 597 | Federal Budget 2026: Are YOU a Winner or Loser? (Property Edition)
2:05 – “The Most Important & Ambitious Budget In Decades”
5:00 – Negative Gearing Changes Explained – What Happens From 12 May 2026
9:33 – CGT Changes Explained – Why Property Investing Just Got More Complex
11:59 – Emergency Webinar Announcement – Regional Risks, Buyer Depth & Market Behaviour
15:19 – “Location Now Does 90% Of The Heavy Lifting” 
17:30 – Why Rents Could Rise Further: The Unintended Consequences Begin
19:52 – The New Wave Of Property Spruikers & Why New Builds Could Dominate The Pitch
23:12 – New Build Incentives Explained 
26:04 – Treasury’s Predictions vs Reality: Will Prices Really Fall?
30:14 – What’s The Pathway For Young People Now? 
33:16 – Why Investors REALLY Buy Property –
37:35 – The Politics Behind The Budget: Will These Changes Actually Fix Housing?
41:20 – Listener Questions Answered: Grandfathering, New Builds & Investment Strategy
53:05 – So… What Should Investors Do NOW? Ben & Bryce’s Practical Takeaways
57:10 — Commercial property, SMSFs and companies: the three structures to watch
59:27 – Bryce’s New Buyer’s Agent Initiative 
01:04:03 — When regional markets collapse, the cowboys disappear — and someone else pays
1:07:05 – Final Thoughts:  Don’t Panic, Understand The New Rules First

👍 Like this video if it helped!
🔔 Subscribe for weekly insights on property, finance & money management
📲 Join 19,000+ investors on Moorr: https://moorr.com.au
💡 Access free resources via MyKnowledge: https://moorr.com.au/myknowledge

#thepropertycouch #federalbudget2026 #negativegearing #cgt #jimchalmers #newbuilds
The podcast discusses the 2026 Australian federal budget's property changes, especially negative gearing (limited to new builds from 12 May 2026) and abolition of the 50% CGT discount (replaced with cost base indexation). Existing properties are grandfathered for negative gearing but CGT changes apply to post-valuation gains. Key claims: these measures act as a tax on lower-income earners, will increase rents, reduce rental supply in established areas, and penalize investors in shallow regional markets. Location's importance for capital growth is elevated to 90%. The principal place of residence remains tax-advantaged. Webinar on 26 May 2026 is announced. Criticism targets speculative trading and lack of investment philosophy among some buyers agents. Speakers: Bryce Holdway and Ben (co-hosts), with mentions of Jim Chalmers, Dan White, Evan Lucas, David Robinson.deepseek-v4-flash

You know, you go to our neighbors with Singapore, you can even go to China, you can go to the US, even our neighbors over the ditch. They have much more um enticing tax system to invite uh investment into our country. JT3OzDKrucUhttps://www.youtube.com/watch?v=JT3OzDKrucU&t=298sCombine Skills and MCP to Close the Context Gap — Pedro Rodrigues, SupabaseAI Engineer
UCLKPca3kwwd-B59HNr-_lvA		uploaded=2026-05-15T00:00:00.000Z
duration=18:26
views=4,569
likes=103
transcript langs=
tags=ai, ai engineer, ai engineering, software development, tech, startups, software architecture, machine learning
Description
Agents working with Postgres will confidently create a view over a table with row-level security enabled and silently bypass that security in the process. Not because they can't reason. Because they don't know about the security_invoker flag, and nobody told them. Pedro Rodrigues from Supabase ran this exact test: same agent, same task, MCP alone versus MCP plus a skill. The one without the skill shipped a query that exposed data it shouldn't have.

The talk covers what Supabase learned building their agent skill from scratch: critical security rules go directly in skill.md because agents will reliably skip reference files, skills should point to living documentation rather than duplicate it, and opinionated workflow guidance matters more than comprehensive coverage. Their evals ran across Claude and GPT models in three conditions and the result was unanimous. Skills without MCP underperform. MCP without skills misses environment-specific constraints. Together they close the gap that makes agents unreliable on real production systems.

Speaker info:
- https://x.com/rodriguespn23
- https://www.linkedin.com/in/pedro-neves-rodrigues/
- https://github.com/Rodriguespn
Pedro from Supabase presents lessons from writing a Supabase agent skill, covering three core principles: don't duplicate documentation (point agents to live docs), put critical information (like RLS security checklist) directly in skill.md because agents skip reference files, and be opinionated about optimized workflows (e.g., direct DDL on dev/staging, then use advisor before creating migration file). Live evals on Braintrust (vs Claude Code Opus/Sonnet 4.6, Codex GPT 5.4/5.4 mini) show skills + MCP outperform MCP alone or baseline. Also discusses exposing docs via SSH for agent file-navigation, and challenges of skill distribution. Announces Supabase agent skill release.deepseek-v4-flash

So I know that writing a skill sounds simple, but it can be very complex especially when you have complex product like Superbase. For starters, I'm Pedro. I'm an AI tooling engineer at Superbase. I'm an MCP enthusiast AI in general. Feel free to connect me on LinkedIn and I'm also a co-founder of the Lisbon AI Week in Lisbon. We'll be on late October this year. And if we're talking about me at the moment, I usually prefer doing this on dark darker darker mode. I don't know how many of you prefer dark mode over light mode. The majority. I thought so. So let's do this presentation on dark mode instead. So we I think we can all agree that agents are already smart enough, right? They can do very they're very capable of doing mundane tasks by themselves. But when you present a task about something that they've haven't seen yet or you've updated since they were trained like your product for example, they need the right guidance. For for example, Superbase we noticed that they would usually either miss some some security pitfalls that we have like row level security instructions that they have to set to to basically not expose your app. They could just usually operate on stale knowledge on their training data and they're very lazy to and very stubborn to admit that they don't know and they do need to find fresh information. And also we would like to guide them on specific workflows that we think are the most optimized for agents on our products. So for starters, how many of you know or have written a skill before? Okay, so what I'm going to say is probably not new for the most of you, but just to get an introduction on skills. So skills are folders containing instructions, scripts and resources that agents discover right? Progressively they discover. This is the main selling point of skills and they they have this envelope called front matter where they have the name and the description. This is how the agent is going to decide when to load the skill. Then they have the actual instructions inside the file the main file called skill.md and then optional bundled resources like scripts to perform actions or reference files that the main file can reference for more information that doesn't have to be loaded immediately to context. So we tested out at Superbase we experienced giving the same agent in this case was Claude Sonnet 4.6 the same prompt for a simple task. Like we had an app like a collaboration collaborative app and we wanted to create this new SQL view, right? On top of table that already had row level security enabled. So the users could only see the information that belonged to them. We gave we gave them just the the MCP on one condition and the MCP plus the the agents the agent skills. And the result was well the expected. If you don't know in Postgres, if you create a skill over a table on top of a table that has RLS enabled, if you don't explicitly pass that that flag over there the security invoker equals true, it will bypass the RLS. So basically the view will expose data that is not this exposed by default on the on the table. So the agent with the skill with the knowledge was able to get this this information implemented correctly and safely while the one that only had access to the integration to the to the MCP tool did not. So for this we decided well just like this we wanted to enable agents to know how to work correctly with with Superbase. So we decided to we're announced actually announcing today this this Superbase agent skill that I've been working on on this couple couple past of months and to make things official I'm actually going to try something. Wait. I'm going to live tweet it on stage. So it's live. All right. So what exactly is this skill about and what lessons can I share with you? So if you're building a skill for your product, you can build one like we did and happy to discuss the all details like this is free text text and we haven't achieved a a standard for it. So happy to to chat about it later. So to break down on some principles that we converged to. The first one is that don't duplicate information. Yeah, like treat skills as a document documentation for yourself and you will not duplicate your documentation, right? You already have documentation on your product. Just point the agent to it to the most up-to-date. You have to be very stubborn with the model to go to ask them to go search the the web or your documentation. Provide the guidance. Tell where to and how to find the documentation, but be very persistent with it to go for it. We also did a run a little experiments and this is still well as I said an experiment and yet to be it's we're still figuring out the details, but we also announced quite recently and you can see it can read it on our blog. We're we basically exposing our documentation through SSH. The main reason behind it is that the agents can now look for the documentation like it was a file system. So they're very familiar with file systems in general are navigating them finding files and information on using Linux based tools. If we expose if you give them this interface to also do this but remotely, our premise is that they will have easy ease to to navigate the the agent. So would also would love after the talk or during the the conference to see and to hear your opinions on this idea. The second principle that I get that I get I have for you is that if something can get skipped it will be skipped. What I mean by this is that besides newer information on searching online, so agents like fetching information online or tool calling it's expensive for for agents. So they mostly default to their training data. The same is true for reference files. We've noticed that even if when the the agent loaded the skill, it will even if it had reference files there, it will be very lazy to load them. And even if it loaded one reference file, if your problem requires more than one the information that it's more than in more than one file, it will most likely will not load two files, right? It's almost impossible and not not even starting on three or four. So you have to be very critical about what you put on your skill.md file from the beginning. You put information that is likely not to change like in our case a security checklist about Superbase that we didn't really wanted the agent to miss at all. So we decided this cannot be on a reference file. We actually started by putting on a reference file and he usually missed it. So we put it on the skill itself. So if you have any information that the agent can just not miss and like defines your product, go to the skill.md file. Do not afford it to put on on a reference file. And lastly, the third principle that I have for you when writing a product skill is to be opinionated. You know your product the best. You know how to work with it. You know how your users or you should know how your users are using it. Don't be afraid of guiding the agents on what on workflows that you think are the most effective when working with your product. In our case for example, managing a database schema, right? I have actually haven't asked this and should have in the the beginning. How many of you know Superbase and what Superbase does? Okay, for So for the ones who who don't know, we're basically a backend as a service. We provide a storage database, authentication and other uh features that you would need to create a back end out of the box. So, we provide as I said we provide a database and the agent can interact and manipulate your your schema right? We found that this for our platform was the best workflow for the agents to efficiently manage the schema. So, in this case run uh direct DDL operations like change the schema freely on your um development or staging database. Once you're happy about it, we provide an advisor to our basically linked to give any uh security or performance issues that the database could have, fix them, and only then create the migration file. So, this will prevent the agent to create a migration file every time he changes the schema. We found this to be the best um workflow to manage the schema. So, for us it should be in the um in the skill when working with with Superbase. How we tested this skill? So, we're living a very interesting times where we now can test free tests. We we can now test um documents. We can now test documentation, right? Uh this would be uh completely bonkers to think uh years ago. Now, I've basically been testing a markdown file. And how did we do this? Uh through evals. Uh so, for those of you who don't know evals, evaluations for a short for evaluations, are basically uh tests that you can run mostly like you would run uh on your CI, uh but now instead of evaluating code you're evaluating an agent and LLM and its behavior, what tools it's it's calling, what's the it's reasoning, right? And so, we ran on an early stage we ran a set of six specific scenarios for Superbase. So, ongoing Superbase projects in different uh in different scenarios, right? And we ran it against four different uh agents from two vendors um in three test conditions. Uh so, I think we can conclude that it's pretty unanimous that the skills were actually improving the performance the test completeness score uh because they were providing the right guidance to to the agent. So, if you can take something from this talk uh when building a skill for your product is that points for your single source of truth. Point to your documentation basically. Be opinionated. You know your product. Don't be afraid to to show it. And start minimal. P2-rXTmb9hghttps://www.youtube.com/watch?v=P2-rXTmb9hg&t=391s&pp=ugUEEgJlbg%3D%3DFederal Budget Reaction with Economist Chris RichardsonMark Bouris
UCPX01K1JgNS5pyzUtwtCtsQ		uploaded=2026-05-13T00:00:00.000Z
duration=1:04:37
views=62,349
likes=1,545
transcript langs=
tags=
Description
Chris Richardson is one of Australia’s most quoted economists and the founder of Rich Insight, bringing decades of expertise from his time at Deloitte Access Economics to decode complex fiscal policy. 

Chris and I might not agree on every aspect of this budget, but that is what makes this conversation worth having. 

In this analysis of Australia’s latest budget, Richardson reveals why our living standards have barely grown over the past decade while other OECD nations have surged ahead by 17%. He dissects the real economic effects of the budget on inflation, interest rates, and the Reserve Bank’s delicate balancing act. Richardson also exposes the “off-budget” spending that keeps billions in government expenditure hidden from public scrutiny and explains why housing affordability will not be solved by capital gains tax changes alone.

00:00:00 — MB's thoughts on Federal Budget's Real Economic Impact
00:12:44 — Why Australia's Living Standards Have Barely Grown
00:16:27 — Productivity: The Key to Long-Term Economic Prosperity
00:20:13 — How Government Spending Impacts Inflation and the RBA
00:29:14 — Supply, Not Tax, Solves the Housing Affordability Crisis
00:41:22 — Unpacking Off-Budget Government Spending
00:54:35 — Immigration: Economic Benefits Versus Housing Challenges
00:58:11 — Chris Richardson's Advice for Future Budget Reform
Chris Richardson discusses the Australian federal budget 2026-27, focusing on its economic impact rather than individual tax effects. He critiques the 'intergenerational equity' narrative as political, arguing housing supply (not tax policy) is the core issue. Key claims: negative gearing and CGT changes will not help young buyers; older investors will outcompete for new housing. Richardson highlights stalled prosperity growth due to weak productivity, off-budget items (NBN, Snowy Hydro) obscuring fiscal reality, and a mismatch between immigration and housing supply. He calls for regulatory reform (e.g., national construction code) and genuine tax reform to boost disposable income. Overall, he views the budget as a reform attempt but insufficient to address Australia's low prosperity trajectory.deepseek-v4-flash

I just finished podcast with uh Chris Richardson. Um he's the former um economist at Deote um access um and uh now he's he runs his own business called Rich Insight and he's a he's a great economist but he's very technical very very technical and the reason I wanted to do that podcast with Chris is because I want someone to unpack the um treasurer's budget for 2026 as it's going to affect 27 and beyond more in an economic sense like how is it going to affect the Australian economy Not how it affects individuals being affected by the capital gains tax. Not how it affects people relative to uh losing their ability to um deduct the interest they pay to the banks relative to the rent they paid. That is negative gearing. It's not about not about any of those. It's about actually unpacking the fiscal policy of the budget relative to how it affects inflation potentially and therefore of course all of you know how inflation that is uh will be managed by the Reserve Bank in relation to interest rates. So what I'm trying to find out is will interest rates go up after this budget in an a general economic sense. I'm not I didn't talk to Chris Richardson about capital gains tax per se andor negative gearing or or tax on trust by the way. Those affect individuals they are and they were designed by the way and Chris will say it um that they're going to have very little impact on affordability for housing. They're going to have very little impact on new supply of new housing for people to buy this budget. The narrative around this budget is called intergenerational equity. In other words, looking at the problem that exists for younger people and let's call people under 35 just by you know just for the sake of it. And actually there is actually is an intergenerational inequity. In other words, the older generation own a lot of assets and they've enjoyed high price rises through all unusual periods post GS uh uh GFC during and postco. Um but they've also enjoyed high pri prices or higher prices relative to what they paid for maybe 15 or 20 years ago. They've enjoyed it because the government has not kept up supply. to the levels of immigration and to the levels of population growth. And Chris Richardson will talk about that. Supply is the key here. It's not that people got rich at the cost of people who didn't get rich. It's not that older generation have sucked all the dollars out of the system. It's because we have not supplied enough properties into the market relative to the demand for those properties. that is you know young people becoming of age and or andor immigration or both we just this government has not supplied enough properties not just the federal government the federal and the state government and the councils and Chris goes on to say to some extent it's also about us as individuals because us as individuals a lot of us don't want any new things to be built in our street because you know we worked hard our whole lives to buy this house which we want to live to leave to our children or our grandchildren whatever the case may be or we want to live there till the day we Why? Cuz health has improved. We don't have to be put in a home or a hospital, which is fine. But we should be doing, the government should be doing is finding new green fields and opening up new areas for younger people to move into. But the narrative is the thing that's wrong. The treasurer's narrative is wrong. The treasurer saying this budget is designed to help younger people is politics. because Samarus sat right here last week and said exactly that. He didn't say it was wrong, but what he said was governments, all governments, all those people he advises at least know that there is a massive vote that they're missing out on that's going to the Greens that's sitting between young people 18 through to 35 cuz they don't like either party. Some of them may end up moving over to One Nation, but there is a huge amount of people sitting there. So this narrative around this budget is directed at trying to make those individuals who vote for governments in the future and there will be an election in 28. Oh guys, we in our budget, this budget and future budgets, we are looking after you. We are trying to mend this intergenerational inequity. In other words, we're trying to get you into a house. We're trying to give you what your parents and grandparents have got. Now what I'm annoyed about is that that narrative is wrong. This budget will not help younger people get into a home. By the way, the very people who are supposed to have caused this intergenerational inequity, the older people, they've been protected because effectively they've been grandfathered on any gains that they've made to date. Their 50% discount on the capital gain that they're going to make still remains. They will still get that discount. So the government didn't want to punish them because they're worried they might lose their vote. So So those people are fine. They're fine. But nothing's happened down the other end of the end of the board. Like the, you know, the books have been pushed down one end of the shop, but the other end's empty. So you young people are not going to get anything out of this budget. Nothing's going to happen. Nothing's going to change for you. Interest rates aren't going to go down, so you can afford to borrow more money. Um the uh banks aren't going to lend you any more money. um the government's not going to give you a deposit. No one's going to supply more housing so that there's more competition in terms of those people are trying to buy these properties. In actual fact, I think this budget is going to create intergenerational inequity. And I tell you why. The exemption, in other words, those people who still be able to get negative gearing for new purchases post today or post last night applies to new housing. So, anybody who's got coin and they want to invest in the property market and get negative gearing, which could be you as a younger person, but anybody cuz that's avail this is available to everybody across the board. An older person might say, well, instead of buying an existing property now and renting it out and getting negative gearing on that property and, you know, getting the capital gains tax discount that I know I'm not going to get that anymore, what I'm going to do is I'm going to go and buy new housing. Anything that's new, I'm going to buy that. I'll put in my 20% deposit because, you know, I've saved up a deposit. I'm retired. Well, I've been saving it up. I'll borrow the money from the bank. I'll negatively gear it. Those people are now exempt. They get the negative gearing. And when I go to buy it, I can afford to pay more than the young person. Those people will out compete young people every single time. So, as usual, government involvement in an asset class usually all it ever does, this is my experience, this is all I've ever seen. It's just happened recently in the 5% deposit government guarantee scheme. All it does is increase the price of the asset which then in a rising interest market interest rate market right we are right now puts that beyond the reach of a younger person. So all of a sudden those properties those new properties will be bought by the same people who've been buying properties for the last 10 20 years. They will control that market. They'll be looking for people to rent that because they're not going to live there. They own the big house somewhere else. They're going to put on the market for rent. When someone young goes along to rent that property, they're going to pay the market price cuz you know people have paid a lot of money for it and they want to get a return. Then that will lead into their ability to save a deposit for the future. So this this system this these initiatives by in this budget as far as I'm concerned and I'm going to call it out are not going to work. In fact, they're going to have the opposite effect like they always do. It might have a a a positive effect for a couple of months, but soon as everyone gets in the market and starts working this arbitrage out, they'll take advantage of it. And by the way, you don't blame him for taking advantage of it because it's a free market. People will invest their money where they can and where the demand is that they'll push the prices up, especially if the supply is low and the supply will remain low. We're not going to get any great supply. what we should be doing, what governments could be doing, they could be saying, you know what, if you're an employee or you're running a business and let's say you earn less than 150 grand where you're paying a marginal tax rate of 30 35%. young person or any person if you elect to into a scheme, what we'll allow you to do is only pay 20% marginal tax rate on that 150 grand. In other words, you can save 10 or 15%. But the condition of that saving of tax is that money must be invested into your super fund alongside your compulsory superanuation that your employee pays. But you are investing this money now not the So the employee is going to invest his 12%. You are alongside that employee going to invest this money into your super fund and you the person earning a less than 150,000 you can access that money over time at any time if you choose but only if it is to buy a house or a dwelling that money will be taxree in your super it's deductible to you. you get a a deduction for it. You're only and you've made the election. So, you're only paying 20% tax. So, you're saving money. If you don't touch it, it sits there forever. You can pick it up when you turn 65, but the only time you can access this money is to buy a house is extra money. Now, maybe this is, you know, got flaws in it from a tax point of view and there could be, you know, Treasury will run the ruler over it if they ever did. Um, I'm sure there's flaws in it, but point being here is this is the sort of stuff that I would have liked to seen in the budget. this sort of real tax reform that actually helps out the younger generation, solve this inequity problem. And the only way you're going to do it is to reduce tax, give them more net money so it allows them to build a deposit which allows them them to go into the market and compete with a full deposit. That's what we need to see. That's what I would like to see in this budget. So, you know, um I'm sorry, Treasurer and Prime Minister. Um I don't cop it. I think, um this is unfair. I don't think it's achieved the thing that your narrative keeps talking about. Um um no doubt you're going to have lots of arguments with the Greens about this cuz they're going to say the taxing that you've put in is not enough. The exemptions that you've applied are shouldn't be there. So, it's going to be hard to get the support of everybody. Hopefully, you know, we don't have some sort of double dissolution. This uh budget doesn't go through. Yeah, Jim, it was courageous, mate. Um, but at the end of the day, it was courageous because I know I think I can tell you feel that this has not been accepted and it just didn't go the way everybody thought it was going to go. And this is a tough period. We got interest rate risings. We got the Iran war doesn't look like it's going to get solved too quick. We got um the price of crude oil is going to be a drama. We got petrol pricing, you know, or or fuel out the Bowser and everywhere else in the country causing inflation. We got potentially more interest rate rises. We're in a tough period. And what I was hoping for was something that actually will help this country grow. And by the way, in this podcast, Chris Richardson talks about growth and he talks about prosperity. And one thing that Chris is a bit disappointed in is that this is not a prosperity budget. And the only way to make things more affordable is put more money in people's pockets. Not by government handouts, but by the country growing prosperity, which is what we need. Anyway, enjoy the podcast. Uh Chris is a very knowledgeable guy, one of the best economists in the country. It it does get pretty deep and pretty uh into the minutia, but like if you're interested in understanding how a budget actually works and the economics of all this stuff, it's worth a listen to. >> Chris Richardson, welcome to Straight Talk mate. >> Many many thanks for having me, Mark. What we thought would be good today to talk to someone of your expertise about is the economic effects of the budget or perhaps even the economic objectives of the budget and then and what do you think will be the economic effects of the budget in terms of it being executed objectively. So obviously a big worry to our audiences and you know Australians generally is how do you see this budget looking for the year ahead and perhaps periods after but just let's talk about the year ahead relative to inflation. How does it look? >> Um let me just step back uh for a moment because the so the federal budget is about a quarter of all the money uh in Australia goes in and back out. Um, you can kind of think of it as as is true. You know, every uh family, every community, every nation has a has a social compact. The federal budget's a bit of a social compact. I we tax workers, we tax businesses, we spend it on the young and the old and the sick and the poor and defense forces and and the like. I tend to think of any budget, any social compact having two big aims uh and both of them are part of the backstory uh to this particular um budget. So two aims are to make people better off you know to to raise the standard of living uh over time sustainably uh so the sort of prosperity aim um but there's also a fairness aim as we uh as a group become more prosperous uh over time uh are we sharing those gains well and the true backdrop uh to this budget you know I I don't necessarily think it is the the inflation challenge at the moment of Reserve Bank. I don't necessarily think it is uh Iran uh and and the war in the the Middle East. I would step back a little further and say across our lifetimes, Australia has been a magnificent prosperity success story. Uh we spent a long time with our standard of living uh moving up um at times including decades faster than uh you saw in the US. But in the past uh decade our living standards have barely grown. If you look across the rich world uh as a whole or the average citizen in in that sort of rich world club the OECD their their living standards have gone up um about 17% uh in in the past decade. Now that's their income. Um but it's disposable income. You know it's inflation adjusted. It's per person. uh in Australia, it's less than a third of that. And because we're not becoming more prosperous at the same rate that we used to do, we are getting grumpier with each other. You're seeing fights over the the pie, you know, again, a very um dominant part of this budget. Uh and and you are seeing um you know, everything from social media to our voting patterns. we are grumpier uh with each other and and it does worry me I you know I actually like a bunch of things in the budget but it worries me that it hasn't done enough to break us out of that low prosperity trajectory uh which means we may stuck uh stay stuck fighting over fairness um and that's not a great place to be. >> How does one measure our standard of living? So what is that? What is that measurement? Is it a universal one? >> I just use basic uh numbers coming out of the Bureau of Stats. Um the exact same numbers used in the exact same way by the OECD. They look across a bunch of countries. Uh what you are doing is taking your total income from everything from wages, salaries, dividend, interest, uh you know profits, however you're getting the rent. Um taking out two big things to make it disposable income. One is uh personal tax. Uh the other is interest payments. Uh and then inflation adjusting it over time, putting it on a uh per head basis. Uh and and at least at the index level, you can then compare across countries as well as across time. Um in in economics, there is one truly great oneliner. the the uh Nobel laurate Paul Krugman uh once said productivity isn't everything but in the long run it is almost uh everything. Australia stopped getting better at doing stuff about a decade ago. And when that happens, right, your your living standards don't increase. Uh and and that productivity fail is a prosperity fail. Uh is what's got us I think in many ways uh into this current um mess. >> Is the ultimate outcome very sensitive to tax rates or is it more about wage growth or is it what is it more about? Is or is it not more about anything? is about everything. >> Well, it is about something in particular. It is about wages relative to prices. So, you know, all nations uh have a personal tax take. Uh ours is relatively high uh and and uh you know, it has increased a bit uh over the longer term. But it's not personal tax that has been make or break uh for our lack of prosperity growth uh for a long time. It's wages haven't gone faster than prices. And people love to reach for all sorts of conspiracy theories around that. But it goes back to the productivity thing that I said. We are not um you know making great uh use of new technologies. We are not investing uh in stuff uh you know I don't mean the financial investments. I mean the you know the the road the rail the factory the computer the the whatever else. Um and that combination, you know, we're not using our skills as well as we could. We are not using um our our environ built environment uh out there as well as we could means that we are not advancing at the rate that a bunch of other nations are advancing. As you look across the OECD, uh we're still uh in terms of uh the increase in our living standards over the past decade, we're still uh beating the palms. uh and it always you know it's nice to do um but they are essentially bottom of the list you know and uh under US uh top of the list in terms of gains in its living standards uh and I do think Australia can and should do better our politicians by the way aren't make or break around a lot of that um but they can either stand in the way of our productivity gains our prosperity gains or they can uh help them along uh and and For some decades now, you know, we have been um in in a nation where our politicians have not challenged us as much as they should, haven't made us change as much as we should. Uh and and therefore we haven't had our political classes giving us a boot along uh on that prosperity front. >> It's it's very interesting. So um uh the RBA's mandate sort of also talks about prosperity of all Australians and also the welfare of all Australians and those two in some respects conflict. But we sort of understand when inflation is really high, we got to look after the welfare of everybody. Therefore, we put interest rates up. Um when uh inflation is nice and low, we need to grow or be prosperous and we put interest rates down. Pretty simple. Um that's very simple at least for me to understand when it comes to the government relative to their policies fiscal policy. So RBA's monetary policy for our audience but when it comes to fiscal policy when the big one is you know the the budget the big event is the budget does the government also look at those two aspects prosperity therefore growth measured by growth and welfare to be fair it does look at all that um but again you rais a really important point around the the backdrop you know at times people pretend And there is a magic wand, you know, well, we could all just be more prosperous if this, if that. Sometimes it's let's just, you know, raise everybody's wages, which uh at one stage Australia in the mid 1970s did that and of course we got a lot of inflation and we did not get any change in prosperity. Um there are all sorts of people out there who will pedal simple solutions for complex problems. If you look at the problem that the Reserve Bank has to juggle, it promises Australians that um over time it will stay on top of inflation. And what is inflation? It you know inflation you it crops up when we have too much money chasing too little stuff. when our spending runs ahead of the ability of the economy to produce the stuff that we're spending on or in in the economist jargon, demand is ahead of supply. Uh and that is a situation that we had before uh the Iran war in inflation had um you know it looked as though it was going down for a while but it had popped its head back up. Uh the Reserve Bank had brought out the hammer and and was whacking that nail and then this war happened. It is worth noting and you're talking about the budget and you're entirely right. Right. It is worth noting that what we are juggling in Australia in the near term is not that dissimilar to what we juggled back in 2022. In 2022, right, there was already inflation like we had before the Iran war and then Russia's invasion of Ukraine sent energy prices up. uh and and you got off the back of that combination a worsening uh in inflation and again we're getting that worsening uh in inflation now uh and governments so the uh I've mentioned the feds are about a quarter of um the spending in in Australia by the time you add in the states you're about two-fifths 40% of of all the spending in Australia um the the politicians essentially reacted to that by uh giving us money, right? You know, and lots of subsidies for this and that uh and and I suspect there was a certain amount of grinding of teeth from the Reserve Bank uh around that. Um because it sounds good, you know, in a bunch of cases they handed us money in ways that that reduced the measured inflation rate, but they still actually worsened inflation because they were giving us extra money uh at a time when there was already too much money chasing too little stuff. So if if I might be able to ask you then in that regard the Reserve Bank Governor in her post announcement press conference uh last week did say I can't remember exact words but I remember one of the words she used exactly she said Australians could be forgiven for for feeling poorer. Um and that sort of sort of puts a knife straight into you know how we feel about our standard of living. So we feel poorer. That goes right to the very heart of that. And she has been very um as you would expect to the government reserve bank or Federal Reserve of any uh country u been very careful not to be critical of the government relative to fiscal policy but has made some references to um increases um by government in spending. For example, the 250 bucks that they're going to give to everybody. Not that that's and by the way it's not coming into 2028 anyway but it doesn't matter. Um she has been sort of made some references to it and then the prime minister has made reference to this being a a budget which is with full of labor values. Um I don't quite know what that means but I could guess. I guess I can guess. Um, do you think in that in that context, do you think that this budget is one that is aimed at growth or constraint andor will it have an effect by increasing pressure on inflation by increasing private demand? >> Yeah. Yeah. Yeah. and and and you you are uh setting out I I thought of three tests uh for the budget. One just briefly if you like was insurance in the sense of um paying more for fuel really hurts but running out of fuel especially diesel our farmers feed the world our miners make us the money our transport sector moves everything that you know is our lifeblood. If we were to run out then that would be a real problem. are the you know the main task for the government right now uh is to make sure that we're getting supplies and that our supplies are secure. Uh the second one though and you talked about it inflation. Um because back a handful of years ago uh when when the Reserve Bank was taking money out of the economy uh governments and and the states were arguably worse than the Feds, governments were putting the money back in and and if inflation is too much money, chasing too little stuff, it was a real problem. Uh and we had in the uh week or two leading up to uh this week's budget uh some state budgets, right? You know, Victoria, Western Australia, and they have, right? So, um Victoria's got no money, but they still spent some more on their punters. Uh Western Australia's got heaps and heaps of money, but they spent more uh on their punters. And you could just hear the teeth start to grind uh at the Reserve Bank. You know, the the states being part of the problem. And their fear would have been that maybe the federal government would do as it did a handful of years ago. You know, the um because an energy crisis is one where the budget is better off but families are worse off, families are poorer. Uh as the governor pointed out, the temptation for governments to hand over some money is very high. Um to this government's credit, it has not uh extended things like that fuel tax cut. It hasn't handed out too much money. Um but I'm I'm cautious of uh saying that they haven't um you know made life a little more complex for the Reserve Bank. Um or or at least I'd say they haven't helped the Reserve Bank. Uh so the last big budget update we got ahead of Christmas about 5 months ago um the estimate of spending uh in the financial year we're just about to start was $811 billion. Do I hate myself, but I know that number? Yes. Uh and then the update we got in the budget, uh it was no longer 811 billion in spending. It was 830 billion in spending. That extra 1819 uh billion. And then you know, you can understand some of it there were policies. There's new policy stuff. Some of its defense, some of it's you know fuel security, some of its pharmaceutical benefits, some of its uh well governments do spend more when inflation goes up and and the economy weakens. Um, some of it is the continuing blowout in in the NDIS that put the boot up the bum of the government to to get more serious on reform then. But that increase in spending 5 months ago the expectation was that spending in the year just about to start by the federal government would increase by 3.1%. Um, as at the budget they said well hang on uh we now think it's going to be 5.3%. And I do think that will have raised a couple of eyebrows at the Reserve Bank. So um you know yes the reserve will be quite happy there wasn't a big cost of living uh package uh we have not repeated those mistakes. The states though are still throwing some money around and the feds are spending more than they had uh said they were going to do last time they updated us. I'm imagining you remember the um Costello years and uh let's go back to the mid 2000s early period and uh Peter Costello did talk about um intergenerational equity in those days. This is this has been an ongoing conversation and and you know treasurers post him you know hockey did um you know swan did etc. So, it's not something new. Um, and there probably is an intergenerational problem in that, um, all the wealth seems to be at one end of the table and the younger people are struggling to get there. That makes sense. What's interesting about this is that, um, Castello's thesis about fixing intergenerational um, inequity, if that's the way we put it, is that instead of running deficits, we should run surpluses. And the reason we should run surpluses is so that we can reduce national debt. Of course, he wasn't talking about state debt. He's talking about the federal debt. And the justification for that was we don't want our kids and grandkids and future generations to have to pay um either an interest or alternatively in extra tax the debts that say my generation, perhaps your generation have accumulated for them. But today, if we fast forward that, the intergenerational um inequity still exists um and and in in the same format that existed in the mid-200s. I actually remember in 2001 election when John Howell was going for the election, affordability, housing affordability was his main state policy and he launched it in um Adelaide. I remember him launching and Chris Joy, who was where I first met him, Chris Joy wrote a paper uh for Malcolm Turble when Malcolm was a chair of Goldman Sachs. Um I remember the event very well. So we haven't really solved affordability and we haven't and we haven't solved intergenerational inequity. We've tried we we haven't solved it. The thesis for solving that today seems to be a lot different. What what do you as an economist how do you feel about um solving for this problem as an economist relative to fiscal policy for example? >> Uh so uh there are three uh standout things uh that you can do. you you mentioned one of them that's to have a better a stronger long-term budget so that we're handing on less debt uh to to the generations that follow. Um to be fair to this budget, it does that. Uh to be less fair, uh it gets there over the longer term by assuming we'll be able to make some pretty big savings in the NDIS. uh and that's going to be hard to do and certainly the speed of the turnaround that the government is looking for on the spend on the NDIS is pretty fast. Uh so it's hard to find too many people uh uh here in CRA who think that that will be um achievable. Um but so yes, you can improve uh the fairness over generations by being more careful with the longerterm budget. Um there's been a bit of that uh in this budget. you can be uh fair to younger Australians if you give and you've mentioned it a few times if you give a greater potential around growth. I've called it prosperity. Um you don't get uh growth in living standards without growth in the economy. The growth in prosperity it is it's essentially all the same. And there are some things right you know there'll be all sorts of changes around artificial intelligence and the like uh that and that that chopping and changing in the economy will create some um uh some problems on the way through but it will also give us a more prosperous economy. we will get um greater prosperity out of those technology changes and the like. What worries me uh is that we are you know not getting enough of a push from our political classes and haven't done for decades around the everything else of prosperity. There are all sorts of things we do uh in the federal budget uh and outside the federal budget that are astoundingly stupid and that hold us uh back on the prosperity front. Um I I am a sad sad human being. Uh Mark, I love tax, right? You know, and I think we can and we should do better in tax. But you look at some of the biggest tax reforms we can make uh in Australia. And then you look at well, you know, what's the uh economic models that people um like me and others run. You know, what's the boost to prosperity we get get out of something? You may be surprised uh to hear that the you know tax I I love it to death. We can and we should do it better but it's not make or break around um prosperity. There is um a a little known push by the federal government uh to change noncompete clauses. That's you know you you get a new job, you sign the bit of paper that says uh as and when you leave that job uh you can't do this, you can't do that. uh and and uh here in Australia about uh one in every four workers signs those uh and they're way too restrictive as I like to say the the clue is in the name. All right, non-compete clauses. Uh we are stopping competition and competition is a grand thing. um if we do uh you know really wind back the scope of those clauses or even get rid of them and and protect intellectual property directly um you can have as big an impact on prosperity as some of the you know the huge tax fights that that you know changes out of tax that we can get. So I do worry that sometimes we we uh roar down rabbit holes rather than um chasing some of the easier wins that we could achieve. In the context of that then Chris maybe you could explain to me and our audience um why over the last 12 months we've had changes to super which probably at the margin don't make much difference relative to the budget changes to um trust tax the way we we tax trusts and all this capital gains and um you know the elimination of negative gearing or the changes to negative gearing. um why do we have that as such a big deal if in relation to you know growth andor prosperity they're not going to have that much of an impact what's what's that all about then >> yeah and and and they're not going to have that much of an impact and and let me add because I I do worry about so um I'm I'm broadly happy uh in particular with the capital gains tax change but um I do worry that it's being marketed it's being spun uh as um you know, this solves housing affordability in Australia and and the good um academic uh work in this area has never claimed that. Uh and and you know the simple illustration over the next 5 years the changes to um capital gains tax and a negative gearing uh will raise an extra $3.6 uh billion in tax. Now that's a lot of money. I would bend down uh and and pick it up. Um but uh across the same five years we will uh as a nation um pay about 600 billion uh in terms of uh mortgage interest payments not uh principal just mortgage interest payments uh so 3.6 6 versus 600 or look at the total size of Australian housing currently valued at uh 12.3 trillion. Uh the these sort of changes will have an overall impact on housing. But if you think of housing as a big rock and we are moving the lever there um we got a tiny lever and this is Australia's largest rock. I would take you back to 2021 2022 even at that stage before interest rates started rising. uh in Australia, you could have made two um relatively reasonable arguments about why Australian housing was so unaffordable. One was um well, interest rates had been falling for a long time during uh the pandemic, they fell to essentially nothing. Um and and when money is cheap as chips, uh no wonder housing prices had gone up and and uh when when interest rates in Australia rose, you would think, well, hey presto, um house prices will come down, they'll become more affordable. the alternative argument uh said you well look you know for decades uh the the Australian housing policy has been the word no you know no you can't build it no you really can't build it there in that leafy suburb because older and richer people don't want you to um if in the rare occasions we say yes uh you know we we put all these rules and regulations that make it stunningly expensive to do and if you roll on to today you interest rates have risen a lot over that period. Uh and and it is the equivalent uh of the Reserve Bank abolishing um negative gearing and and uh the entirety of capital gains tax uh discounts of any kind many times over. Right? You so whether you're talking about interest rates uh capital gains negative gearing, you are talking about uh the cost of money going into property, right? Um, we've done the equivalent of of all this stuff around negative gearing and uh and capital gains taxes and it did not stop a big rise uh in house prices in Australia. We now know, right, we have great evidence uh that the the way to fix housing in Australia is uh going to be the political fight of the uh the rest of uh our lives. you know, it is convincing older and richer uh Australians who don't want change uh in and around uh the suburb where they live that if population is going to grow this much uh we need to change a lot. >> If I can interpret that, I think what you're saying is the rules and regulations are not conducive or are not encouraging any more supply in those particular areas for example. But and because of Australia is such an unusual nation with you know 55% of its population whatever it is living in three capital cities and you know they're surrounded by mountains and sea. Um it's very hard to go beyond those places. So supply is a problem. Supply is a huge problem. Did you see anything in the budget that's going to help us with supply therefore help us with perhaps um access to for younger people to buy their own place andor affordability? >> Uh yes I did. Very happy to see it. also fair to say, you know, it's it's again not a big enough lever. Uh so the budget has an extra $2 billion essentially going into infrastructure um around housing, all the you know the the sewage and and the rest of it. Uh and that is great cuz we haven't been um spending enough. Uh but uh the ultimate supply in Australia requires street by street, suburb by suburb, council by council. We've got to say yes. uh where for decades we've said no and and the people who said no, the the not in my backyards, the Nimbies who said no, thought they were doing the right thing, but here we are 40 years later and and we know we have killed the prospects of a generation by continuing uh to say no. And and the feds um it's hard for them. You know, the feds have levers that can have an impact on the states, the states have levers that can have an impact on local council. Uh but at the end of the day, this is still a fight. uh where um you know older richer Australians uh who are already there don't want change around them and that is uh the the ugliest political fight uh you can ever imagine. I I've always liked um uh there was a a speaker of Congress over in the uh US many years ago, Tip O'Neal. He said all politics is local uh and and all our housing problems are local and it's hard for the feds to fix it. Could you explain um the connection or the dynamic between public spending and private demand? So when the reserve bank governor talks about aggregate private demand has been increasing outside of outside of um fuel t uh fuel prices I'm talking about um and she often says it where does public spending money spent by the government fit in governments not just federal but states as well fit into that? Uh so feds are about a quarter and in the states are about 40% of all spending uh in Australia. Um but of course you know they they tax and they spend sometimes what they do is uh give a tax cut or give a subsidy. You know you're seeing that with fuel taxes uh at the moment. You've seen it recently with electricity uh costs. Um there was another tax cut uh announced in the budget. So sometimes even if uh governments aren't spending more, they are giving families uh or perhaps businesses uh the the uh money to spend more. Um inflation doesn't care if you like uh whether an extra dollar got spent by uh a federal government, a state government, uh a family, a business, whatever. Um, all that inflation cares about is how much spending in total we're getting relative to our ability to supply uh that spending. Again, I'd go back to that basic equation, too much money, chasing too little stuff. Uh, and and that's I think some of the frustr I'm not one of those people who thinks the Reserve Bank um can or should jump up and down about uh governments. That's not their role. they are there to fight uh inflation uh and and the politicians to be fair to them they have a different role and politics being what it is sometimes you know they are going to make the task of inflation fighting harder with their decisions that's what it is you know I don't expect uh the governor of the day or the reserve bank to be jumping up and down about what governments do that's not how it works uh if you want an an assessment of what governments are or aren't doing you know there's plenty people out there who can give opinions. You you you can't necessarily expect the Reserve Bank to be the one, you know, the part of government to be jumping up and down about uh the rest of government. >> We often talk um and it's it's in the media all the time about the national debt um being 9, let's call it 60 billion, close enough to a trillion. Um, maybe you could also explain to our audience that's not the end of it because I think the debt of Victoria alone is uh a couple hundred billion. Um, does that fit into the national debt or is that in addition to the national debt? So, and what would our national debt look like when you add everything up? Couple of thoughts for you. Best way to measure debt is not gross debt, it's net debt. If I've got a big housing loan, but I've got a bunch of money in the bank, I'm better off than somebody who's got the same loan um but no money in the bank. The net uh of our financial assets is important. So the Feds are close to a trillion dollars as you know uh in debt. Uh but net debt once you allow for the money they've got stashed away in various places is about uh 600 billion. Um, second thought is, uh, you know, well, uh, whether I've got a big housing loan, uh, and whether or not I've got money in the bank. What's also relevant is my wage, how much I'm earning. Uh, and and the same is true for governments, right? You look at the nation as a whole uh, and and you look at that uh, 600 billion uh, in in federal debt, you kind of need to compare it to national income. Uh and and I suspect uh this will come as a surprise to you and to many people um if you're looking at net federal debt as a share of Australian national income uh that is below where it was before co you know uh yes co was expensive but it was temporary uh and and our national income has uh grown a lot and we've had a burst of inflation um you know which has boosted the size of of national income but it hasn't boosted the debt. So I am less worried about federal debt that a bunch of people are. what has changed over the past decade and you mentioned it you know the uh Victoria with its 200 billion debt a bunch of others uh states have also got debt uh they also have less financial assets so that the the reduction to net uh is is um uh more of an issue for them uh to the extent that Australia has a public sector debt problem it is more uh the states than the feds uh these days >> thank you And are there any insights you can offer around something that rarely gets discussed but the offbudget budget? So in other words, things that weren't disclosed in the income expenditure budget that are more in the capital side of the budget or what we're going to spend what Australian government's going to spend on capital items. >> Yeah. And and and the distinction is a is a different one. So um if if there's a given measure of the budget that the media and analysts concentrate on uh then the politicians have an incentive to to put money that it doesn't show up in the measure that's been concentrated on. Uh and uh until that charter of budget honesty back in 1998, we we used to um uh look at a number that was made better when governments sold an asset when they privatized >> for example like Comworth Bank or Telra. >> Yeah. Yeah. And indeed the you saw a lot of privatizations at the state uh level and and um just before change of government from uh Kading to Howard uh Jeff Kennet was privatizing some things that let him pay back some debt to the feds. um the the uh repayment of some Victorian government loans um allowed the federal government to say uh at that stage right the the budget is in good nick um you know whereas it was uh being propped up by oneoff privatization uh receipts effectively um and and so uh economists including me said well hang on you know we need to fix that and then we had a charter of budget honesty um our problem is that when we changed what we look at We simply change the incentives for the politicians and don't get excited by one side or the other. Right? I I if you think one side of politics are genius and the other side are incompetent and evil, then you are a deep disappointment to your primary school maths teacher. All right? You know, they they are all flawed human beings and they are dealing with flawed incentives that we as a nation have given them. Uh and and as soon as we changed the incentives, as soon as we said, well, you know, um we're now going to concentrate on this budget measure, not that one. Uh it gave them in an incentive to to put different money. Instead of privatizing, we were sort of nationalizing. Suddenly we had all sorts of funds. You know, we got funds around housing. Uh we got funds around climate change. Uh we've um got got everything from uh the NBN to snowy hydro uh to student debt. uh all sorts of things sit off budget. >> In other words, they're not in the current budget. >> Uh they they are not in the measure that we look at the most. Uh and and the the the sort of punter way to think about it is I don't mind if something goes off budget. Uh if if that's just a all right, you know, money's going to go out, but it's going to earn a bit of a return and that money will come back in. I don't care. I don't need to look at that closely. It can go off budget. That's fine. Our trouble is um that our rules and our reporting mean that effectively stuff goes off budget and then we stop caring about it. So uh increasingly over time that's where the trash uh goes, you know, and and that money goes dark. We don't look at it uh enough. uh and and so you you know uh for example when you have debt write offs as we did recently with student debt uh it doesn't show up in or or not immediately it only shows up very slowly uh in the main measures that we look at right you know so there's a real incentive for um our governments to put off budget and then to forgive that debt you know because the system sort of lets them get away with that it's not by the way the only way uh in in which they they do things. They they tend to um uh any given budget gives you some numbers over 10 years, but it gives you detailed numbers over four years. So, they try to be careful in the four years and mysteriously years five and six are suddenly much worse, right? You know, there's that dodge. There's all sorts of things we do need. Uh and I would it is an incredibly nerd and niche point. Forgive me, right? But if we give our politicians such terrible uh reporting rules, of course they are going to report them. And I I'm desperate to change those rules. Uh we are letting our politicians uh consistently pull uh the wool over our eyes. I think what you're sort of saying here is that there are a number of items that have effectively been classed as assets for under the in this environment and don't appear in the expense column or the income column. The income column being the the tax they collect and other things. The expense column being the thing that they spend day-to-day on the government that is in terms of a budget. But there's another column over here called assets. Snowy Hydro for example or NBN. Um, Snowy Hydro, by the way, is not even completed. It's in fact, they've stopped doing some work on it recently because it was a cave- in or something, but they're going to keep throwing money at it. But that's money that coming from, it's either borrowed money or alternatively, it's coming out of the tax pool. It's got to come from somewhere. It's cuz you got to spend it. >> Yeah. And and and the the um acceleration in the amount of money that's headed off budget is spectacular. to be fair to the budget this week. Uh it didn't throw, you know, it threw an extra 5 billion in over uh four years. Uh and um I'm not that excited. It was only 5 billion, but there has been uh a a truly massive amount. Uh we are currently looking across a 5year period at $115 billion. Uh that that sits off budget. Again, I hate myself for knowing that number without having to check it. Um uh but yeah, you know, Australians deserve better. Uh and it's such a nerd thing. We would be outraged if businesses uh were rotting their uh accounts. Uh you know, if if the auditors were failing to to jump up and down about all this sort of stuff. Uh the behavior is bad because we let it be bad. Well, I think what you're saying there too, Chris, is that um it's really our responsibility or perhaps more the responsibility of professionals like yourself because most punters wouldn't understand this, but um there is a level of obligation or responsibility for people in this country to hold the government to account for these sorts of things. We're talking about hundreds of billions. We're talking about seriously big dollars. And the question becomes, will these so-called assets actually one day make us money or or at least we get some utility utility out of them at some stage? We don't know the answer to that. Um because I was only thinking about the NBN the other day. You know, the NBN great, but you know, when you look at M's uh Starlink, I mean like I mean if you got a farm for example, even got if you got a house, Starlink works really well. Um and uh it costs the consumer you know three or 4 hund bucks and uh we've yet we're spending billions on the NBN and uh I know it's valuable and it's we use it here. >> Yeah. The technology changed. So you might have thought the NBN was a you know good and safe investment and you could set it off uh off budget there cuz it was going to be okay. But the technology changed and and we've lost money. Um, you look at uh the snowy hydro, turns out it was um uh a lot harder to dig uh and a lot more expensive and a lot slower to dig than we thought. So, it might have been reasonable to to put it off budget uh to begin with, but here we are. We now know we've lost a fortune. Um there are all sorts of uh thing or or you know um uh some of our money these days uh props up ad aluminium smelters uh because there are votes in it or they props up the uh uh production of Tim Tams and I love Tim Tams but I don't want you know my taxpayer dollars uh supporting it uh you know at the cost of everything else. um we let crap happen. That's on us uh much more than it is on on the politicians. >> Thanks. That's a that's a very clear answer on that. Thanks very much. The the one that wasn't the the word that was not used, I don't think in everything that I read in relation to the budget, and I did use AI to help me. Um the word that didn't come up was immigration. Um, do you reckon you could just uh sort of uh sort of drill down a little bit on immigration relative to fiscal policy relative to inflation and probably finally the standard of living, our standard of living cuz that's where we started. >> Yeah. Yeah. Yeah. And it is worth um going back a couple of decades uh when um migration was a clear positive uh for Australia. But because we have not built uh or when we built we haven't built in the right place or when we built we haven't you know the rules and regulations have made it stunningly expensive. But that isn't the fault of the migrants right that that again that's on us. We've screwed up. Um but the worse that housing has become uh the more of a complication that the pace of uh our migration uh has been. Now migration uh the government has been tightening up. Um but we have a system that lets you appeal this appeal that meaning that uh the slowdown in migration is occurring more slowly than the government had expected. uh this financial year in the budget the other day the government just revised up the uh expected net migration this year by 35,000 people I think it was 20,000 uh next year um to be fair that number is coming down to be really fair you know if we want to get back to a society that that can be u much more welcoming as we should be we have to be doing better on housing and a range of other fronts um we've made uh the migration equation for Australia much more complex uh and we can or we should do better. >> Do do you think there's a a mismatch or there has been a mismatch relative to policy on immigration into Australia and and and we're talking about net immigration so there's people leaving and there's people coming um relative to um the policy relative in relation to housing these people has there been a mismatch? Oh, yeah. And and again, that's on us. We should be much better at building housing uh than we are uh and we're not. There are other complications though, right? They when uh migrants arrive, federal governments tend to make money. They get the taxes. State governments tend to lose money. They've got to, you know, build the roads and and the schools and the hospitals and the like. Um but it's it's uh the feds who get to make the decisions. So, they tend to make money out of it. uh and and uh you know that has a bit of an impact. They also like um net migration uh to be higher partly because it makes it less likely that the economy ever tips into recession. Uh politicians hate headlines about recessions. More people means that's less likely to happen. to be clear, right? You know, the the um uh the politics of migration are made much more complex not just by those things, but by our policy failings on everything else. Uh and those policy failings on everything else have lasted uh for so many decades, right? We that responsibility lies clearly with us uh to get them better. >> Do you think overall that this budget was a reform budget? >> I do. which is not the same thing as saying I like um everything in it. There's some things in it that I quite strongly disagree with. Um but it is fair to say if you look back in in the last couple of decades, we have had, you know, white knuckled scary budgets as we fought off crisis, right? You know, and the budget had to do really important things during co uh during the global financial crisis. And and again, not to say that our decisions were perfect either time, but we know cuz we could compare to what other nations did that both times we did better than other nations and and um you know federally uh it was under one side of politics with co and another side during global financial crisis. What we have not done uh for many years now uh our politicians have not challenged Australians enough. If an opposition says, you know, we really need to change, we don't vote vote them in. If a government, right, think of the 2014 budget, says, all right, we do need to change, and this budget said we do need to change, we tend to kick them out at the following uh election. As an electorate, we have begged our politicians to do nothing. We have elected the ones who promise to do nothing and then they deliver on that promise of doing nothing. Our problem is that our the barnacles continue to grow. You see it in housing. You see it in climate policy. You see it in federal state relations. You see it uh you see see it in our um tobacco taxing. Uh you see it in our gas taxing. Uh you see it in the NDIS. You see it in in the deal, the sweetheart deal that gives um West Australia extra money even though it's got money coming out of its ears. We do dumb things and I find that very frustrating. >> Can I ask you one final question, Chris? Um, in the interest of time for for you um and I appreciate the time you spent on this. If you were advising the treasurer, tough one. And uh and he said to you, "Look, Chris, this intergenerational inequity is just wrong. We got to fix it. And and the way I want to fix it is I want to get young people into a house." What would you whisper in his ear? >> Um I I would say and again, you know, I was happy enough with the capital gains tax changes, but I would say it's not about tax. Um it is about our stupid regulations. Uh and and I was part of that reform uh round table uh here in CRA last August. Uh one of its key recommendations was just stop adding things to the national construction code. uh those regulations and and if you are in a nation where one of the key recommendations is just stop right you know it it helps you show uh the stupidity of what we have done um I described it earlier as as for decades Australian's uh housing policy has been the word no right no you can't do that we have to make it yes uh and that is incredibly urgent because even if we have perfect policies and you know federal state local council even if we have older, richer Australians saying, "All right, okay, I get it. I' i've need got to change my mind. I got to be willing uh for this to happen." Um, you've then got to build it, right? And that's going to take a very long time to fix. Uh, and that is the the uh tough news. There is no magic wand uh in housing. And I I suspect the the uh fix that it needs uh is not merely automatically slow because of the building process, but it has to travel through older and richer people who really do not want that sort of change. >> Yeah. Well, and would you offer them an incentive to accept a change? Would you say to older, richer people, look, you know, if uh you five people in those five houses all alongside each other all agree to sell to this developer who's going to put a block of apartments up there, maybe um I don't know, there's some special incentive for you, some some tax incentive or you know, I don't know, whatever. Um would you do you think that's the way out of this? Because I mean, how do you how do you how do you change people's attitude towards like you just said, the five older people living in a leafy suburb, they've always lived for the last 50 years, have paid off their house, have worked hard, paid the taxes, they don't want a five-story building popping up next door to them. Um, how do you incentivize these people? What do you think? >> And and um I'm assuming they don't have kids and grandkids uh they clearly not worrying about them. Um and the answer is and you're seeing it in New South Wales at the moment as they have changed uh the planning rules and regulations around train stations for example you are suddenly getting um you know builders going to them and saying well hang on uh there's money if you wanted to you know we can now do stuff that we couldn't do before there's money here so there is um an incentive a financial incentive to change once that change is allowed uh we are not yet at the point uh in Australia where uh much change is allowed. Uh there's a spot I walk past in Sydney all the time. It's a, you know, a little half a terrace and it looks terrible. It is tiny. It is dirty and I suspect uh it costs many millions of dollars and squeeze it. There is no actual parking spot, but squeezed in under the sort of bay window. Uh there is a very expensive looking Porsche. We are creating a first world nation that has third world housing. Uh and and that just drives me crazy. You know, why are we uh shooting ourselves in the foot like that? >> Well, Chris Chris Richardson, former um Deoid Access Economics, but probably importantly today, your business. You're the founder and principal of Rich Insight. I really appreciate your time. And by the way, thanks very much for explaining these complex minutia that sort of sit in budgets that by the way the media never talks about. They just hit the headlines all the time. Um and the headlines obviously are important. Some people will be upset about CGT and some people going to be upset about um negative gearing going and trust taxes and all that sort of stuff. And that's for another day, for another topic. That's not for you and I today. But for you and I, what I really appreciate is you sort of getting into the minutia and sort of giving us context about that sort of stuff. I'd really appreciate. Thank you very much. I I love it all too much and it's my birthday. >> Thanks, Chris.
