tags=capital gains tax, negative gearing, tax strategy, australian property, wealth building, federal budget 2026, Morgan Bushell, Simon Loo
Description
The tax rules just changed overnight, and most Australians have no idea what it actually means for them.

In this episode, Jeremy Iannuzzelli went live a day after the 2026–27 Federal Budget dropped, breaking it down with buyer's agent Simon Loo, mortgage broker Morgan Bushell, and Todd Sloan from the Pizza & Property Podcast.

Jeremy breaks down what the numbers really mean, which investment structures are now under pressure, and why companies and SMSFs are becoming the preferred vehicles for serious investors. The panel also explores borrowing power, where opportunities still remain, and why the lead-up to 1 July 2027 could be critical.

Before you make any decisions, watch this episode and understand what's actually been proposed and what it means for your situation.



❓👇🏼Ask your questions using the link below: 👇🏼❓
https://whatschoolshouldhave.com.au/askanyquestion



Get in touch with Jeremy and his team: 
Website: 
https://www.praediumpartners.com.au/

Simon Loo:
https://housefinder.com.au/

Todd Sloan:
https://pizzaandproperty.com/

Morgan Bushell:
https://www.fullcirclefinance.com.au/






Disclaimer:
This podcast offers general financial information and advice and should not be considered as professional accounting, financial, tax, or legal advice. Listeners should seek personalised guidance from qualified professionals before making any financial decisions.

The opinions expressed by the host, guests, or callers are their own and do not necessarily represent those of the podcast, its sponsors, or affiliates. The podcast does not guarantee the accuracy or reliability of any information provided.

Listeners are responsible for their own financial decisions and should not rely solely on information from this podcast. Participation in the podcast does not establish a professional relationship, and information shared during calls is not confidential.

By listening to this podcast, viewers acknowledge and accept these terms. If you do not agree, please refrain from listening.
Australian federal budget delivered May 12 by Treasurer Jim Chalmers. Key changes: negative gearing on established residential properties effectively removed for new purchases after 7:30pm May 12; CGT 50% discount replaced with indexation method from July 1, 2027; properties owned before budget grandfathered with split calculation; trusts face minimum 30% tax on distributions; new properties (additional dwellings) retain negative gearing and depreciation; companies and super funds favored structures; legislative process includes consultation, lower house, Senate, potential amendments before enactment. Impact on investors, borrowing power, shares, crypto, business sales. Panel: Todd (host), Jeremy (tax/structures), Simon (market), Morgan (borrowing). Key dates: May 12 (budget night), July 1, 2027 (effective date for CGT indexation). Domain terms: negative gearing, CGT discount, indexation method, grandfathering, new home definition, trust taxation, carried forward losses, borrowing power.deepseek-v4-flash

Review transcript
Hey everybody, there's been a huge amount of information out there regarding the budget that happened on the 12th of May at 7.30pm delivered by Jim Chalmers. labour budget. Obviously, lots of changes that he spoke about in the budget speech. And I've done so many podcasts over the last couple of days and wanted to share with you guys on what school should have taught you just a recent one that I did on a live YouTube podcast with Pizza and Property and some other great professionals as well. But it's really good. It really nails in what are the changes and what are the things that you need to look at. Also, more importantly, for everybody that's out there, the budget speech is a speech. What generally happens after the budget is it will go through its consultation period where there'll be lots of different professionals and industry bodies that get involved just to really sit down with the Treasurer and actually go through the budget notes. What is it actually going to look like? What is the legislation? How do we draft the legislation? Once that's gone through its consultation period, it will then generally go through the legislation being drafted. It will generally go through the lower house first, then up to the Senate to be debated. Now generally with major and large tax reforms like we've seen in this budget, tends to go through maybe some compromise, maybe some amendments, goes back up to the house, upper house, back down to the lower house before things become legislated and acted in tax law. So biggest advice is don't stress, there's be lots of things that will come out regarding the budget over the next couple of weeks. You've got to keep your eyes and ears tuned to the telly. If you listen to podcasts, YouTube channels, watch them, gather as much thoughts as you can. But this is a bit of an uncertain time where very hard to create a strategy when we don't have the legislation just yet. So I hope you like this podcast that we've done talking about the budget and some of the changes. Keep your eyes on my YouTube channel and iTunes and Spotify. I'll be releasing lots of videos as things progress about what things you need to be considering, structures you need to be considering to all help you make those great decisions from a tax perspective. Everything school should have taught you in one place. With a property portfolio of over 17 million and an accountancy firm of over 16,000 clients, All right, guys. Now, we should be live across YouTube and Facebook. Simon Lou, Jeremy Ionizelli, Morgan Bushell. It's time to talk budgets. There's been some very big changes that have happened in the last 24 hours. CGT changes, negative gearing changes, some of them are potentially not even going to fully take effect the way that everyone's already been talking around the water cooler. Jeremy, you and I were just talking about this beforehand. There's gonna be some big borrowing changes as well. Morgan, you and I were talking like late last night. And Simon, you and I were chatting a little bit more about what potential effects this is gonna have on the market. Now, we've got so many questions that you guys have already sent in ready to fire at Morgan, Jeremy and Simon. We literally had hundreds. So some of them we had to kind of smoosh down together. Otherwise, we'd probably be here till next year's budget as well, still answering questions. But then we've also got a few as well that are going to come through from the live stream. So whether you're watching on Facebook, whether you're watching on YouTube, if you've got a question, we can chuck it on here and ask straight away. Get as interactive as possible. The only silly one is the one that you want to ask and you don't but otherwise gentlemen welcome welcome morgan jeremy and simon how you guys going good mate how are you um are you all typing your response there or it'll be over it to be honest man it's just been i mean we've been talking about the budget but we think honestly this whole budget thing has been on the cards for um weeks And, you know, like it's, there's been leaks every other day about what's going to happen and changes and this and that. And then obviously the big thing dropped yesterday, really no surprises. If I'm honest, you know, we kind of expected the only surprise I would say is the whole negative gearing still applies until July, 2021, sorry, 2027, which I think is going to, um, incentivize a lot of short-term flippers. So is that, you mean the, the CGT side of things, Simon, that's still going to 2027? That and also negative gearing. So I think, you know, the first thing that I thought of is a lot of people are going to be looking for opportunities now to try to flip before July 1, 2027. They might run up some costs, maybe stick a tenant in, you know, so that the renos or the renos or whatever cost they have accrued over this period is going to be offset against tax. But anyway, aside from that, I think it's just been a bit of a... Yeah, I don't think there was any surprises, if I'm honest. And then this entire day, all day, I've just been flooded with investors, what's going on, should I still buy, blah, blah, blah. So yeah, interesting. And this is gonna be phone calls, I think, and text messages and emails that all of you guys are gonna be fielding probably every day for a little while now. And we're gonna go into this in about a five, 10 minute segment from Morgan, from more of a borrowing perspective. Jeremy for more of a taxation structure and then Simon for more of a market perspective. And then we'll jump into the Q&A just as everyone's sort of coming in. But otherwise, before we get into each of you guys, anything else that Morgan or Jeremy you guys wanted to sort of set the stage with or any anecdotes from today that you wanted to share, mate? Not necessarily for myself. I mean, yeah, like the other guys, I've had lots of inquiries about what the right way is to go ahead, what the impact is. So, yeah. Yeah, it's – and at least – yeah, I'm not going to spoil it too much with what you may be going to ask later. But, yeah, it's just – like Simon said, we kind of knew what was coming, so there's no big surprises. It's just – yeah, it's – I guess I mean, we just have to adjust to, right? Yeah, I think that's the big thing. It's about – I guess it is what it is to a certain extent. Now, how do we best play the hand that they've dealt? But by the sounds of what you were saying a little bit earlier, Jeremy, some of the cards might actually change and we might not actually be playing the exact hand that we think we are because this still has to actually pass the Senate. Is that right? Yeah. So this is definitely a budget without throwing, you know, any party under the bus. I've got my own opinions, which I'll keep to myself. But it is a cut your nose to spite your face type of budget. I think they'll struggle to get the intended effect on who they wanted that to affect. You know, they're after that top one to 5%, but they're actually going to wash up probably the 85% of people that need a lot of these policies to grow the wealth. And we've also got a understand as well that this CGT change doesn't just impact property, it impacts all investments, from shares, cryptocurrency to gold and super bullion and all the other things. So I stress to everybody and I stress to nearly the 300 emails I've received today and 90 phone calls since six o'clock this morning that the devil will be in the details. What they've given us is a budget which has a lot of fluff in it. In the back end, then it starts to be put into proper legislation, which they present to the floor of the lower house, and then it moves forward to the upper house. A couple of things I was surprised to hear that I thought Greens would be supporting this like heaven and high water, but it doesn't seem to be the case. There's things they support and there's a lot of things they don't. So they'll get it through. Just to quickly jump in there, Jeremy, what's one of the things they're not supporting? Because I would have assumed the same thing. Yeah, capital gain tax. I love the negative gearing. They've been pushing on about taking away negative gearing because it's disproportionate in the sense that when you are negatively geared and you're on a 47% tax bracket for every dollar you're negatively geared, you get 47% back. And if you're a 32% tax bracket for every dollar of negative gearing on that same property, for instance, you're only getting 32 cents back. So understand where they're coming from about the disproportionate tax system, but taxes are disproportionate based on the income we earn. People are still spending the same amount of time. So the argument doesn't go both ways that the Greens have. However, it was interesting to see the lack of support they had for capital gain tax because one of the reasons why Peter Costello and John Howard removed the indexation method in 99 was because it was sucking out and reducing productivity. There was no investment happening during the 90s. You didn't have properties transacting like they were today. You didn't have investment into new businesses because if it did shoot up quite a bit, you were gonna get stung with hell of a lot of tax when you take into account the indexation method. So it was taken away to give a lot of productivity to bring back investment into the Australian economy to get the Australian people excited about investing again. And it had its intended effect when it was removed. We've gone through the last 26 years with probably the most prosperity out of any country in the world when it comes to living standards and people. So it was interesting to see while they spoke about all this, I was more interested to see how they were going to frame it. And in my personal opinion, I think there's going to be a lot of There's going to be a lot of pushback, especially from my industry when you get the big guys involved that actually know what tax legislation is and how to interpret correctly. I think that's where the needle and the penny is going to drop for the government and there'll be amendments. So, mate, amendments happen. to budget proposals. The Division 296 was proposed in one way. It was amended, reshaped. It didn't pass the upper house and then eventually got through with a lot of compromise. So we might still see a lot of compromise to happen in this federal budget. And Jeremy, we may as well open up with yourself first, mate. One question that I actually wanted to ask you was around the carried forward losses. I was actually just reading this before. From July the 1st, 2027, losses related to an existing residential investment property purchase from 7.30pm Australian News Standard Time will only be deductible against other income for residential properties, including capital gains. However, when an investor has excess losses, they will be able to carry forward that excess to offset residential property income in future years, enabling losses to be carried forward, ensuring investors remain able to claim deductions in the future and costs of maintenance. So does this basically mean negative gearing isn't gone? It's just been changed around and kind of moved forward? The concept has changed how negative gearing works. So, again, when you had losses that arose from a property, you could claim that as a deduction against your employment income, your work-related income, your salaries and wage. What they've done is they've segmented the return to say your income and your expenses for work is how you get your refund and the interest and dividends and all that stuff. But your property side of things, that's a separate portion within the return. And you'll only be able to claim negative gearing against a few related property profits that you make and if you don't have enough property profits and you might have excess losses because you sold a property and you made a loss maybe capital loss that is you can utilize that against the next property you purchase and maybe the passive income that it generates so they're really segmenting just the property section with inside the return and almost almost isolating it like it's only the business so you don't get a benefit and that whole purpose of what they're trying to achieve, Todd, and for the listeners, is they're creating a brand new floor rate. And that floor rate of tax is 30%. They don't want anyone to access any better tax rate than 30% from your investment-related profits, essentially. And that's a little bit of a bee in my bonnet as accountant because the way I would work is I would factor in the tax rate of zero all the way to 47%. And it was up to me to minimize my client's tax to bring it as close as I can to zero. Now, zero starts at 30. So effectively speaking, they're saying that 30% now is your best case scenario. And my tax rates to play with now sits between 30 to 47, as opposed to zero to 47. So they're really limiting. There's still a lot of tax we can save for clients because there is a lot of movement between that 30 to 47% mark, but there was a hell of a lot more benefit between zero to 47. So that's going to be the major thing that I want to share with people and explain to people. As an accountant, the best I can get now, best we can get is 30%. It's potentially no longer zero. And that's obviously going to change our borrowing power in, well, I'm not going to say a big way yet, but Morgan, you're going to give us more detail on that in just a moment. But Jeremy, what are the other big takeaways that everyone really needs to understand as a property investor from both either taxation or structuring point of view? So first couple of things, let's go through the top level points. So negative gearing on established residential property is effectively gone now for new buyers post the budget. For all of us who hold properties prior to the budget, we still have the ability to continue on as normal from a negative gearing perspective. It's just any future properties we purchase, those will be trapped as losses and later utilized against income that we generate from properties or potential capital gains. And that's effective pretty much for any property with a date of contract after 7.30 PM on the 12th of May. For new properties, you will still have the ability to claim negative gearing. The definition of new property, I thought you could knock it down, build a brand new one and you've got a new property. That may not be the case. You have to actually correct actually have to create more dwellings. So knocking down a home and building two, for instance, townhouses, apartments, all those other things. So that's something to consider what a new home is. Also, if you buy a home from a builder, it has to happen within the space of about one year of completion for it to still be deemed as brand new. So that's something to consider and depreciation rules will all stay the same for new properties. You also get the benefit of being able to choose both methods, which is the capital gain discount if you sell that new property in the future or the indexation method. Now for everybody out there, what's the difference between the two? And we've spoken briefly about it, but for long-term investing, I mean for low growth long-term, you'll often find the indexation might be better off to calculate what they call the real gain. But if you've got very short-term growth and you want to exit it, you are going to pay a truckload of tax. And this is one of the best ways I can explain it. And I'll use shares as an example because the numbers are smaller to understand. But if you buy a share, on the 13th of May under the new CGT rules for $100. You hold that share for 13 months and sell it for $200. If the CPI, which is how the indexation method is calculated is 4%, roughly at the end of that 13 months, the cost base of your shares is now $104. Now, you've sold it for $200 less your index cost base of $104. You now have to pay tax at your marginal rate on $96. compared to the 50% CGT discount method, where when you had that share sold for 200 and bought for 100, that $100 gain would obtain a 50% discount. So 50 bucks goes in your back pocket and the other $50 goes into your tax return as taxable income. So that's the difference. The difference now on that example is $96, which you pay tax on at your marginal rate versus $50, which you pay tax on. at your marginal rate. So effectively under my example, and it's not that exaggerated, these things do happen. you might be paying double the tax that you would have under the 50% discount method. So there's a huge grab there, a significant grab there from the government for capital gain tax. And that's why I've been talking for the last month about the government creating a win for tax system, because it doesn't feel as bad, I suppose, mentally or from a psyche perspective, that you're giving the government a bit of extra money after you've received this lump sum of cash. So there's to think about. One of the big ones, it sounds like the pre-1985 assets lose their CGT exemption. Essentially, they'll have a valuation being done as at the 1st of July to create that new cost basing for the indexing method. So that's huge for a lot of people that have properties that they've purchased before 1985. That's, I think, the month of September. For any property investor that has existing properties, that valuation point in time as of 1 July 2007 becomes very important as well. I'll slow this one down because it's a lot to digest. If you sell the property before 1 July, 2027, you'll still be able to obtain a 50% CGT discount method. So that's brilliant. But if you sell property after 1 July, 2027, two calculations happen. So you've got a calculation at a 50% CGT discount up to the value of the property as at 1 July, 2027. Do you just basically get like a retrospective approval if you were to sell it in three years' time, that kind of, not retrospective approval, retrospective valuation or how they calculate it? You can. You can get a retrospective valuation being done. It must be done by a registered property valuer. It can't be done by a real estate agent that's a market appraisal. So effectively, the two calculations are the 50% CGT discount based upon the normal method of tax that we calculate up to the valuation price on the 1 July 2027 date. And then any time thereafter, once you've created the valuation cost-based index is now under the indexation method for CGT. So there's two calculations now that accountants have to do if people decide to sell their properties after 1 July, 2027. So it's a form of grandfathering for everyone that's listening. It's a form of grandfathering, but not traditional grandfathering. So it's kind of split grandfathering almost. Correct. Correct. Correct. Yeah. One of the big ones that blindsided me and I still don't know how they're going to write this into legislation. So I'm very interested to see what they do was that trust now will face a 30 percent tax as a minimum on distribution. So that that to me is very, very interesting how they're going to write it. That goes against, you know, history, historical ways of tax minimization through the use of trust and the ability to distribute to the beneficiaries and utilize marginal tax rates from different people. But for them to create a floor of 30 percent tax on the distribution, that to me is a huge negative. Because if you have an income that you've generated from that trust, from passive income the property provides, or from a capital gain, for argument's sake, and you were to distribute that money to your spouse, where their income would have been zero, and normally you would distribute 20 grand to them, and that's a great way you can get 20 grand tax-free from your investment. Now, under the new legislation that they proposed last night, it will face a tax of 30%. which in this particular case on that 20 grand now that you distribute to your spouse where you thought you'd pay no tax, you are now paying $6,000. So that's a massive and significant change in historical tax legislation around trusts. It's literally rewriting the playbook on it and speaking to people that have been in the industry for nearly 60 years. They don't even know how it's going to be written because there's things to consider like how they're going to factor in that 30%, whether it's imputation credit, effectively if it flows through to the individual or not, and the individual gets a credit or an offset for it. So that's a really big one. And I still maintain that. Look, when I first started buying properties, it was easy to get positive or neutral cash flow properties off the bat. But over time as, you know, property prices have increased and the rents haven't caught up, you know, from my perspective, negative gearing has been a necessary evil to buy properties in areas that are more likely to grow. And I keep talking about major capital cities in areas where affordable housing still exists because ultimately that's where humans have to live, right? At the end of the day. And I think the winners of this particular budget are some developers. But, you know, it's going to be, it's going to be a, a, a, inevitable, you know, at the end of the day. Can you do it? Sorry, Simon to jump in. Can you just talk in a little bit more about the details around why you think rents are going to, to increase Mike? Cause I'm sure that's something that everyone's kind of let forward a little bit on. Well, rent will go up because investors just won't sell, you know, or are less incentivized to sell. Investors will also be, new investors will also be, well, at least I guess the herd investors will be reluctant to buy, which will make less stock available. Look, collectively, I think if you take away negative gearing benefits, especially for some of the newer landlords that have purchased in the past five or so years, they're just going to pass the expenses on to the tenant or as much as possible. And the underlying problem is still not addressed. Huge housing shortage. We don't have enough houses. We've got more people pouring into this country. And a lot of comments I'm reading are saying, oh, just because landlords increase rent, it doesn't mean tenants are gonna pay for it. Well, they are because for every house available, there's dozens, dozens, if not hundreds of people needing that property to live in. So yeah, I think, look at the end of the day, the people that the government was trying to help, So renters and first-time buyers, I think they're the ones that are gonna lose. Yeah, I kind of want to mention something that I was speaking to Todd about off air, actually, because we were kind of like hypothesising some ways that people could still think know use their existing properties to help them get tax benefits um so maybe you can actually chime in on this a bit but at least one thing we're talking about is people i suppose doing really large equity releases from existing properties to get some of those tax benefits but then also then use that to buy property with more or less smaller loans and it's something that you know i've kind of been thinking about most of like today and yesterday because Yeah, I think the people who could probably successfully do that and get the most out of that scenario would be the kind of people that most likely have a ton of equity and also a ton of borrowing power. But then is that the best way to approach investing? I'm not exactly sure. It's probably only applicable to like ultra high net worths or not, you know, your mum and dads. I'm curious to know what you think though, Jeremy. Our challenge is, is that you extract equity from a property the deduction goes where the equity went to from a purpose perspective. So a lot of people go, so I'm just going to go and extract equity out and I'll make it ultra negatively geared. Well, it doesn't work that way. Where the equity goes to is where the interest is claimed. So that's a very important thing to note because they've got this very special concept with the ATO called Part 4A, which is a tax avoidance rule. And they want to make sure people are doing things with inside the spirit of the law. The strategies around that are as well, you might be buying properties in other entities. Your goal would be to pay off those properties in the other entities in the longterm, work towards paying them down after all non-deductible debt is taken care of first. And then you focus on those properties, which are highly negatively geared potentially in your own name if they are in that particular case. So your strategy now isn't just tax, but also what you do at certain points in time with your investment portfolio. And now no properties or no assets are created equal. You've got to really strategize what's more important in the portfolio and what do you focus on and attack first. Debt recycling, Morgan, you and I have been a big fan of it. that potentially may have its implications because a lot of people try to do it to obtain cash in the hand through, you know, lowering non-deductible debt, extracting the equity back out, pushing that towards a new property in the goal of getting some additional tax benefit back. I know a lot of high net wealth individuals use that to buy high value properties. That has its challenge now because you're not going to be getting your 47% back for properties purchased post 12th of May, 7.30pm. So, That throws a bit of a spanner in the works to a beautiful way of recycling your debt to gain some tax benefits. From a market perspective, I know just myself and speaking to some people to get their concepts, what we will see, happy to be corrected as well, anyone here, but that top end of the market where land tax makes up 40% of the rental income and council rates and interest, and these properties are 50, 60, 70, $100,000 negatively geared, you're going to see that top end of the market start to say, well, we're not going to buy that top end for investment anymore. We're going to start to come down to that middle end of the market. we've got cash to be able to maybe lower the debt or maybe potentially high yields and we probably will see more competition. So more demand in that middle market, more demand in that affordable market, which goes to answer one of those questions I saw from Manpreet. How do you see the market in the short term under 650K? I think that's going to have a lot more pressure. It's going to pump because that's typically Typically where those yields are a little bit stronger and typically where you start to say, well, you know what, 10, 15 grand negatively geared that I can't claim as a tax benefit is a hell of a lot better than 100 grand. I've got legacy clients. I've genuinely got legacy clients that they've got properties where land tax is through the roof. They've owned it in their personal name for such a long time. They're going to start to question if it's worthwhile keeping it. through that extreme amount of capital growth they've seen because they were getting that tax benefit cash wise via the refund to help them hold that property long term. So it'd be interesting to see that convergence of markets and what that does and how it creates additional demand, especially in the more affordable area. And one of the main things I think we've all missed and the budget last night absolutely glossed over it because it's a main sticking point is we had 1 million migrants come in over the last four years. another 1 million migrants to come in over the next four years. So that's 2 million migrants to come in. We have just created, and I know, Todd, I'm going to use your backyard for this, but we've just created Adelaide, if not potentially all of South Australia in the space of two years. And our infrastructure and our housing build program has not even kept up anywhere near to what's coming in. So that itself is going to put significant pressure on rental, on housing market, and there's only one place to go. Description
Need a hand?
Need some help sourcing your next investment property? 👉 Book a Free Call with Aaron: https://bit.ly/4f0Yadu

Looking for some investment minded lending strategies? 👉 Book a Free Call with Brad: https://bit.ly/43lwpYX

RBA Rate Hike: Will 5% Interest Rates Break Australian Property?

In this episode of Talk Property To Me, hosts Brad East and Aaron Downie break down the latest RBA rate rise, the Big Four banks’ interest rate predictions, and what a potential 5% cash rate could mean for Australian property buyers, homeowners, investors and first home buyers.

With the cash rate sitting at 4.35% and some banks warning there could be more rate hikes ahead, Brad and Aaron unpack whether Australia is heading for more mortgage pain — or whether the property market could keep moving despite higher interest rates.

They also discuss why rate rises don’t affect everyone equally, how government spending and inflation are putting pressure on the RBA, why first home buyers may be hit hardest, and why some investors may still find strong opportunities in affordable, undersupplied property markets.

If you’re watching the Australian property market, thinking about refinancing your mortgage, trying to understand your borrowing capacity, or wondering whether now is still a good time to buy property, this episode will help you make sense of what higher interest rates could really mean.

In this episode, we cover:

Why the RBA raised interest rates again

Whether Australian interest rates could reach 5%

What the Big Four banks are predicting next

Why government spending may be making inflation harder to control

How higher rates affect mortgage holders and first home buyers

Why rate rises don’t impact cash buyers the same way

How refinancing could save some homeowners money

Why borrowing capacity is becoming a major problem

How investors are moving into more affordable property markets

Why rental pressure could get worse if buyers are pushed out

Whether higher rates will actually slow the Australian property market

Why some property markets could still grow strongly despite rate rises

🔍 Key Topics Covered

✔️ RBA rate rise Australia
✔️ Australian interest rates
✔️ 5% cash rate prediction
✔️ Big Four banks interest rate forecast
✔️ Australian property market
✔️ Mortgage rates Australia
✔️ First home buyers Australia
✔️ Refinancing home loans
✔️ Borrowing capacity Australia
✔️ Property investing Australia
✔️ Rental market pressure
✔️ Inflation and government spending

⏱️ Timestamps
00:00:00 – Intro
00:00:40 – Why The RBA Rate Rise Matters For Property Buyers
00:01:18 – Energy Rebates, Inflation And Government Spending
00:01:46 – Why Higher Rates Don’t Hit Everyone Equally
00:03:11 – Are You Paying Too Much On Your Mortgage?
00:03:31 – How Much Refinancing Could Save Homeowners
00:04:30 – What Is Really Driving Inflation In Australia?
00:07:04 – Big Four Bank Interest Rate Predictions
00:08:11 – Supply, Demand And The Australian Property Market
00:08:37 – Will Higher Rates Push More People Into Renting?
00:09:17 – Maitland Property Growth And Investor Strategy
00:10:06 – How Rate Rises Impact Borrowing Capacity
00:10:21 – Why First Home Buyers Could Be Hit Hardest
00:11:00 – Why Investors Are Chasing Affordable Markets
00:12:10 – The Real Problem With Higher Interest Rates
00:13:04 – Is It Still A Good Time To Buy Property?
00:13:16 – Property Markets That Could Still Grow Despite Higher Rates
00:14:39 – Final Thoughts: Buy What You Can Afford
00:15:09 – Refinancing, Borrowing Capacity And What To Do Next

💬 What do you think?
Do you think higher interest rates will slow the Australian property market, or will supply shortages and buyer demand keep prices moving? Drop your thoughts in the comments below — we read them all.

Thanks for watching, and if you found value in this episode, don’t forget to like, subscribe and share.

👤 ABOUT THE HOSTS

BRAD EAST
Brad East is the Managing Director of Wisebuy Home Loans, an award-winning mortgage broker who has helped thousands of Australians secure finance through both simple and complex applications.
👉 Website → https://wisebuygroup.com.au

👉 LinkedIn → https://www.linkedin.com/in/newcastlemortgagebroker/

👉 Instagram → https://www.instagram.com/bradeast_mortgagebroker/

👉 Facebook → https://www.facebook.com/bradeastofficial

AARON DOWNIE
Joining the podcast in 2024, Aaron Downie is a seasoned property investor and Director of Mackenzie Buyers Agency, bringing deep market insight and practical investment experience.
👉 Website → https://www.mackenziepropertygroup.com.au/

👉 LinkedIn → https://www.linkedin.com/in/aaron-downie-1b2749134/
Transcript discusses Australian cash rate predictions (Westpac forecasts two more hikes to ~5%, others predict pause), RBA Governor Michelle Bullock criticizes government spending and energy rebates as inflationary. Claims inflation is driven by government spending (180k new federal jobs, office upgrades) not consumer demand. Notes boomer spending up 14%, rate rises disproportionately affect first home buyers and families. Predicts double-digit growth in affordable markets, lower-end market to skyrocket due to investor activity. Recommends refinancing existing mortgages (average 0.5% above market) and highlights cash buyers unaffected. Entities: RBA, Westpac, CBA, NAB, ANZ, Michelle Bullock, negative gearing, capital gains tax, LVR, borrowing capacity.deepseek-v4-flash

What the RBA governor said about the most recent rate rise and basically see if we're in for more pain or whether we're going to be coasting for a while. Yeah. Everyone's obviously seen the headlines everywhere that the RBA has gone and and raised interest rates again. So, we're now sitting at at 4.35, but they're obviously not working together with the government because the government's spending their heads off. Yeah. And they're coming out with these incentives to drive the market. And then they're trying to push back with raising interest rates to slow the market down. So, an interesting thing that the governor came out with yesterday, the RBA governor Michelle Bullock, was that she would like the government to stop giving rebates and incentives on energy because it's actually making their job extremely hard because they're doing these like energy price We know energy prices are out of control for infrastructure regions reasons cuz we're moving to a green energy and it's aging infrastructure. And of course if inflation sticks around it's going to affect all Australians but that's an important distinction distinction between inflation affecting everybody in the economy and then interest rates only affecting the people who are trying to take out mortgages which are families and first home buyers. UCvOPFdkhCBIK2Y5QbDtOZXA		uploaded=2026-05-13T00:00:00.000Z

tags=asx investing, asx share ideas, rask podcast, asx podcast, stock market investing, how to invest, share market investing
Description
In this 2 Sense episode of the Australian Property Podcast, Pete Wargent and Chris Bates react to a Federal Budget that could reshape Australia’s housing market faster than many investors expect.

Recording the morning after the announcement, they break down the headline changes: negative gearing being restricted to new builds from 1 July 2027, a tougher capital gains tax regime, and a new minimum tax rate on trusts. But the real focus is what those changes do to behaviour. Pete and Chris unpack why investor borrowing capacity could be hit, why established-property demand may soften, and why areas with heavy investor ownership could feel the pressure first.

The episode also looks at the flow-on effects for rents, first-home buyers and housing supply. With vacancy rates already tight and rates still elevated, the pair explain why the market could split further between scarce owner-occupier-grade assets and investor-led stock such as some apartments and regional hotspots. They also discuss the likely policy response, including a bigger role for the 5% deposit scheme as government tries to keep demand from falling too hard.

If you want a practical conversation about what the Budget could mean for prices, rents and property strategy from here, this is a timely episode to queue up now.

Episode Chapters:
00:00 Budget Day Reaction
01:28 Why This Budget Is Different
04:05 Key Changes Explained
06:07 Market Impact Overview
07:55 Borrowing Capacity Hit
09:24 Investor Alternatives
13:51 Wealthy Investors vs. Everyday Buyers
16:19 Regional Markets at Risk
20:13 Finding New Equilibrium
22:38 What Happens to Rents
28:39 Possible Workarounds
33:49 Listener Q&A: Dual Income Properties
43:29 Listener Q&A: Rentvesting Strategy
48:43 What to Watch Next

Follow us on social media

DISCLAIMER: This podcast contains general financial information only. That means the information does not take into account your objectives, financial situation, or needs. Because of that, you should consider if the information is appropriate to you and your needs, before acting on it. If you’re confused about what that means or what your needs are, you should always consult a licensed and trusted financial planner. Unfortunately, we cannot guarantee the accuracy of the information in this podcast, including any financial, taxation, and/or legal information. Remember, past performance is not a reliable indicator of future performance. The Rask Group is NOT a qualified tax accountant, financial (tax) adviser, or financial adviser. Access The Rask Group's Financial Services Guide (FSG): https://www.rask.com.au/fsg
Podcast discussion on Australian budget changes to negative gearing (restricted to new builds from July 2027, grandfathering for existing investors) and capital gains tax (minimum 30% rate). Key claims: changes will revalue all properties, reduce investor demand (40% of lending), smash borrowing capacity due to removal of negative gearing benefits from servicing calculations, increase rents, and shift strategies toward owner-occupier homes, share investing, or high-rent properties. Entities: Pete Went, Chris Bates, CBA, Treasury. Risks: oversupply of investor-heavy apartments, decline in new supply, marginalization of renters, and challenges for rentvesting and buyers agents.deepseek-v4-flash

I suppose the big two factors Chris negative gearing and capital gains tax. If we assume that this is going to go through, it absolutely changes the market completely. And I don't think people really get it. I think people just assume it's business as usual. It'll just wash out, etc. It really does revalue every single property on the market in some way. Thanks for watching this RAS podcast. >> Hello, welcome to the Australian Property Podcast. I'm Pete Went from Alan Went Property Buyers. I'm here with Chris Bates from the Alco Group, mortgage specialist. Chris, it's budget day or it's the morning after actually for us as we record this. Uh loads of news in the budget this year, especially for the housing market and property and taxation. So, I thought it would be great to jump on and just spitball some scenarios with somebody who lives and breathes the housing market. So, looking forward to a chat. >> Yeah, I mean it's absolutely crazy. I mean, most budgets are a bit of a non-event really. You know, it's just sort of a political play, but um this one in relation to property um is absolutely huge. I mean, I guess I was sort of, you know, on this show, we just kept saying, you know, we'll just have to wait and see what happens. Wait and see what happens. And I guess I had a belief that you know they were potentially going to obviously CGT was obviously going to get changed and how they actually did it was you know is it going to be um indexation is it going to be a 33% discount you know there was all these things people were making forecast and what was going to happen and I I don't think anyone really predicted what happened there as well so we'll talk about that but even negative gearing I mean I was like are they really going to go for it? Are they going to potentially just make it on new builds? Are they going to grandfather it? are they going to do it on one or two properties? And I think they've gone at it pretty hard and I think it's going to have huge impacts on the market. It's been very interesting the last 24 hours to to sort of watch people uh particularly people who are heavily related to the investment industry and their reactions because it does really change their business model. It's been quite funny watching how they pivoted this morning and and sort of like it's not a big deal but it is absolutely a huge deal and um I think we need to just talk about that because you know I don't even think the broking industry sort of get it as well and I don't think um you know a lot of buyers agencies are so conflicted because that's their model. They have to basically do a 180 on their model and um I don't think they're really grasping that they need to change. It's not um just a small change. >> Yeah. So we haven't had chance to chat yet. This is a this is a real time conversation. But I'm I'm glad to hear we're on the same page because I've seen the same I've seen the Instagram videos and people saying, well, you know, we only target the top uh x% of properties. We won't be impacted. It's like, well, yeah, turn it up. This is a major change for housing taxation. Look, and it might be a moment in time and um you may see that if prices fall and rents rise and things recalibrate and rental yields increase, we could get to a point in maybe a year or two or three years where um things sort of get back to normal in averted commerce, but there's definitely going to be some conions in the short term. So why don't we just step through um what was announced in the budget just briefly and then we can come on to talk about well what happens next uh in terms of the logistics and then what do we think will be the impact for the housing market and then we can talk about uh the strategies that will and won't work in the new environment. I'll just I'll just run through probably the three key points as they relate to the housing market. Uh there was lots of other stuff in the budget as well. Of course, there always is. But this is being a property podcast. Let's do that. So, first thing is so negative gearing uh will be restricted to new builds from the 1st of July 2027. Um for investors in established properties, well, you can carry forward any net rental losses until the property becomes cash flow positive or when the property is sold. And only then do you realize the benefits. So, you still get benefits, but the cash flow in the short term would definitely be a challenge, you would think. Um, existing investors would see their benefits grandfathered and presumably most will just hold on to those properties to keep benefiting. Um, I don't think it will add to dwelling supply. In fact, Treasury forecasts that lower housing price growth will result in 35,000 fewer dwellings being built. Um, but the government's hoping to offset that with some investment in infrastructure. That's the first thing. Second thing, Chris already mentioned, capital gains tax. So, a shift in the method of calculating CGT, but the minimum rate going forward will be 30%. It seems to be no way around that. And that's not just for property. That's for all assets. That'll be commercial property, but also in the stock market. And it doesn't seem logically very progressive because everybody will be paying at least 30% capital gains tax. And then thirdly, maybe a lesser impact on the housing market. There'll be a minimum 30% tax rate on trusts and discretionary trust. So I suppose the big two factors Chris negative gearing and capital gains tax. So yeah, these are it's another broken election pledge. Well, it's actually several broken election pledges all in one go. Last time around it was um household energy and power bills. This time it's wholesale changes to the housing taxation and the legislation thereof. So um so what do you think? Obviously it's huge. Um so what do you think is going to happen? >> Yeah. And I think we're going to be wasting our um energy if we start saying is this fair? Is this unfair? Is this the right decision? Is it not? Like I think they've already made their decision. Does it go through parliament or not? Etc. And we could go and make a lot of noise and talk about all these unintended consequences down the line. But if we assume that this is going to go through, it absolutely changes the market completely. And I don't think people really get it. I think people just assume it's business as usual. It'll just wash out. Um, etc. It really does revalue every single property on the market in some way. And I think people probably aren't grasping that. The grandfathering thing is massively misunderstood because I think what the issue that people think is, "Oh yeah, I've got the tax benefits locked in." So that's great, but that only protects your tax benefits. It doesn't protect the value of your property. The value of a property is based on who is going to buy your property in today's market based on today's rules. And that is different to yesterday because yesterday an investor could buy your property and get negative gearing and pay lower CGT. today they can't get negative gearing and they can for the next 12 months but let's just investors buy for more than 12 months. So going forward investors can't get the negative gearing and they're going to have to pay a lot more CGT potentially depending on how long they hold that property. So it's investors are and the final element which we're trying to figure out today is how banks respond to this with servicing calculated because investors are at the mercy of how much money they can borrow from the bank and at what interest rate and what terms and borrowing capacity is going to be absolutely smashed because negative gearing benefits won't be included when investors go back to banks to see how much money they can borrow. And so the investors are going to be less advantageous because and much harder to hold. You know, if you look at where interest rates are right now, um you know, RBA rate at 4, you know, one and then, you know, maybe it's going to go up a little bit over the next or 4.35, sorry, but then it's going to go up maybe one or two times more. So you're looking at bank interest rates for investments probably closer to 6 and a half%. Right? Particularly because investors go interest only. Then if you add in you know an additional sort of um so that's six and a half% in terms of cost but if you look at yields around the country you know Calality basically released their report it's around 4% around the country right but you have costs so let's say your net yield is maybe closer to 3%. So you're looking at about a 3 to 4% difference between the income versus the cost which is your interest and that is just on a million dollar property that's like 30 to $40,000 a year of negative cash flow. So even if you can borrow it, like not many people are going to be able to afford a negative cash flow that so investors are going to find a extremely hard and why would you want to why would you want to go and be basically down $30 $40,000 a year um with the potential maybe getting some of that back on future rental in some point in the future and then pay more CGT. So I I think it really does change the economics for investors. investors are going to make a decision of well, you know, do I even buy an investment property? Do I go into shares which is, you know, you can negative gear shares still? Do I upgrade my home? You know, do I go into commercial property? It completely changes their fundamental options. And I don't think people, you know, when when investors are basically buying driving 40% of lending right now and most of that is one or two owner property investors. They're not these big portfolio buyers. If you, you know, basically say to them it's no longer that worthwhile for you, you're going to see a huge reduction in invest in lending and when 40% of it is what's driving the market now, that's going to have a huge impact on the buyer pool going forward. >> Well, yeah, completely. So, I think on your point on the legislation, well, that's going to pass through the house easily enough because the government has a majority. I think uh then they would have to go by the end of 2026, they'd have to pass it through this Senate. But I think with the help of the Greens, they would probably manage to do that. I mean, ironically, the Greens would probably want the government to go harder and actually get rid of the grandfathering in the name of intergenerational fairness. But assume, as you said, let's just assume that goes through late 2026, early 2027. So, plenty of time for one July next year. I mean I I suppose one factor which has already happened in New Zealand is that they went down a similar route in New Zealand with interest deductibility and then as soon as there was a change of government all all of it was reversed. So I think it sets up a a potential 2028 election showdown which will basically be on taxation particularly capital gains tax but also negative gearing and also immigration setting. So, the coalition has already said that's what it's going to stand on. But, as you said, Chris, let's just assume for the purposes of this discussion that all of the changes go through and maybe Labour wins the next election anyway, so it stays in place. Well, uh, Trent Saunders of CBA said prices will fall 3% as a result of the changes and rents would rise gradually. I think, well, this is the big question, isn't it? It's a debate that's been going on more or less since 1987, the last time the negative gearing had been um scrapped and then reintroduced within a couple of years because rents surged in Sydney and Perth. I mean, as you said, um if you got a house in Australia, the gross rental yield about 3 and a half% uh for a unit, the gross rental yield 4 and a half%. But if you're borrowing at 6 and a half% practically nobody is going to be looking at that saying well let's buy an established property uh from uh well from today basically because as an investor because those losses are just going to get carried forward in perpetuity. You're never going to get to a cash flow positive situation. Uh so you would only sort of get to recoup those losses when you sell. And if the market is getting smashed anyway, um the the the growth prospects are presumably lower anyway. So I think the biggest question though is what happens to rents? Um you know, you know, a lot of the debate around 1985, 1987 period was that we had relatively low vacancy rates in Sydney and Perth and then rents exploded. Um yeah, one way or another those rental yields have to go I would say at least 50 basis points higher if not more which means prices have to come down, rents have to go up, a combination of the two but one way or another those rental yields just have to go higher. So I think um yeah I mean in the meantime I think a lot of the property advisers will be sproing house and land packages because there will still be negative gearing for new builds. So, be very wary about that big uh surge of property advice that's about to come down the pipe. Um, but I think yeah, I think a lot of people the rent vesting strategy will go out the window for the next couple of years. I think more people will focus on uh buying a place of residence or renovating a place of residence or um and I think at the top end of the market the wealthy will actually just go towards the one asset which is still capital gains taxfree the family home and I think actually at the bottom end of the market probably that would be supported by the 5% deposit scheme for first home buyers and rising construction costs and financing costs. I think it's that it's just that broad middle section of the market that's going to really see the most impact and particularly areas where there's a lot of investors or investment properties. Well, that that whole dynamic now presumably will have to change. >> Yeah. So, a lot of the content I've seen is ah, you know, the wealthy aren't going to have it. And it's true. The ones with big portfolios with a lot of wealth can restructure things because they're not relying on sort of the neg they can afford the negative cash flow and they can sort of offset that cash flow against other properties and they can use company structures and they can use but that's a very very small part of the market. We're talking sort of well under 1% of investors are in this camp and so they're not going to be able to support when there's 3 million investment properties out there if they can potentially still you know and they're going to find it harder if they can still buy well that's different but the other 3 million properties are owned by sort of families you need that's the demand you got to worry about and so the person who maybe has a house they've got a bit of equity they're looking to do something for their retirement they're looking to build some wealth typically they would go and buy an investment property right Now, they would go, "Well, how can I afford the negative cash flow on holding that plus pay my mortgage, and I'm going to have to hold that, you know, and the negative cash flow, I have no idea when that's going to end, you know, I don't know what rents are going to do. I don't know what interest rates are going to do. Um, I'm going to have to pay more CGT when I sell it. Um, I'm also buying very late in a cycle." You can see that a lot of investors spend under 700 grand. I mean, um, you know, real estate.com.au you did said that 50% of research uh searches are under 700 grand. We know that we know that you know particularly for the last 5 years the the smaller and the lower end of the markets where a lot of investors have gone. But like that makes it really hard to afford the negative cash flow on those properties. So those investors just aren't going to be there. like it's just the the the reality is that they're just not going to able to afford the negative cash flow or have the desire to do it. Um and you know they got to um unless they see a massive decrease in prices and you know where the real risk is is that people naturally investors sometimes do have to sell divorce death they're better used to put that money into other things like pay off their mortgage or retire or you know help the kids out etc. My worry is when you look at particularly in the capital cities and um you know investors maybe in say in the Brisbane apartment market or the Melbourne or or even the Sydney apartments if a lot of these apartments have been driven heavily by investors and if a lot of those investors decide to bail yes they've grandfathered their benefits but you know if those markets you need a lot more investors to be entering those because every time you sell those now another investor is not going to buy it has to be a first home buyer and you just there's a there's a cap on how many first-time home buyers can actually enter the market at any point. And you know, if if five people in your block decide to sell because they're worried that there's not enough strong long-term investor demand, then you've all of a sudden you can see an over supply of, you know, listings. Um so I I my worry is definitely in any market around the country where it's got a lot of um investors or are reliant on a lot more investors entering to support prices and don't have really strong first home buyer demand. You know um you know these high density apartments that were poorly built for the last 20 30 years I think are in a lot of trouble. The second thing is I think that a lot of markets around the country and I reckon this is also why they've um done this policy is the proliferation of buyers agents this investment focused buyers agents that are borderless buying anywhere in the country have gone away from buying in capital cities have gone to sister locations to the the city center and have now gone to regional locations and have pushed the price of properties up way past what locals can afford. I think they've been displacing locals and I think what the those communities are yes if you had a property in a regional town you're like yeah great I I it was only worth 400 now it's worth 800 I've made money but you're not looking to leave you you know you're looking to stay here and you know that your kids and your friends and those ones who haven't um got into the market can't afford to stay yes they can rent but they can't afford to buy and I reckon that anyone who's basically bought in the last four years in these regional locations are going to be Hey, how do I afford to keep this property? Yeah, I got negative gearing benefits, but I can't refinance my loan because um you know, lending's way tighter. Interest rates have gone up on me and I need that money because I want to I want to buy a house one day and I'm better off to sort of bank that money. And so I think you could easily see these markets correct where a lot of those investors bail and there's no new investors buying in there. So it goes back to only what the locals can afford. Um, and so yeah, I'm not trying to be a doomsday here, but I do think there's some fundamental flaws because, you know, the when market commentary comes out, it'll say, look, there's a 2 or 3% impact of prices. People don't buy the market. There's 11 12 million dwellings out there. People have got individual properties, and some people will go will be really affected, particularly in investor hotspots in recent years or long-term driven by investment markets like apartments for example. Um, and some people will actually benefit if you own a really good asset. You know, this is why we're not concerned as a business for our clients. It's why Pete, you're probably not concerned about your clients that have been buying for the last 5 10 years because you would have bu bought scarce properties that are driven by the owner occupy market that aren't driven by investment markets. And so even if the investment market pulls out, it's not driven the current price isn't valued based on investors. It's highly likely that owner occupy would buy it. And so those markets, if anything, the investors in those will say, "Well, yeah, I'm not concerned about a flood of the market. I've got negative gearing. I've got a lower CGT. Um, I'm just going to hold this property." And I think you're going to see this two-tier market, this flight to quality, not just um, you know, current investors holding, but you know, you know, buying your home becomes even more enticing because you know that that's going to grow tax-free and you can't even afford to buy an investment property anyway. >> Well, totally. Yeah. And I agree completely that in the short term this is going to be messy. I think um yes you can model an a decrease in prices of 3% or 4% or whatever but as you said at the local level if you've got no buyer for your property then yeah the the modeling goes out the window and the sentiment impact is going to be large I would say especially in a rising interest rate environment which we now have. Um, if I I mean I suppose I've done a bit of content in the last 24 hours and if I sound relatively sanguin I I guess it's more just from I guess partly due to the gray hairs appearing on my head but you do see over time a lot of these things come and go and markets in particular have a way of finding an equilibrium. So yes um there'll be a short-term recalibration or adjustment but sooner or later things get back into some kind of equilibrium. I think as well if you actually look at housing markets really over the longer term anyway what drives them is supply and demand not so much the tax benefits although as you said this does have a second order impact on borrowing capacity for investment properties but I guess if you look at the budget papers um yes they're still forecasting immigration to fall but actually the figures have been revised up uh by quite significantly by more than 50,000 I think actually the AB PS put out figures this week on arrivals and departures. Arrivals are at all-time highs and yes, there could be some double counting in those figures, but clearly immigration has stopped slowing and if anything, it's it's been rising now for the last um 12 to 18 months. But I think in terms of the rental yields, I mean, just to put some numbers around it, um let's say you you want to see a 50 basis points increase in rents. Well, that's going to happen. And I think as we've talked about on this podcast before, it's not so easy just to say, well, an investor sells and a home buyer buys and it's just a straight one for one swap. I mean, I think yeah, rents are unfortunately going to go up. I mean, that and that's hard for inflation, right? Because it's included in inflation. So, then that is also inflationary. Um, I think what you really need to probably also can think about is the 5% deposit scheme has very been very calculated here. If you're a local in those markets and you've seen the price risen so much, you've already been forced out because the it's gone way beyond what you can borrow as a as a as a couple. And borrowing capacity has fallen over the last 12 months. Hasn't gone up. it's gone, you know, has gone down because interest rates have been going back up. So, locals borrowing capacity has been falling together with their appetite and fear has been. So, local demand is going to be lower and then there's going to be no new investment demand into these locations. And then also, if you're an investor in those locations and you say bought for 500, it's worth 900. It doesn't take a rocket science to think, hang on a sec, should I just bank my money? And then all of a sudden an investor sells and then another investor sells and you get this no locals want to buy at current prices because there's choice and then all of a sudden this mark another investor sees what is it now worth 800. I mean, this is the thing with illquid housing markets. If you go to the regions, a lot of times you had a huge influx of investors in a really small market where you've got maybe just um a few dozen properties for sale and prices have just rocketed higher. You have to sell it to a home buyer because a, you know, and a home buyer is going to say, "Well, why would I buy something that's 2, three, four, five years old when I can buy something new and I get the new oven and I get the new, you know, everything's new?" You know, why would I buy a 2, three year old investment property um and pay the same price as what I could get something new? And with building prices already gone up so much, it's unlikely that you you know those investors are going to benefit from building prices going up and you know new costs more in a few years time like it maybe but you know my worry would be that you see these proliferation investors going to new property they get the negative gearing benefits and then they go hang on a sec when they sell it well they have to sell it to a home buyer and home buyer says I don't want that I I'm just going to go buy something new myself um and actually get the new oven and and not something that's been lived in by a renter for the last 2 3 years. Uh, is that one of your concerns as well, Pete, or how do you see it? >> Yeah, totally. Yeah, I mean, when this came to the 2019 election, May 2019, there was one of the things that the modeling talked about was that yes, you you created a two-tier market now with housing. So, an investor buys a new apartment or a new off the plan house and land package or townhouse and they've got negative gearing benefits. But when they come to sell, oh, there's no resale market because the next buyer, if they're an investor, they don't have access to negative gearing benefits and therefore the property is not worth what it was what they paid for it and then they're sitting on a loss and then they don't want to sell. So, I think that'll be another interesting dynamic to watch. In theory, in the short term, we might get proliferation of people going into new builds. It could sort of generate some new supply. But I think if you map that out over a few years, I think actually housing supply is going to be slower than otherwise would have been the case just because we haven't got any price growth and who's who's going to build in that environment or who's going to buy new in that environment. You already mentioned interest rates going up, construction costs shot up again. Uh so the housing supply factor is going to be huge I think. Um yeah I mean so I guess those are the things to watch. What happens to supply? What happens to rents and yeah does the government actually step in and try and pump the first home buyer market because otherwise everything drops off a cliff. Stamp duty take at the state level does and um yeah it could get pretty messy. So I've got um couple of questions. >> Yeah. No one's going to be buying new apartments because like developers can't make this happen. They've building prices have gone up. interest rates have gone up like they were already struggling to get feasibility to work. Um to actually make a profit on those developments is ridiculously hard. And so this government idea that all these investors are going to go in the 2015 and build all these apartments and you know create all this rental stock, it's just not going to happen. Um and so you know this this you know the idea that this supply of rentals is going to get replaced with new stock unfortunately isn't the case. And unfortunately, you will find that rents will go up, but also the problem with a rental crisis is it doesn't hit everyone evenly. And if you do get kicked out of your rental and then you have to go and find a new one and vacancy rates are really low and there's 10 applications for that property, the investor or the owner of that property is going to go for the one with the best application. And that means that nine others don't get it. And particularly if you're, you know, self, you know, single, you know, you're not on a high income, you haven't got much savings, you know, like you're just going to get marginalized. You're just going to get pushed to the bottom. And the person who's got the full-time job, um, or a couple with two full-time jobs is going to get the rental. And I think we're going to see some major issues. I would say you know they're the ones who've you know often are saying negative gearing and CGT discounts the problems with our property market. And unfortunately I think what they're going to do is do the opposite. But I think they're actually going to create a massive rental shortage and a lot of the people that they're there to represent are going to have bigger issues than they had prior to this change. That's not great for anyone renting, but it's really all the people that get marginalized uh through that as well. >> There's lots of question marks.
