Uh let's go through a couple of questions from our listener Q&A. So uh we had a couple of uh people just ping off messages after the budget. One from Brave Beth uh on property strategy. I've got one investment property. I'm now thinking to shift strategy to dual income properties. What do you think? Um, yeah, I can see what you're thinking because, um, if you can't get negative gearing on established properties, then you obviously want to higher rental income one way or another, whether that's commercial property. I think the with the risks or the potential challenges with dual income properties. Well, firstly, just getting them set up in the first place. The upfront costs can be higher because you got to have separate utilities, potentially approvals could be expensive and you get budget overruns. You've also got potentially regulatory issues at the council level. Also, if you've got more than one tenant, you've got a bit more complexity in the management. And I think as well, you just got to be a bit careful because as Chris will tell you, a lender doesn't always assume that you've got two in two tenants there the whole time, paying full rent the whole time. So, the borrowing capacity might not be as strong as you might think. Um, and also I think um to the previous point, you've got to think about one day you're presumably going to sell this property and who's going to buy it? Your your buyer, you know, or the the resale pool is potentially going to be a lot thinner because you're really going to be selling to another investor. So, uh, I can see your thinking. Uh, but you just got to be aware of those risks and, um, yeah, it's certainly not for everyone. What do you think? Look, I think the ones that will do the top properties that could work in this scenario is if you've got a really highly attractive owner occupier property that's scarce that is um not going to be replicated that you know is going to become more and more desirable to sort of families to sort of live in within our capital cities for example and the incomes are there and then you can create multiple incomes off that without destroying the quality of that asset. for that family buyer. So, you know, like a studio apartment above a garage that, you know, that's got its own private access that you know is attractive to a family market who, you know, wants to have friends or family stay with them. So, you can have two incomes on the property, but it's not like you've cut your block up in half and you've lost your backyard to put a, you know, a little granny flat out the back and the family market saying, "Hang on a sec." Like, I want that backyard. I want to use it. I want, you know, having a garage or a pool there or something. So, you know, this whole like multiple income, you know, off a property, you've got to be really careful because if another investor doesn't buy that in the future and their family market doesn't want it, you could basically butcher your asset. So, be really careful. So, but if you can get multiple rental incomes on a good asset, you know, that income is going to be really important because it's going to reduce the, you know, the outlay or the capital that you have to basically support that property assuming you can't get negative gearing. We haven't spoke about it Pete but the rise of share investing is absolutely going to be the case coming out of this you know um and you know I think that's good for society like you know we should be encouraging people to be investing in the stock market and you know not just here and around the world and and and you know that goes to companies and and start we got more and more funds you know people want to raise money I mean the irony is that you know a lot of companies worth a lot of money right now in the stock market um but you know uh I think that's the thing I do I reckon they'll do some carve outs Personally for early stage company and you know investing in private companies I think that you know that's a big gap in their policy. It's very anti- >> sort of short-term sort of investing. You know you're trying to make gains over short time frames in companies. Like for example if your friend's starting a business and you go look I want to invest in them and but I don't want to invest for 10 15 years. Well, at the moment, you would say, "Well, yeah, I I I I'll give you $100,000 and if I sell that in 3 years time and I make $500, well, I'm going to pay a huge amount of capital gains tax." Um, and so it's not really sort of it's I think it's anti- sort of establishment of new businesses and the tech and I think there'll be some tweaks that they'll make for that type of investing, I reckon. Um but I also think as if you you will see the rise of share investing and you'll see that people will use their capacity because you can negatively gear it they can sort of dollar cost in through the markets and I also think that people will just naturally go for the best home they can afford because they know that that's going to grow for them tax-free and and they go well we can't afford to buy an investment property should we you know just renovate and I think there'll be a renovation boom as well because people will go well you know I've got that capacity there I can afford a bigger mortgage. I'm not going to go and spend it on another investment property. Why don't we just renovate? And so I think that will support um you know, but that also pushes up prices of properties of houses because it in you know, increases the the streetscape and the gentrify suburbs faster as well. Um yeah, what do you think Pete? Anything else? >> Well, yeah, I think you're right. I think it it's not really at least what's been announced today doesn't seem particularly favorable for startups or entrepreneurialism or even the share market more broadly with the capital gains tax but as you say there could be some carveouts to come so let's not preempt that I also particularly enjoyed your scala pronunciation of garage there you're a true liver of puddlin at heart it seems so uh yeah let's we've got one more question here from rent vesttor um I was thinking to do a rent vesting strategy Should I just buy a first home instead? I mean that's pretty broad and open-ended question but yes I mean I suppose it's definitely there'll be a lot of people thinking the same thing if um because at the moment we've got people in city like Sydney where they're saying okay well I can't really afford to live in Clove Valley but what I can do is rent somewhere close to the city and then maybe I can use my borrowing capacity to buy a few properties in cheaper locations around the country and I can use that maybe later on as a stepping stone onto the housing ladder. Well, if you're not getting negative gearing benefits, well, yeah, a lot of people will be just looking to buy a first home instead and just getting whatever they can afford. So, yeah, I'd certainly say speak to a mortgage broker about um you know, your potential borrowing capacity. I think generally speaking, conditions are more supportive actually for the bottom end or the entry level property because developers have largely stepped away from that space and we've got these first home buyers coming in. So from that perspective, the market conditions are more positive for that. Um, but also say, yeah, if you're not sure, take some advice. Um, obviously I don't know your full circumstances. Have a chat to an advisor. Um, yeah, and also probably a mortgage broker as well. But what do you think, Chris? I think rent investing is just not going to be the thing that it was. And as you said, there's been a stack of businesses that have sprung up over the last 10 years telling people, don't worry about buying a home, just buy rental properties all around the country. Um, I think that model is going to die. What do you think? >> My WhatsApp's going absolutely nuts. Um, of all our team around lending changes. I just got an email around SMSF lending, you know, um 90% up to 4 million, you know, like it's we, you know, there's a lot of people that are going to be extremely concerned with this change where it's completely changed their business model. Us as a business, it's not. You know, we we as a have always been focused on really buying scarce assets for our clients and very much owner occupier focused and very make sure people think through their home and their decision and if they are going to buy investments be extremely careful what they buy, where they buy and look to buy, you know, scarce assets that they're driven by in occupying markets and not try to chase short-term hotspots. And so for us, it's a little bit of a pivot. You know, the clients that maybe have going down the investment route, you know, do they look at upgrading their home? do they look at shares, you know, do they even, you know, and they and they potentially, you know, property is just not something they can afford to do, right? So, we just have to but you know, for some businesses right now, they're going to have to try to find new ways. And I think that, you know, that renting strategies is extremely challenging right now. The other thing as well is that yes, prices at the bottom have been going up for the last 3 4 years at a higher rate than the medium and the higher end of the market. and the medium and the higher end of the market's absolutely hamstrung because interest rates are high, borrowing capacity is tight. But at some point that that you know when I guess there is more confidence around inflation, I'm not saying that's this year or next year or the year after but at some point and you know the the government tries to stimulate the economy with say lower rates, you will find that that market will come back and and the gap between their current property and their next jump is smaller than it probably has been. If you compare that to say 2122 and you know particularly if that low end of the market keeps getting supported because a lot of first-time home buyers are going there and so they they will naturally at some point in their lives are sort of getting big the kids are getting bigger or they're outgrowing their apartments. The other element is this is you you know we've seen the capital cities basically have all these approvals for apartments and um you know we're increasing zoning etc. I I generally don't think that they're selling. I I think that, you know, they're great developments. They look good on paper, but the older generation who they're targeting are really hesitant to to basically um to buy them, you know, unless they're really scarce, you know, development that is never going to get built out on. They're just in their dream location, they're really good builder, they're really confident on, they will buy them. But, you know, anything that's just a bit average, why would you buy it? Because you just have no idea what's going to happen to planning or zoning and it's going to take 2, three, four years for it to come out of the ground. And those developers would be like, "Well, yeah, I know I thought it was 3 million, but we're not going to make any money on that. We have to increase it to 3.3 million." And so, I think that and why would you downsize? You know, if if you know that if if if the number one thing that is going to get taxfree is your home, why would you want extra cash right now to put it into assets where you're going to have to pay higher CGT? You can't put more money into your super. And so this is very also um anti- downsizing as well because if you left your money, you know, in your home, it would have kept growing taxfree versus if I put it into shares or I got to pay high CGT if I put into my super, I can't do that anymore. So I think you'll find that a lot of the older generation will just sit on their hands even more so. um because the home still is protected. >> Yes, there's a lot to play out before the next election uh May 2028 or earlier and um in the meantime there's going to be a lot of jostling and a lot of changes and no doubt there'll be plenty of opinions out there over the weeks ahead. Um so we'll be here to guide you and report on what's happening. Uh so as usual, try and keep a cool head and uh if you're not sure about what to do next, have a chat to your advisor and uh send in your questions as well cuz we always like to cover whatever is causing you headaches or otherwise in the market. So Chris, what have you got planned for the next week? >> Uh we're actually going to Cber on the weekend to a friend's got a farm down there or her dad her dad's got a farm and um every year they do sort of a a working bee. It's an olive farm. He hasn't had volley for a few years, but it's still fun going around and trimming the branches and he's got little sugar gliders there that come and sort of land on you and we plant trees and we swim in the dam and so um yeah, it's it's going to be good fun. Bit of line dancing and a bush bush indig. So, uh that's that's this weekend. >> Very good. Well, it's cricket season for me. I'm playing a few games of cricket at the moment. So, uh yeah, I'm just about holding together at the moment. Haven't broken a rib or dislocated a shoulder just yet. Um so, yeah, fingers crossed I stay fit. Um, so yeah, send in your questions and um, yeah, always looking forward to covering those and thanks for your insights today, Chris. Sounds like it could be an interesting year ahead. >> Yeah, and let's just keep tracking this week by week because obviously you're right, there's going to be lots of pivots, but um, yeah, don't fall for the the nonsense right now. This isn't a big deal. It absolutely is. And, you know, some people, if anything, are going to be, you know, take a big sigh of relief. If you got a good asset right now, you're like, "Yeah, few like this is good." But if you've got an asset that is a bit more compromised, and that's the other thing I haven't said, I know I've said a lot today, but the in the regional locations, the big issue with these buyers agents is because they were buying on scale, they didn't buy the best properties in the suburbs. They bought busier roads. They bought the south facing blocks. They bought the ones with weird blocks, the worst part of the suburbs, the they because they just had to buy fast. It was if you didn't buy now, prices were running and they got so many clients buying. And so that famous quote, when the tide goes out, I think what you'll find out is a lot of these investors will go, "Hang on a sec. Yeah, you bought in that location, but you didn't buy me a good asset and I can't sell it right now." Um, because it is one of the poor properties in the suburbs. So, yeah, I think there's going to be a huge test to these buyers agency businesses um because I think that the quality of their assets are really going to get shown. >> To be fair, you've been very consistent on that point and you called it right. The thing that's uh making me furrow my brow is I can I could just picture people now saying that the new thing to do is going to house and land packages in the boondocks and uh yeah the next round of investors losing money will be uh springing from that well. So let's wait and see what plays out but um yeah thanks for all your thoughts today Chris and look forward to chatting next time around. >> See you next week. >> Cheers. VMemhtlsoNkhttps://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VMemhtlsoNkAgents Don't Do Standups: Building the Post-Engineer Engineering Org — Mike Spitz, PFFAI Engineer
UCLKPca3kwwd-B59HNr-_lvA		uploaded=2026-05-15T00:00:00.000Z
duration=17:49
views=7,897
likes=214
transcript langs=
tags=ai, ai engineer, ai engineering, software development, tech, startups, software architecture, machine learning
Description
PFF ran a three-month case study: two engineers against a team of ten, same codebase, same customers. The two shipped five times a day. The ten shipped once every five days. Output measured by ticket complexity came out at 10x. Customer satisfaction went up, not down. Mike Spitz, their CTO, started with one reframe: stop asking how to help engineers go faster and ask how to make the agents faster instead.

The talk covers what that reframe actually dismantled. Standups went away because tickets auto-update from PR state. Sprint planning went away because estimates are irrelevant when the bottleneck is no longer human. Code review got split: agents handle style and naming, engineers handle system design. The spec to lightweight design doc to auto-generated ticket to PR flow replaced most of the coordination overhead entirely. What survived is a short huddle every other day, a strong opinion about which engineers thrive in this setup, and a QA agent that spins up on staging after every merge and checks acceptance criteria against the ticket.

Speaker info:
- https://x.com/mikespitz_uk
- https://www.linkedin.com/in/mike-spitz-89741243/
Case study at PFF (sports data company) Jan-Mar: two engineers using AI agents achieved 25x more deploys, 10x output, and customer satisfaction 8.6/10 (up from 7-7.5). Scrum replaced with huddles, spec/LDD flow, auto ticket creation, no sprints or standups. Agents handle code reviews on trivial matters and QA. Use feature flags, trunk-based development. Recommendations: pick best engineers, slow phased approach, experiment on non-critical systems, encode engineering patterns in composable skills, start with boring tasks. Highlights need to adapt process and culture for AI era.deepseek-v4-flash

Review transcript
[music] >> All right. So, I'm in the slot before the snacks. I'm here to speak about the host engineering org and a case study we've been doing at PFF started in January and we finished it in March. PFF is a sports data company. We help NFL and NCAA teams figure out what they should be doing and we also have a consistent We also have a consumer arm which does fantasy football, sport betting and for those of you who know American football there's a draft happening end of the month and so allows people to basically play as one of these teams. We're fully distributed engineering team. So, we got engineers in India, we got engineers in Spain and all the states in America. Um but first let's go into some kind of stats. So, we have 100 million page views annually. We have 9 million drafts happen on an annual basis. It's a fairly popular tool. And the issue that we had was we were 200 employees around 20 engineers and we were falling behind our competitors. We were focusing on sport betting stuff, but a lot of our consumers were actually interested in this and then with Cloud Ops coming out, I started experimenting around November on a personal level and then it got spun out to two engineers. One of them was our strongest front end engineer and the other one was one of our strongest full stack engineer. So, really the question is asking was instead of figuring out how we can help engineers go and output more, how do we help make the how do you help make how do you help make the agents quicker? Right. So, I think if we all think historically software engineering you got the Agile Manifesto, software craftsmanship. Um you've got a lot of good perks and benefits for engineers from foosball tables to sleeping pods to pretty much every perk that most other industries really don't have and it's cuz we're the bottleneck and if companies are able to go and optimize for that, it gives them a lot of benefit. But now things are changing. So, I just want to go straight into how the case study ended up. So, we had a 25 times more 25 times more of deploys. So, the two engineers were deploying five times five times every five were deploying five times every day and the other team was a team of around 10 engineers. They're doing it pretty much one deploy every five days. There's a big obvious caveat here, right? Smaller engineering teams are always going to be quicker than the big ones. So, there is an element of that multiplier that is just from it being a smaller engineering team. However, that smaller target team still had to coordinate all of those daily still had to go and coordinate all of those deploys with the bigger team. So, there was still that there was still that kind of issue that was happening. And this one this is always hard. How do you validate like the output is actually helpful? And the number of PRs isn't helpful. The amount of code isn't helpful. So, we basically blended the number of tickets with the code complexity and we found that their we found that their that their output was 10x. Got another slide which might look a little bit hectic, but just bear with me. Um So, these were the features that we went and constructed with those two engineers. It took them under two months. If you're going to do this before, we were estimating it's going to take four months. The big thing you can probably have a look in the in the top half is one of the engineers gets unblocked in under a month and then start building other stuff. Whereas in the old way, they're both blocked for three months and so you get this thing where you now have a compounding increase where it's not just faster for that stuff, but you're now able to do a lot more stuff than you were able to do before. And the one thing that I really want to go and basic basic go and highlight is doesn't matter if the output's more. It doesn't matter if the number of deployments are higher. What really matters is really matters is basically if our customers if the customers are happy. And so we did statistically we did statistically significant surveys and the average quality score was 8.6 out of 10. What's interesting was before AI we would probably average seven seven and a half. So, we weren't really delivering what the what the customers had been interested in. So, Scrum did not survive. We were we were basically having a look not just on an engineering front and all of the delivery gains from that, but also from a process standpoint. And so, no need for a project manager. We don't need to play multiple games of telephone and everything we're doing was optimizing to be as quick as possible. Engineers aren't the bottleneck. So, we don't need to have all the old ceremonies that we had before. So, what did we have? It looks pretty basic. We basically had a We basically had huddles. So, those huddles were basically every other day. They'd be like half an hour maybe an hour. You'd have the engineers, someone from product, someone from the design team and you would speak about the things you've been building the last a couple days. You get instant feedback. We would try to deploy to production as fast as possible in an MVP state and get as much feedback as possible also. And that development flow is we have a spec. I think most of us are probably fairly familiar around this, but we get the agent to go and interview us. We get feedback on that spec and then we make a lightweight design document. That's done by That's done by the agent. So, we have like a skill and the and the nice thing about that is it analyzes how we've done all the LDDs before and so anything that we're building is in the same kind of ethos as everything else that's been built. So, it's not a cloud code It's not like a cloud code specific kind of thing. And then those LDDs they get distributed and we get feedback from all of the the engineers. And then at that stage we automatically create all we automatically create all the tickets and then the PRs after that. So, sprint planning we don't have sprint planning anymore cuz we don't need to have an hour going and basically estimating tickets cuz those estimations don't really make any difference. Something we're not doing at the moment, but we will probably have to do once the once the subsidization of tokens ends is we'll probably have to go and estimate the token expenditure to go see if we want to actually spend this amount of money on it. The daily standups we don't have to do cuz all these tickets get auto updated. That is specific to the status of the PR. So, so with the PRs open it goes auto in progress. If it goes into review it updates it, it gets merged, it gets closed. These things were obviously manageable to do before, but it's all been made a little bit easier. Sprint refinement we don't need to do cuz that happens in the spec and the LDD flow and then when we automatically create all of the tickets, we structure it in a manner so that the tickets are made so none of them are blocking each other and if there are ones that are blocking each other, that information is flagged up and highlighted. And retrospective this one might be a little controversial, but we rely on the customer satisfaction, the customer survey. That's the main thing really and then you got the normal development metrics like the deployment frequency. We ask all of our engineers to really flag when there is an issue to flag it immediately and to instead of hanging on till the end of the sprint to go and go and flag that issue where sometimes I think all of us have probably felt that our our feedback hasn't really been heard cuz it's been a sprint before. Oops, sorry. So, how do we start? You got to pick the engineers which have the best system in the knowledge and I think every engineering team probably has one or multiple engineers where if anyone ever gets kind of stuck or hung up on anything they're like, you should speak to them. They'll be able to sort it out. The other one is you should you should go slowly. So um there is an appetite to give everyone all the coding assistance and open it all up, but I think with this you want a slow phased approach. And then you should experiment in non-critical systems. So, that's what I did in the November in the in like the two months before the case study. I was making small proof of concept features going going and pushing it to production. Didn't really get much attraction. So, if there was a bug or a mistake it didn't really matter and then we moved it to the thing which gets 100 million page views after that. Um the big big thing here though is not everyone can drive a sports car. And that's all right. There's That's not a big issue, but I think I think everyone needs to be a little bit honest around the engineering org and engineering team. And this new era is going to be hard for a few engineers. And I think the the type of engineer will there really strive is one who is curious. It's the engineer who if they haven't figured it out, they're going to spend a little bit of time just figuring out how something's been built. They'll be able to still be able to they'll be able to smash this easily. The old style of engineering which needs something really uh prescriptive as a spec, I think they're going to struggle. So, in the engineering side of things, you need verifiable verifiable verifiable deterministic tasks. I think a bunch of people spoken about this today. So, it ranges from everything from all the different types of tasks. But, there's also things which are specific for your product or your org. So, for us, it's feature flag cuz we do a trunk-based development. Um or it could be generating the interactive elements. Um generating the analytics for the interactive elements. Um Next thing is the agentic code review. So, with us, we didn't really like relying on agents to do code reviews for system design and what engineers would usually do code reviews for. We use agents to do the code reviews that engineers hate getting any feedback from. So, that's like the variable names, this doesn't fit the style or like those kind of opinionated manners is much easier to offload. Uh and then you can remove that whole kind of emotional aspect out of it. And then you just allow the engineers to focus on the big picture. Um we still need people involved. But, it's really heavy on the spec, really heavy on the LDD, the lightweight design document where you figure out how we're going to build this. And the thing I haven't touched on at the moment is the product. We're in an era where everyone's able to create anything in an hour. But, a lot of these tools have the brand feel and the product feel of something that's been created by a cloud code. And so, if you really want to get the best out of it, you really need to make sure that the engineering team is spending time making sure it still feels like basically every other product from the company. So, how would I recommend attacking this? You should go see your engineering development life cycle like a like a kind of a like a kind of a factory. So, you should think you're in a factory. How do you break that up into the each small composable element? So, in a car factory, there's one thing which is building a building a building a a door, another one fitting a steering wheel. The exact same thing happens for engineering, right? So, you have the branch name, you've got uh creating feature flags with trunk-based development, you've got the if you build APIs with a specific software design a pattern, you should abstract that into a composable skill. The one thing I would flag is I'm not a big fan of um a consuming other people's skills if they have strong software kind of opinions that are in contrast to the today's engineering org cuz you're just going to kind of end up in issues. So, you just need to make sure that any skills that are getting composed are still kind of matching. Uh And yeah, this is just at the moment pretty much everything is fully autonomous from the spec, LDD, ticket from the PR. Uh we have a QA process also. So, what happens is um whenever we merge a PR, it automatically deploys onto staging. When the deployment on staging has happened, we will spin up a a QA agent which has a look at all of the tickets that have happened, has a look at the acceptance criteria, and it will go and then QA against that. And then if everything's a pass, I mean, great. And and then if everything hasn't, it will flag what those items are. And the bit we haven't gone and done at the moment, but I'm um I'm but I'm aiming in the next um a couple months is to then have an agent have a look at those tickets, find out where the acceptance criteria hasn't happened, and then automatically create the PRs. And so, then you get into this flow where agents can basically uh self-heal. And the cool thing about that is it lets us do multiple things in a parallel cuz we're now in a position where we basically trust agents as well. Uh where do you still use people? I mean, I've experienced this, but they do like to use they they do like to use they like to use shortcuts. Um So, got to make sure the the the security side of things is behaving. A product feel, I spoke about that a little bit earlier. And then the scale and engineering complexity for task, the LDD is really meant to help on this. So, I think we've all experienced where it'll make a thousand lines of code or over-engineer something. If you're really prescriptive on that engineering document at the start, you can really uh prevent that from happening. So, what things should you go and aim for? You should start with boring repetitive tasks, ideally things engineers hate cuz you're going to get the most amount of buy-in from that. Remove as much redundant process as possible. And I know it can be strange when you've been doing something for like a two decades plus to just uh chuck it all out, but I would ask you, what is the purpose of this meeting? What's the purpose of this a process? Is it just cuz everyone else has been going and doing it before, or is it cuz it actually helps out? Uh make sure your team's a personal kind of engineering culture and the patterns are encoded in skills. So, if there are software design patterns that you really do, so for us, whenever we build an API, we focus on the service repository pattern. Uh make sure your guardrails are fully functional before you get into before you get into an autonomous flow. And you should start out with the best engineers. Things you should not do is to try and onboard everyone at the exact same at time. Uh I think a big reason why a lot of these things haven't really panned out is cuz we've given everyone a cloud code codex and given them a hackathon and like, "Sweet, we've done everything. They should now be able to sort it all out." It's just not that easy. Every engineering org is completely different uh and every engineering org's style is really different. And you just need to go and have a slow-phased approach to make sure uh that that transition is happening smoothly. And that's why I think those small companies are at a huge advantage of the big enterprise companies cuz it's really easy for me to scale this out when I've got 20 engineers. It's really hard for me to scale this out if I was in charge of 100 or if I was in charge of 1,000 engineers or if I was in charge of 10,000 engineers. So, um but you don't want to be too shy. You don't want to be too You don't want to be too conservative because the fact is that there's a lot of other companies that are going full speed. And um Um for us, even from our perspective, I felt a few months behind and I was kind of feeling it. And I think if you think about the compounding impact that I was talking about at the start, that's just going to carry on happening. So, like a few months behind at the moment might be six months behind in a few months, might be 12 months behind uh a little bit afterwards. And then again, just just the just uh take it slow. Uh needs a phased needs a phased kind of approach and rely on your engineers to let you know whether you can scale things faster or if it needs a bit more time. I know I spoke to you about a lot of features that we built, but I didn't show you any of the features. Uh if anyone does want to have a play around, feel free to scan the the QR code and then you can have a look at all the stuff. Uh cool. Thank you, everyone. >> [applause]

Description
This week's federal budget included an overhaul of negative gearing and the capital gains tax rules in a bid to move thousands of homes from the hands of investors to first home buyers. 
David Speers sat down with the President of the Real Estate Buyers Agents Association Melinda Jennison and the Grattan Institute's Matthew Bowes for this week's Insiders on Background. 

00:00 Welcome
1:07 Melinda Jennison, President of the Real Estate Buyers Agents Association and Matthew Bowes, Grattan Institute
1:46 Immediate impact of tax changes on auctions
2:53 Melinda Jennison expects less competition in the 'affordable' segment of the market
3:21 Unlikely to see price retraction immediately
3:56 Matthew Bowes says modelling shows minimal impact on prices
4:41 How the types of properties people want to invest in and investor behaviour might change
6:35 Slight rise in home ownership expected due to changes
7:28 75,000 additional first home owners forecast
8:26 Trend towards first home buyers buying more afforable options to build up for deposit
9:36 Housing supply
11:06 House prices
14:38 Coalition's proposals
16:20 Impact of tax changes on rental prices
18:15 Will tax changes help young people who are locked out of the market?
21:29 Goodbye

Subscribe: https://ab.co/3yqPOZ5   Read more here:

Welcome to ABC News In-depth, where you'll find our long-form journalism and other videos to help you understand what's going on in the world around you.

Watch more ABC News content ad-free on ABC iview: https://ab.co/2OB7Mk1

For more from ABC News, click here: https://ab.co/2kxYCZY

Get breaking news and livestreams from our ABC News channel: https://www.youtube.com/user/NewsOnABC
Like ABC News on Facebook: http://facebook.com/abcnews.au
Follow ABC News on Instagram: http://instagram.com/abcnews_au
Follow ABC News on Twitter: http://twitter.com/abcnews

Note: In most cases, our captions are auto-generated.

#ABCNewsIndepth #ABCNewsAustralia
Discussion of Australian budget's housing tax reforms: negative gearing limited to new builds; capital gains discount reduced; Treasury forecasts 75k more owner-occupiers, 35k fewer homes supplied, ~2% lower price growth, minimal rent impact ($2/week). Impact varies by market due to supply-demand imbalances. Opposition's alternative includes $5bn housing infrastructure fund and migration cuts. Older, wealthier Australians benefit most from existing tax concessions; rent-vesting strategy less viable for first home buyers. Key entities: Angus Taylor, Jim Chalmers, Treasury, Grattan Institute, Real Estate Buyers Agent Association.deepseek-v4-flash

Review transcript
Well, if there's one word to define budget week this week, it's probably bold. The government finally showed some boldness on tax reform and its effort to reign in the spending on the NDIS. The opposition was arguably even bolder. Angus Taylor with his back to the wall went for broke with his promise to index tax thresholds to inflation, slash migration, and deny welfare payments to non-citizens. The competing visions set the battle lines for a furious debate that's going to run really for the rest of this parliamentary term. More immediately though, we're going to be looking at how the government's changes on negative gearing and capital gains tax in particular are likely to shift the property market. Will investors start moving away from existing properties and start turning up at auctions for newly built homes? Will first home buyers finally have a better shot? And what will all of this mean for house prices and rent? I'm keen to take a closer look at what all of this means for the property market. I'm David Spears on Naval Country at Parliament House in Canra. Welcome to Insiders on Background. Well, Melinda Jennison is the president of the Real Estate Buyers Agent Association of Australia and Matt Ba is from the Graten Institute. Welcome to you both. Thanks for joining us. >> Thanks for having me. So we know um negative gearing rules have been grandfathered for existing investments. So there's no need for a fire sale I suppose of existing investments there. But as of this week, new investments can only be negatively geared if it's a newly built property, not an established home. And of course, the capital gains tax rules are becoming less generous as well. So Melinda, let me start with you. What's this going to mean for auctions, you know, as soon as this weekend and in coming weeks? What sort of immediate impact do you expect to see here? I think that the results will be mixed to be honest. Um we have already been seeing some of the auction clearance rates pull back in recent weeks as some of the information in relation to the budget has been leaked. Uh however, I think that the overall result will be dependent on who are the buyers in specific markets around the country. So those areas which are dominated by owner occupiers are likely to see less impact than areas that are dominated by property investors. For the foreseeable future, I definitely think we will see property investors take a um side step. They will pause to reassess whether they divert or or change their um investment strategy to look at other options or alternatively they'll look at restructuring to um consider other tax effective ways to purchase. And so Melinda, just sticking with you, I if what you've painted there is what we're going to see, does that mean when you talk about owner owner occupied parts of the market perhaps not being immediately impacted, but where the investors are, what what parts of the market are they? Just give us a sense of what parts of the market we're talking about there. >> Look, I think in the affordable segment of the market, we've seen a concentration of investment activity in recent months. We've also seen first home buyers um competing for the same stock uh since the first home buyer guarantee scheme came into effect in October last year. So I think that we will see some impact or or less competition in that segment of the market. Um >> so does that mean some of those properties coming down in price do you think in that in that part of the market? >> I don't think we're going to see price retraction immediately. Um and in fact I think that general statements when we're talking about Australia's property market as a whole need to be considered with caution. Um Australia is not a single property market and I think the supply and demand metrics at a local level are very dependent on um you know the number of properties that are available for sale and the buyer depth at a specific suburb level not even at a city or regional level. So Matt, do you agree with that sort of uh take on what we can expect to see in the immediate term as a result of these changes? >> Well, I think that's entirely right that when we look at the the things that are really driving the property market over the long term, it is that balance between supply and demand. And what we're really talking about here with these tax changes is a slight shift in where that demand comes from. So, we're going to see somewhat fewer investors and maybe somewhat more homeowners, but ultimately there's a lot of homeowners out there. There's a lot of demand still there. And so, when you run that through the kind of models that the Treasury has used and the kind of estimates that we've done show a pretty minimal impact on prices. >> So, again, the follow-up to you is the same, Matt. What does that mean for different parts of the market, established homes, uh, apartments, different parts of the bigger the big cities? What What does that look like? Well, I think there's probably two impacts there. One is just as we've said, you know, this is something that advantages homeowners. They get to keep their big capital gains tax exemption, which is really the biggest tax exemption that there is now in in the property space now that we've we've retrenched on some of these other ones. And then I think there's a second impact, which is that the types of properties that people might want to invest in might change with these capital gains tax changes. And that's because rather than trying to invest purely just to chase that that really attractive capital gains tax discount, that discount's now a little bit less generous and we might see some more investors who are looking more at how they can earn a return through the rental income of properties. That's that's certainly what the government seems to have intended. If you read kind of their their budget papers, they seem to be sort of supporting this idea that there'll be more investors perhaps who are looking at new housing supply certainly, but also higher density housing that traditionally has seen less of those capital gains and more of that rental income. >> So Matt, does that mean more activity uh more buyers, particularly investors, in the newly built uh at at the lower end of the market? >> I I think it's really hard to say which part of the market that they'll come in on. And I think the big thing to to remember here is just that the big things that are impacting the property market are really those those long-term supply demand dynamics which aren't really changing a lot because of this policy. Although there are there are other policies in the budget that matter there and also what interest rates are doing. You know that that is a huge impact on how much investors are wanting to get in the market and what kinds of price appreciation we'll will be likely to see. And Matt, you mentioned that the the greatest um tax advantage now is the the owner occupied, you know, family home that's still going to be uh capital gains taxfree. Does that mean we will see more um demand or or uh interest in those established family homes, the bigger bigger homes in in the suburbs? >> I I don't know that this changes that dynamic so much, but I think that that dynamic is still there, right? I think this for a long time in in Australian housing policy, the homeowner has kind of been the goal, the the person we want to support. Certainly, that's what a lot of politicians will will say. I think that forgets a little bit that there are a lot of other people out there who say are renting and who probably are not really impacted that much either way by these kind of tax changes. But when it comes to homeowners, certainly that's that's the goal of this policy. And I think we will likely see slight slight rise in home ownership as a result. >> Okay. Well, Melinda, we know Treasury in the budget have said that they're expecting about 75,000 more owner occupiers to enter the market over the decade. Very difficult to, I guess, model or forecast what's going to happen over a decade in the housing market, but do you think that number is is about right, a 75,000 increase in people getting into the market? >> It's really hard to predict how it's going to change um owner occupier behavior. owner occupiers are always looking in specific segments of the market based on affordability. And I think one of the bigger drivers of where people are able to buy is based on, you know, how affordable a specific market is. And I guess some of the um changes to negative gearing also do impact first home buyers that cannot afford to purchase in the location that they live because ultimately some of those buyers in the past have leaned on rent vesting as a strategy as a way to build equity um temporarily. Um they sell out in the future, use that equity to purchase their own home in the location that they work near family. >> Does that become more popular? Yeah, because we hear that the stories of more and more younger people doing that, going with negatively geared property to help build up for their own >> uh home deposit. Are we talking about a big number that are doing that and has it has it been increasing? >> In recent years, we've definitely seen a trend towards first home buyers starting with a more affordable option, especially those that live in locations that they simply cannot afford to purchase their first home within. So, it's very hard obviously for first home buyers to build a deposit through savings alone. And so they do look at other investment vehicles to fasttrack that wealth that wealth creation ultimately not not for any other purpose than than trying to purchase their own home in the future. So that's an unfortunate consequence of some of the tax changes that um have been proposed because we're no longer going to see that as a viable option for rent vesters. Matt, we know when it comes to supply, the Treasury um and you know, perhaps surprising for some to see this in the budget, the Treasury says these tax changes in and of themselves will lower supply by about 35,000 homes, but other measures in the budget, uh $2 billion for housing infrastructure and so on will more than make up for that with with more supply. But in and of themselves, the negative gearing and capital gains tax changes are going to, you know, slightly reduce supply. Do you agree with that uh forecast from Treasury? >> Well, I think it's just being honest, frankly, about what the impact of lower prices is in the housing market. You know, when developers are looking at whether a project stacks up or whether a green field development stacks up, they've got to look at all of their costs, obviously, or the cost of the land, the cost of the construction, materials, and labor. But they're also looking at the price they can sell the the ultimate the home for at the end of it. And when home prices go down, that does mean that it it makes some of those projects a little bit harder to stack up. And so those kinds of estimates that Treasury's put out about say a 35,000 I think reduction in housing supply over a decade, that's relatively consistent with what we've previously estimated for the kind of tax changes that we recommended. And so that that doesn't strike me as as too surprising. I guess as you say, the big question is these other policies in the budget, including that local infrastructure fund, can they really get at the major barrier to to housing supply in our cities, which are these these planning barriers, these restrictions that states put particularly on apartments and town houses in established suburbs that have made a lot of those suburbs far too expensive for particularly first home buyers. >> I want to ask you both about the impact on house prices. You've both kind of touched on this. Again, in the budget, it says we won't necessarily see house prices fall, but the growth will be about 2% lower as a result of this. Um, Molina, you touched on the fact that it's not one homogeneous market, the housing market. What impact are you expecting then on prices of houses of units generally? >> I think the impact as I mentioned previously will vary depending on which location um individuals are located within. So even in the last 12 month months we've seen such um different performance across capital city markets. Some have moved in excess of 20% while others have shifted um less than 5% at the same time in the same conditions. So supply is one of the big factors that influences property markets and there are some capital cities that are um facing a structural under supply with 30 to 40% um listing volumes rather listing volumes 30 to 40% below the long-term average and other markets um we're seeing listing u listing volumes in in line with long-term supply. So obviously when you've got a structural under supply in markets like Perth, Brisbane, Adelaide, Darwin, you're going to see um that you in a standard um market where demand remains constant. You're going to see prices increase. Now of course these changes will impact on the demand side, but um as an example, I'm based in Brisbane and we have seen in the past for some properties um more than 10 or 15 offers on a single asset. Now, um, in the last few weeks in the leadup to the budget, we have seen the number of offers decrease as well as the buyer volume. However, there's still more than one buyer for every property. So, while we still have this structural imbalance between the number of properties available for sale and the number of buyers that are in the market, it's likely we're going to still see price pressure in those markets that are under supplied. >> Yeah. And and that goes to the the factor of supply and demand as you say, Matt, when it comes to what these changes on negative earing in capital gains tax will do to to house prices. I mean, what do you think particularly looking at the big cities and the difference between inner city, middle ring, and outer suburbs? I I mean I think it goes back to this point, right, that when we've got a real imbalance between supply and demand and and that's something we've particularly seen, I think within some of the the pricier suburbs in our major cities, but it's also something that that's impacting, you know, growth areas of our cities in in some places, particularly fast growing cities like Perth and and Brisbane that have seen a lot of growth in recent years. when you've got that imbalance, when you're pulling one investor out maybe of of that sort of auction process, um you've still got a lot of people who are sitting in there behind that investor who can bid, you know, just a little bit below what they would have bid and who can then hold up that price. And I think that's why the the price impacts that Treasury is modeling, the price impacts that we've modeled are pretty small because we're really talking about a compositional shift rather than a fundamental change to the the overall size of demand in in the Australian housing market. We know uh Angus Taylor, the opposition leaders promised a $5 billion fund for housing infrastructure. It's something that the coalition took to the last election as well. The government did have a $2 billion fund along similar lines in the budget. It says it's already put uh I think more than four billion into this area. Look clearly Angus Taylor saying he, you know, wants to do more on this infrastructure side of thing, the power, water, sewage, road connections and so on that can get projects moving faster. He wants to link migration to housing completions as well to try and bring down migration numbers, the biggest cut, one of the biggest cuts in the nation's history, he says. Um, look, is it is it possible to say, Melinda, whether that is going to do a better job of dealing with um supply in the market to get that supply of housing up? I think it's a it's a good start because we definitely need to um ensure that land that is available um has the services uh completed to ensure that developers can actually complete the subdivisions and then start construction. The bigger issue that we face right now is that construction costs have escalated so rapidly off the back of COVID. Um we also see um governments of all levels um embed taxes and statutory charges within uh brand new properties. So of course that keeps prices elevated. Um and now we've got a lot of uh the investor incentives aligned to those uh new properties as well. Let's not forget that first home buyers are also incentivized to purchase those brand new properties as well through various um grants and government incentives. So, we've all of a sudden increased demand in the more affordable uh segment of the market in new dwellings as opposed to established dwellings. And you made the point um earlier that there's going to be a change in the composition of buyers in some of the inner city locations. That is a very real risk that uh we face. And if we do see a higher concentration of owner occupiers in inner and middle ring locations where the established homes are largely located, that is what ultimately may result in upward pressure on rents as the rental supply actually diminishes over time. >> Yeah, that's interesting. Matt, do you agree with that? I think Treasury says there'll be a minimal impact on rents. Two bucks a week, I think they were suggesting on average. in the inner city and middle ring suburbs. Could it be more? >> I I don't know that we're going to see again a particularly large impact on rents when the impact on this policy of of supply is is pretty limited. You know, we're talking about a housing market where at least the the intention is to build more than 200,000 homes per year. And we might get close to that depending upon what happens with construction prices and interest rates over the coming couple of years. So, you know, 10 10 or so thousand homes over a couple of years is not necessarily going to really swing that impact, swing that balance, I think, too much. Either way, I think that the bigger point to be made here really is around when it comes to the coalition policy of of funding, you know, those kind of enabling infrastructure projects. The the real question is are they kind of additional? Can we target them to places that actually need them rather than just, you know, disrupting activity that would already have happened anyway and been funded by states. And I think the bigger question as well is are they going to adopt the same policy the government has of saying that to participate in these kinds of funds, states actually need to engage in the kinds of reforms that we really need to see to enable more land to be built on for housing. And that's something that happens in our inner cities, but it's also something that matters in our growth areas where there are those infrastructure coordination challenges and money helps with solving some of those challenges. But it's by far not the only problem. So we need to we need to look at that the problem more holistically. >> It's an important point and we haven't really gone to and don't have time to go to what the states can and should be doing when it comes to things like stamp duties, land release and uh approvals uh for these projects that are really going to fix the the real estate issues in Australia. But let me ask you both finally looking coming back to what the government's put on the table this week uh because these changes are very real. They're happening now. um are they going to help the the people the government says they want to help here and that is younger Australians who feel they've been locked out of the market? Melinda, will what they've done make that difference? >> For some the answer might be yes, but for many others I believe the answer would be no for the reasons outlined earlier. Um, depending on where people live and property values in those immediate locations, some people will find themselves locked out of the market where they work and where their family exists simply because they don't have the capacity to be purchasing their first home in those areas um due to affordability issues more than anything else. um and they can no longer rely on strategies like rent vesting where they purchase a more affordable asset elsewhere to build up that equity position to later um circle that back into purchasing their own home. So I guess the answer depends on the location and the property values in that location and how affordable it is for those first home buyers to be purchasing. >> I mean I I guess they can still rent vest a newly built property, but um what's what's your point there that there's just not that much available? Look, I think that um long-term when we look at the capital growth opportunity on brand new properties versus established properties um there's definitely a trend whereby established properties whereby you're actually paying more for the land up front and less for the depreciating part of the asset which is the building. Um that all flips and reverses with brand new properties. So quite often we find that um if people need to sell quickly after purchasing brand new um they can be in a negative equity position because the asset or the building component depreciates at a faster rate than the land around it appreciates. So there are risks associated with purchasing brand new that investors especially must be factoring in before they pivot entirely on a tax change or a policy change relating to tax. when it comes to property investing, fundamentals matter more than the tax position. And I think that that's an important um differentiation that people must realize before they pivot and immediately, you know, target or or chase the tax benefits. >> No, I think that's a really interesting point. And Matt, final word to you then. Uh it's the same question. Will this budget deliver what's on the tin for those uh who are being told you've been locked out of the market for too long now you'll get a crack? What do you think? Well, well, certainly when we look at, you know, who are the types of Australians who've benefited from these tax concessions over a number of years now, they are disproportionately older and wealthier Australians. And so I think that does just in a a tax equity sense create opportunity for younger people that that wasn't there previously. And you know I note what Melinda was saying around rent vesting but ultimately the biggest people biggest group of people who are negatively gearing when it comes to investment properties are those older and wealthier Australians rather than than younger Australians. So on net I think that that does have a real impact on on their housing potential. I guess it comes back to can the government also deliver on supply and on those policies that help out with renters who really at at the moment probably haven't seen as much in the way of of government support um but who are probably doing it the toughest on this really tricky housing market at the moment. >> Well, it is going to be fascinating to see how all of this plays out and you've both helped me better understand the complexity of the housing market and all the moving pieces and how they come together. Melinda Jennison and Matt Ba, thanks so much for joining us. >> Thanks David. >> Thank you. We'll have more on this on Sunday morning. The treasurer Jim Charas will be my guest on insiders at the end of a big budget week. Uh so hope you can join us for that. And PK will be back in the podcast feed on Monday. Bye for now. >> You're making us all feel very excited about being here.

Description
The Australian property landscape just changed forever. 

With the Federal Budget 2026 essentially ending Negative Gearing for established homes purchased after the 12th of May 2026, we are entering a brand-new era of investing.

In this video, I’m breaking down the massive tax shakeup by Treasurer Jim Chalmers, who the winners and losers are, and exactly how I’m pivoting my own strategy for the future.

What we cover in this video:

✅ Negative Gearing: Why it’s "GONE" for established houses and what "quarantining losses" actually means for your cash flow.

✅ CGT Overhaul: The end of the 50% discount and the return of indexation—plus the new 30% minimum tax rate.

✅ The "New Build" Loophole: How you can still access the old tax perks by adding to supply.

✅ Winners & Losers: From First Home Buyers to seasoned investors—who comes out on top?

✅ My Predictions: What this means for house prices, rents, and where the "smart money" is moving next.

The game has changed, but the goal remains the same: building a portfolio that gives you choices.

 —
 
 Thinking about buying an investment property in Australia in the next 3 to 12 months? 
 
 Then book your free strategy session here: https://www.pumpedonproperty.com/free-strategy-session 
 
 What can you expect from your free strategy session?

 In your strategy session, we will discuss:
 
 1. Where you are right now
 2. Where you want to be long-term
 3. What’s been holding you back from achieving your property investment goals until now
 4. Your next action steps 
 
 You’ll walk away from the call with a clear plan in place and the confidence you need to invest in your next property.
 
 —
 
 Want to get the latest insights on the Australian property market before anyone else?
 Then sign up now and join over 26,000+ investors who’ve subscribed to our monthly newsletter here…
 https://www.pumpedonproperty.com/
 
 Looking for the hottest suburbs in Australia?
 Then download a free copy of Australia’s 60 Hottest Suburbs Guide here…
 https://www.pumpedonproperty.com/australias-60-hottest-suburbs 
 
 —

#NegativeGearing #FederalBudget2026 #AustralianProperty #PropertyInvesting #JimChalmers #CapitalGainsTax #HousingMarketAustralia #FinancialFreedom #PumpedOnProperty #RealEstateTax
 
 —
 
 At Pumped On Property, we help investors build their property portfolios with confidence & achieve financial freedom.
 
 As a business, we’ve helped our clients buy over $650,000,000 worth of investment property in Australia.

 We believe you were born to be financially free and living a life filled with choices.
 
 The choice to spend more time with the people you care about, do meaningful work, help others, move better, travel the world, and become the best version of yourself. 
 
 We look forward to helping you get there. 
 
 —

 The viewer acknowledges and agrees that: 
 (a) Pumped on Property (POP) is not a licensed financial services adviser, accountant, solicitor, builder, engineer, architect, town planner or property manager; 
 (b) POP is a licensed real estate agent who conducts business as a 'buyer's agent. 
 (c) POP conveys the information provided on this video channel as general information only and is not tailored to the viewer's particular financial circumstances or expectations; 
 (d) The information provided on this video channel cannot be relied upon by the viewer as providing any advice upon which the viewer might rely in making any decision concerning their financial circumstances or the sale or purchase of any real property; 
 (e) The use to which the viewer may make of the information provided on this video channel is subject to the viewer seeking independent professional advice from legal, financial, taxation and accounting advisers before making any decision affecting their financial circumstances or the sale or purchase of any real property; 
 (f) The information provided in this video channel, given that it is general in nature, is not suitable or applicable to the viewer's individual circumstances, needs, objectives or expectations; 
 (g) In providing the information on this video channel, POP has made no representation, provided no advice, and given no warranty or promise as to the suitability, or otherwise, of any investment in any real property;
 (h) POP is unable to predict the short or long-term future of the global Australian financial market or the property markets and acknowledges that prices may rise, fall, or be stagnant for long periods of time, and that POP has no control over the market or any returns to any investor in the market;
 (i) POP has made no representation, promise or warranty as to the competence of any third-party service providers referred to on this video channel.
 I acknowledge that I have read and understood the disclaimer with respect to POP's services set out above before accessing this video channel.
Australian federal budget 2026-2027 proposes negative gearing restricted to new dwellings from 12 May 2026; CGT discount replaced with inflation indexation and 30% floor from 1 July 2027; $2B housing infrastructure package; faster planning measures. Speaker argues reforms are band-aids, not addressing 500k annual immigration or local red tape. Remains bullish on well-located residential real estate citing 8.2% annual growth over 50 years. Predicts rents will rise 7-12% as in NZ. Encourages buying in uncertainty for long-term gains. Mentions HTW, CoreLogic, CBA, Macquarie Bank, RBA.deepseek-v4-flash
