Review transcript
The Australian government has just thrown an absolute haymaker to the chin of every single property investor in the country from the 1st of July 2026 moving forward. Now, while the media might have you believing that these changes are all doom and gloom and scary for us as investors moving forward, today I wanted to take a step back and look objectively at the pros and cons of the proposed changes and the actual changes that have just come out in the Australian federal government's 2026-2027 budget. So, ultimately ends up with two questions in mind. One, do I still believe in well-located Australian residential real estate? And then the second question I've got for myself and many of you guys would be feeling the same way at the moment, is then what do I do right now? >> [music] [music] >> Now, in preparation for this video, I sat down and had a really good look at the work from HTW as well as CoreLogic, CBA, and Macquarie Bank. Now, while there's so much noise around this conversation in the media at the moment, I think it's really important and I hope that by the end of this video, you've got clarity on what is actually being proposed, what still has to be legislated, and what it means for you and me as residential property investors in Australia in 2026 and beyond. Now, I wouldn't normally take the approach of physically sitting here and reading some of the stuff from Australia's leading analysts and banks, but I think it's just so important today to get to the truth of what's really going on and figure out what some of Australia's leading thought leaders and analysts are saying about this potential issue that will affect all of us moving forward. Now, according to Herron Todd White, the Australian economic backdrop in terms of what's going on in the economy right now, at the time of this budget, needs to be factored into account when we're having this conversation today. Now, the current economic environment in Australia is characterized by inflationary pressures, obviously those being higher than normal, constrained housing supply. We just haven't built enough houses in the right locations in Australia for most of the last 12 years. A subdued and uneven economic environment. So, one week petrol prices are three bucks, the next week they're a dollar 50. One week America is invading, the next week we don't really know what's going on. There's just so much uncertainty in the markets right now, which is extremely common at this stage of the 18 .6 year real estate cycle, as well as ongoing global uncertainty. Wars, trade, all of this stuff going on around the world right now, which is effectively creating a pause for most investors right now, which is where our opportunity lies. Now, according to CBA, the inflation remains elevated, which is driven by inflation in services, housing costs, and continued input price pressures like petrol, like energy, like food across the broader economy. Now, what this has resulted in is the Reserve Bank of Australia having to take more restrictive or reactive measures to the Australian economy, and that's why we're seeing interest rates going up at the moment. Now, according to Ray Dalio in his book Big Debt Crises, which looks at some of the worst economic environments that we've gone through in the last 100 years globally, one of the levers that governments around the world, when inflation's out of control or house price growth is out of control, is they can push the lever on interest rates, which effectively increases them higher and takes away the opportunity for us to spend on a day-to-day basis in our household. Now, by doing this, it constrains spending, it contributes to weaker conditions economically both globally and domestically here in Australia, and it creates a feeling, at least inside me, of being just [ __ ] pissed off because I know the reason that we've hit this position is because the Australian government and global governments printed way too much money in COVID thinking it was going to be the end of the world and all of this is just a knock-on effect from poor policy five or six years ago. Now on top of this in the broader Australian economy, household consumption remains under pressure. So there's higher mortgage repayments, elevated living costs, and although wages have gone up heaps when you factor in the inflation, they actually haven't gone up that much at all. They're just keeping par or actually below the cost of living in Australia right now. Now I think what the Reserve Bank of Australia and the Australian government would love to do is just put interest rates up to like 9% tomorrow and effectively completely cool off and create a mini recession in the Australian market. But that would cause hundreds of thousands of Australians to lose their home and that would be political suicide. So now they're taking this, well let's change some stuff in the budget, let's change some legislation, let's take some more tax from investors around the country, and let's push interest rates up. On top of this, housing supply remains structurally constrained. Now there's a whole bunch of reasons that we could go into around this, but the cost of developing in Australia according to Michael Matusik is somewhere between 30 to 60% higher than it needs to be because of the take that the local governments, state governments, and national government are taking from developers and the building industry to bring on this new supply in Australia. On top of this, there is endless red tape around planning in Australia and we just simply have been letting in too many people and not building enough dwellings for most of the last decade. So again, while the government is saying that this is a proactive change, this is just completely [ __ ] reactive to 10 years of doing the wrong stuff from a policy perspective to keep Australians feeling good and whichever political party is in in power so that they're not the ones popping the bubble or rocking the boat on this stuff. Now on top of this, the labor market remains extremely tight with unemployment sitting this month at 4.3% with monster amounts of skill shortages in key sectors including construction, health, and the essential services limiting our ability to get the right people and the right skilled people into the right jobs, hence bringing in 500,000 new people that are skilled from overseas per year. And then if we take a long view and look at the global economy, Bitcoin silver gold Australian stocks, global stocks, global properties, whether that's commercial, residential, retail have been all over the shop in the last couple of years, as well as the war between Russia and the Ukraine, China and Taiwan, as well as what's going on between America, Israel, and over there in the Middle East. It's just creating an environment which we should have at this stage of the cycle where people are like, "What is actually going on? Are things going to be good tomorrow? Are they going to be bad tomorrow? Should I take a punt and go into the markets now, or should I just wait and see what happens?" And so it's creating fear, uncertainty, and more than anything else, a bit of a reprieve in the market where people are like, "Well, I don't know. I'm just going to sit out for a while." And again, and I'll say this many times this video, once every sort of 7 to 10 years we get a market like this, and if you're an investor with a 5 to sort of 15 or 20-year journey ahead of you, these are the most incredible markets to get bargains that you'll see in a decade. Now, if we get into the nuts and bolts according to HTW and CBA about the federal budget for 2026-2027, and the tax reform. So effectively, there's four things. There's the negative gearing reform, there's the capital gains tax changes, there's a $2 billion housing infrastructure package, which is putting more money on the fire, and then there's the faster planning and housing delivery measures, which effectively is just fast-tracking approval so that they can get more stuff to market sooner. Now, when we look at each of these four things and how they affect me and you as residential property investors in Australia both today with what we've already got and what we plan to do in the future. The first one I wanted to look at is the negative gearing reform. Now, in this 2016-2017 budget, they're introducing significant reforms. But remember right now they're just ideas. They have not been legislated and they still have to pass through Parliament. Now, under the new framework, negative gearing deductions for residential property will primarily be restricted or focused on construction of new dwellings. So, effectively what they're saying is that we want to encourage more supply. So, we're going to take away the negative gearing benefits not for people that currently own property in the country, but effectively people that buy property after budget night, which is on the 12th of May this year. And what they're saying is we'll give you all of the benefits and even more if you're prepared to go out there and build something for us to create more supply as an investor. But if you're going to go out there and buy the established stock in Australia, then you're not going to get the current negative gearing benefits in the first year that you own the property that you would today. Now, the policy is designed to redirect investment demand towards new housing supply by reducing the tax incentives associated with the existing supply. So, effectively everything that's currently built in Australia right now is under one thing and everything that will be built in Australia in the future according to this reform is under a new position. Now, in terms of the policy rationale or the reason that they're doing this, they're effectively reducing the attractiveness of investing in established property in Australia and they're trying to redirect people, like I said a moment ago, towards new supply. Now, what they're hoping that over time is that more investors put their money into new stuff and it creates more supply across the entire country in the right markets at the right time. Now, the economic effect of this for investors like myself and yourself going forward could be lower after-tax returns for newly acquired established investment properties. So, if you buy something that's old, you're not going to get the negative gearing benefit in the first year, but I'll talk about what you will get in a moment. The other economic effect is that there's going to be likely changes to investment holding strategies, portfolio portfolio or property allocation, and the transaction activity over time. So, it might result in some people not selling their established dwellings, and it might result in some investors, especially those working with financial advisers and accountants out there that are trying to save some tax bucks, are going to obviously reorientate those people towards new supply. Now, I've got no problem at all with high-quality greenfield sites close to Australia's metro markets or close to Australia's largest regional markets near the city or near near the beach. But, what you don't want is to be buying the absolute rubbish property in the absolute outskirts of Australia's largest cities or larger small towns and getting these tiny 100 to 300 square meter blocks with the property that's now going to be massively overpriced because all of these builders are going to be saturated with new work. Now, when we look at the second proposed change, it's around capital gains tax. Now, they are introducing significant reforms around the CGT tax, and what that actually looks like is in the past what we've had, the 50% capital gains tax will be removed from assets acquired after the 1st of July 2027 and replaced with what they're calling is an inflation index taxation model on capital gains. They're also saying a minimum 30% capital gains tax floor will be applied to relevant capital gains tax outcomes, reducing the extent to which gains can be taxed below this threshold. So, effectively what they're trying to do with this, guys, and they can sugarcoat it any way they want, is bring in more tax off Australian investors because we've all made money over the last 10 in in Australia, and create a more bloated, more confusing Australian taxation system for property investors with more red tape than ever. This is going to be absolutely ridiculous and there's going to be massive amounts of contention between the Australian government and accountants across the country as these things come into play. As with the negative gearing benefits for residential property investors, the negative gearing concessions will be restricted primarily to new constructed housing. Existing investment properties will be grandfathered under transitional agreements, while newly acquired established dwellings will lose access to negative gearing benefits from effectively the budget date moving forward. Newly built investment properties will retain more favorable tax treatment and from a policy perspective, the reforms are designed again to redirect investment towards new housing construction, reduce the demand for established housing and approve house and improve housing supply over time. So, what's interesting about this one is it's not actually going to affect every Australian in the same way and it's not going to affect all of us in the same way on the same year. So, when it comes to these capital gains tax changes, um there's examples from CBA bank and some of the other major banks in the country where they're modeling this. Um and I've also been talking to my accountant about this this morning. In some instances, I'll actually pay less capital gains tax on a property that I sell if inflation during that time is higher, but inflation is lower at that time and the gains on the property were extremely high, then I'd I'd pay more capital gains tax than I would under the current position which we're sitting on prior to the 12th of May 2026. Now, when we look at the economic impacts according to HTW around the capital gains tax changes, there's going to be a likely moderation in investment demand for established residential property. On top of this, there's going to be increased attractiveness of newly constructed developed dwellings and development projects. There's going to be a potential reallocation of capital towards housing construction and supply creation. And the medium-term outcomes really depends on how much construction there is, what financing there are what finance opportunities there are for new construction across the country, and how the government actually rolls this out. But effectively, at the end of the day, it's about bringing in more revenue for the Australian government from the gains that we've all made as investors over time. Now, the third thing in the federal budget is the $2 billion housing infrastructure package. Now, it's aimed at addressing the supply side constraints in the residential property market and accelerating new housing delivery. So, from a policy perspective from the Australian government, the investment of this $2 billion is intended to remove infrastructure bottlenecks that delay residential construction. Good luck with that. Improved development feasibility and support faster housing delivery. Now, I was thinking about this the other day, like what I would be doing if I was trying to struggle with this massive challenge that the Australian government has. And you can just imagine how ridiculously hard and unpopular this is right now. They don't want to pop the bubble. They can't put interest rates up to 9% and completely cool off the economy. They can't create a crash in the Australian property market where prices, like they have in Ireland, Greece, Japan, the US, or the UK in the past, just drop overnight by 15 to 30% to re-level everything. They can't force employers, like you guys and myself, to increase wages by 100%. They have no impact on energy prices or fuel prices globally, and as a result, effectively, food prices within the country at the moment because of all those in point input costs. And so dealing with all of those constraints, I thought what if we just had a national planning policy, which instead of having local government, state government, and the national government all getting their little piece of the pie and putting their little their little input in, we just had a really really really simple national approach to housing which looked like this. And so when I look at a 600 square meter block, if it's within a kilometer of a train station or a major metro area anywhere in the country, I just know that I can do a duplex or a triplex or a granny flat on it straight away. And I don't have to work with 3,000 local councils and all of this red tape across the economy. But instead of doing that, they're effectively just chucking another $2 billion into it, which is ultimately going to create more jobs, more red tape, and more stagnation as there's more people not doing not knowing what they're actually doing, have more paperwork sitting on their desk. Or what's going to happen is what's always going to happen is the smart builders and developers like the AAVs, the Stocklands, the Mirvac groups are going to go out there, they're going to bring in all of this new supply, they're going to go get all of this money from the Australian government to fast track these developments, and they're going to get all of that effectively infrastructure paid for by the government, and then they're going to drip feed out 30 lots at a time once it's actually ready for the market so that they can continue to keep the prices elevated to make the maximum profit possible. So this again is a band-aid. It's just putting more money into the Australian property market, and ultimately they can't go and sit with every single town planner in every single local council in the country and say change everything that you currently do, start rubber stamping stuff. It's just not going to happen. Now the package around this $2 billion bucks, and sorry for the rant today, but like I'm I'm so fed up with it. And I'm assuming you feel the same way. Like just let us invest. Let us do our thing. And I personally like this sort of market. I know that's bad to say. I love the uncertainty. I love the fear. I loved buying at the end of the last GFC. I loved buying between 2015 and 17 when they made it almost impossible to borrow money because Sydney and Melbourne prices went up too much and they wanted to slow things down. I loved buying in COVID once we realized that health-wise people were going to be okay and everyone was sitting on the fence. I will absolutely enjoy the [ __ ] out of buying in the next couple of years as people sit and wait and see like they always do. But it's just so frustrating like the algorithm that we get now is so scary, so dark. And what people aren't sitting back and going and recognizing is that they want Australia to be 60 million people within the next 50 years. And they want to bring in between 500 and 600,000 new people to the country per year for two reasons: more taxes and more taxes. That's it. They want more people here so they can collect more revenue so that they can build more stuff and they can put more jobs and bums on seats. And that is how it all works. Nobody wants to pop this bubble and well-located housing close to metro or major regional markets in the city, in the beach, in the most undervalued markets and suburbs around the country 15 years from now based on all of the history that I can see is most likely to double if it just does 5% per annum. But if we look at the last 18 years from 2007 through to 2025 every single market on the East Coast of Australia, whether it was Sydney, Melbourne, Brisbane or the big regional markets around them, did over 215% growth, or over 10% per annum. So, when I'm saying it's going to double in 15 years time, it's not me just guessing or pulling a number out of the air. It is based on hundreds of years of history in Australia, the UK, and America, and knowing what people ultimately want. And that is the Australian government wants to keep making more revenue. They want to continue to keep prices elevated. They want to continue to have supply issues. They want to continue to bring in more people overseas. The conversation is not, "We're going to stop bringing 500,000 people a year, and for the next 5 years, we're going to bring in no one." The conversation is, "We're going to build more dwellings, and maybe we'll create half as much as we need, and we're going to tax investors to [ __ ] do it, instead of actually going, 'Interest rates need to go up. We need to stop spending money, and we need to ultimately stop letting people in for a period of time so the entire economy can rebalance itself, but no one wants to do it cuz that's political suicide, and you're going to get kicked out.'" Woof, what a rant. Um the rationale, I love how there's some rationale about spending more of our money. Removes infrastructure bottlenecks constraining housing supply delivery. Accelerates development pipelines. Supports coordination across federal, state, and local governments. How? How How are they talking to each other? They work in different offices from different places in the country. Focuses on supply-side housing reform rather than just demand. Um so, we'll let 500,000 people in a year, and we'll take 40 to 60% of the cost of development straight into the government's pocket, and then we'll put $2 billion into rubber-stamping a couple of developments with the biggest developers across the country. Like, come on, guys. The economic effect is it improves the feasibility of residential development projects for the big boys. Supports the gradual increase in housing supply over time, provides uplifting construction and infrastructure activity, potential medium-term easing of supply-driven housing price pressures depending on delivery outcomes. So, yeah, interesting one for me at the moment to see how that's going to have any meaningful impact on the Australian economy over time. It might create more demand for brand new property which we need, but if you stop letting so many people into the country tomorrow for a period of 2 years, this would sort itself out almost straight away anyway. The last one is faster planning and housing delivery measures. This one just makes me laugh. The reform focuses on improving coordination across levels of government, reducing friction in approval pathways where Commonwealth levers apply. All of the red tape for anyone like myself that's done subdivisions, townhouses, granny flats, duplexes, knockdown rebuilds, renovations sits at the local government level. I've never had a development approval. I'm not sure about you guys at the Commonwealth level. They just take a clip of what I pay them in the stamp duty when I buy the property or the development related services fees, and I don't hear anything from them except give me some tax on the money you make when you sell it. Supporting more efficient development processes through funding, data, and system improvements rather than direct control over state planning schemes. The package includes initiatives to reduce reduce delays in project progression, improving the planning system's capacity, and support digital and administrative upgrades in areas experiencing approval bottlenecks. I just don't know how you go out there to every single state and local government in Australia which is in control of development and supply in the country, and basically boot every single person out there that's not allowing things to get approved, and replace them with someone that maybe like Elon Musk chucks in there, like he tried to do in America to make things more efficient again. It just cannot fundamentally happen. Now, the rationale here, the policy rationale, is it aims to reduce planning friction, supports supports improved coordination, improves efficiency. Um the economic effects is it may contribute to reduce delays in residential development applications. How? Um supports smoother pipeline flow. It It should help things at the bigger end of town for sure. And I suppose if they look at it two ways, right? You can get individual little developers like me and my family doing a one into two duplex project in Brizzy, or you could just go to somewhere like Stockland's and go, you're sitting on hundreds of hectares of land around the country. Here's a billion dollars. Just bring on 10,000 lots in the next 2 years, and here's the money to cover this shortfall from not sitting on it for the next 10 years. Like that absolutely makes sense to me. But again, do you want your investments to be 50 to 60 K's away from away from the CBD, or do you want to be walking distance to the beach with an established home? I don't think for me the fact that I've got a pay a little bit more capital gains tax potentially in the future, depending on how much inflation there was at the day, or the fact that I'm not getting a small amount of negative gearing benefit on my own portfolio is going to stop me from wanting to buy high-quality blue-chip inner-city beachside property in the most undervalued markets in the country, versus going out there and buying tiny lots with crappy builds miles away from anything meaningful in Australia's fringes of the capital cities around Oz. Now, one thing I didn't say, sorry, which CBA pointed out in these proposals, is that we can't get the negative gearing benefit in year one in the future for established properties, obviously we can still get it for brand new stuff. And there is I love brand new stuff. Like I built so many homes, renovated so many homes, done the duplexes and subdivisions for myself and literally over 150 clients. Like I love the strategy in the right area, but that negative loss that you would normally get paid at the end of the tax year will now be offset against the future gain of the property. So, it's not like we're missing the benefit completely. It's just that it's being delayed in the future like it would be in a trust structure currently. Now, this is really important, I think, and this is really cool from HTW and CBA. What does it actually mean for first home buyers and investors? Now, for first home buyers, it increases housing supply pipeline. So, effectively, infrastructure planning and planning reforms aimed at unlocking new housing supply. It increases the focus on growth corridors and regional development. Fringe parts of the city will get big boost from this. Um and it increases the acceleration of delivery, but effectively the word on the street's going to be if you buy something nothing if you build something new nothing will change. And so, people are going to move more into that space, which is cool. Reduced investment investor competition for established housing. As an investor that's bought over 750 million dollars worth of property in the last 16 years for myself and my clients, it doesn't change a single thing that I'm going to do. I won't just go back to adding some granny flats to some of my properties to increase the cash flow. It's not going to stop me from going towards existing high-quality housing in Sydney, Melbourne, Brisbane, Sunny Coast, Goldie, Newcastle, Wollongong. I want to make gains. I'm not in investing to save a tiny amount of tax. You've got to remember with negative gearing you've paid a dollar in tax and you're getting a few cents back. I'm in investing in Australian residential property to make as much money as I possibly can over the next 15 years so that I've got financial freedom and choices in my future, not to save tax. Limited and may [ __ ] affordable relief. So, there's no major new first home buyer incentives announced. Affordability remains tied to interest rates and borrowing capacity. So, they're increasing rates. They're taking away some of these tax benefits which makes investing in property for people like me and you less attractive. And they're not increasing any of the things that they give to first-timers to make it easier for them to get in. So, the policy impacts are expected to be gradual rather than immediate. I expect them to have absolutely no meaningful impact longer medium to longer term on the Australian housing market at all. We'll bring on more stuff. We might bring on too much stuff in some markets around the country. But ultimately, when that happens, the government will just bring in more people from overseas and create a supply issue again. Now, what it means for Australian investors, and this is more important for me. Um the taxation setting shift is it reduced relative tax advantages associated with investing in existing residential property through the negative gearing benefit that we currently received. Greater reliance on newly constructed housing to access more favorable tax treatment. So, some people go and build new stuff instead of buying old stuff is what they're hoping. Changes in the investment incentives, they're improving the attractiveness of new builds and developing. They're reducing the appeal of older stock. And they're saying there might be a portfolio or capital allocation impact. Gradual shift in investor capital towards new supply. Potential rebalancing of investment property portfolios away from established properties. Might mean that some people hold their properties for longer now, which um from my thought process is going to create even more supply issues in the Australian residential property market because not as many properties are coming up for sale, but there's still all of these people moving into the country each year that want to buy something to live in. So, ultimately, there were some major changes announced on Tuesday, the 12th of May, that will impact you and I as investors for the foreseeable future. When they implemented the same changes in 2021 in New Zealand, rents went up 7 to 12% in the year after. The property market went down by about 1% for a period of time. Um it has had some massive impacts in New Zealand, but that was mainly around how they deliberately tried to slow the economy and pull the property market back. They were gutsy. You know, in Ireland, in Japan, in Greece, when their property markets crashed, it was generally due to like global factors like really, really, really bad markets or massive amounts of either oversupply or poor economic conditions in those countries or poor policy. In New Zealand, they were so gutsy, they literally crashed their own property market. They put in place a whole bunch of the most aggressive things I've ever seen. They wouldn't let people from overseas buy. They made it very, very difficult to get money. They wanted you to put down almost unreasonable amounts of deposits in cash, 20, 30, 40% for some properties. And effectively, what it resulted in was a massive crash on New Zealand property prices, but that was to do with so much more going on than what the Australian government's trying to do here. And what they're not talking about is how for the 10 years leading into that, they let the entire world speculate on New Zealand prices and they went up 200 to 300% in 10 years, literally because they had no entry fees or exit fees in the country, and so the whole world is speculating on New Zealand. Now, in 2021, that was put in place, some changes around some of this stuff. In 2024, when the new government came in, scrapped all of it, back to business as usual, and the New Zealand economy has re-stabilized. And so, ultimately in New Zealand, it was put in place in 2021. It was reversed in 2024. Rents went up 7 to 12% in the first year because I'm going to pass my negative gearing benefit that I would have received straight back onto my rent. And I think every single investor in Australia is going to do the exact same thing. If the government's not going to give me my 100 bucks a week from that property, then I'm going to go get it from the market because the good thing is inflation has created so many increases in wages that people can now afford to pay rents. Like the average property that we're buying in Melbourne, for example, at the moment for $750,000 has an average household income of between 100 and $250,000 per year renting it from us. And on average, they're only paying 450 to 500 bucks a week in rent. So, going into the last GFC, for example, rents across Australia were 56% of the average household incomes after-tax income, just rents alone. Right now, in many of the properties in Melbourne, for example, or Geelong, you're talking about 15 to 20% of the household income. Rents could effectively double tomorrow in that market, and people could easily not easily, but people could afford a jump. So, that's one thing that could happen. It didn't have the effect over there that they were hoping of reducing house prices, but some of the other things that they did did have an effect. Ultimately, what all of this comes down to when you boil it down is the government getting more revenue and encouraging new supply. I like the government to be in the green. I want them to be in a surplus budget. I love being an Australian. I love the schools, the universities, the shops, the healthcare, the trains, the roads. I've traveled all over the world, and this place is just so special. Like it is I love the way that the local, state, and Australian governments take that tax money and you can see it. Because going to some of the other places in the world that all of us have been to, you can really see like the corruption and all of the stuff that's going on and when that money isn't arriving in the hands of the right people, you can see what happens to those economies and the infrastructure and the lives that people have. So, we are so blessed here in Australia and I'm so thankful for the way that government does it. But, ultimately, this is just about collecting more revenue in a way that we'll tolerate. And it's about increasing supply in Australia without doing anything fundamental to address the 500,000 people a year that we're still moving in. Because if we ultimately wanted to address these two things in reality, we'd have a national approach to infrastructure development. Um and approvals across all of these things at the local level and the national level, we'd probably be increasing interest rates by 2% and just completely slowing down the Australian economy for two to three years to reset things. We'd stop letting so many people come in from overseas every single year and blowing out the supply and demand related issue. And the government would actually get involved in Australian residential housing again and turn some of those incredibly massive tracts of Australian government land that they're sitting on into developers for affordable housing. And in those particular states, instead of charge making 40 to 60% clip like the Australian government currently does, they take away all of the benefit and tax related revenue and create extremely affordable housing in extremely high quality high quality markets. And if the government really wanted to, they could turn that tap on with the biggest developers and infrastructure providers in Australia in a five-year period, completely completely change the state of the Australian economy and the way that Australia looks moving forward. But, nobody really wants to fix this. The average Labor politician owns two and a bit houses. The average Liberal politician in Australia owns three plus houses. So, it's much easier to say, "We're not the problem." You know, they don't want to say that they've brought in 4 million people in the last 10 years. They don't want to say that we make 30 to 60% of the money from developers in a in the country. And if we were to stop taking that clip right now, that development would be 50% cheaper in Australia. We don't want to acknowledge that the red tape in development is being stuck in the state government offices, the local government offices, and the national government offices with the wrong people sitting there approving things too slowly. They don't want to say that they pumped the Australian economy with so much money that they literally doubled construction prices in the country. Completely blew out food prices. Completely destroyed electricity prices as well. And they don't want to take accountability and say, "Hey, the approach we've been taking in Australia for the last 15 years might have been a bit off. We're sorry. And this is exactly what we're going to do to fix it." Instead, as always, it's the big bad other guy. Misdirection pointed over there. The greedy investors that are speculating on the Australian property market that are sort of the ones again that are going to have to pay for these changes. So, ultimately, it ends up with one question in mind, which is actually two questions in mind. One, do I still believe in well-located Australian residential real estate? The answer for me is absolutely yes. The reason why is despite all of the wars and the changes and the negative gearing and the tax stuff in Australia over the last 50 years, according to Simon Pressley from Propertyology, Australian residential property has been one of the top performing assets in the world going up by over 8.2% per annum for the last 50 years. Now, when I look at the H-tag data over the last 18 years, and I can see that property across the country in the markets that I like to buy on the East Coast of Australia has done over 10% per annum. It gives me confidence that despite all of those changes, a GFC, 9/11, COVID, the stuff we're going through now, we've still survived these markets. It gives me confidence that people are still going to want to live in Sydney near the beach, Melbourne near the beach, Brisbane, Sunny Coast, Goldie, etc. And then the second question I've got for myself and many of you guys would be feeling the same way at the moment is then what do I do right now? Because every single one of us is clear on where we want to be in 15 years from today. And if you're not, I'll tell you where you want to be. You want to be financially free, having a life filled with choices, the choice to do whatever you want with whoever you want whenever you want to do it. You want to be with your family, your friends, healthy, able to do work you love or start a business you love, traveling the world, traveling this beautiful country, contributing, donating your time, having so much money coming in that you can give it to all of the people in your life that you love or the charity or the charitable organizations around the world. And you want to have a meaningful impact and become the person that you were born to be or created to be. It is so simple. And so, how do we do that then if it's not through Australian residential real estate? Do we do Do we dabble in crypto, silver, gold, American stocks, Asian stocks, Australian stocks? Like, what's our alternative? Starting a business? We have to do something if we want to get to a life of freedom and choices. And so for me, I actually felt way more uncomfortable last week and uh particularly 3 months ago to sort of a year and a half ago when all of this FOMO was in the market. People were paying stupid prices and everything was going ballistic. I loved buying and I'll say this again at the end of the the GFC and once we got through the worst of COVID health-wise, and there was a buying opportunity, and I love buying this current market. There is an incredible opportunity right now for the small number of investors out there that can handle this type of market, and it's not for everyone. It's not for people that have a low tolerance to fear and risk. It's not for people that get distracted by the blowing of the wind, and every single thing that comes through the algorithm online or through the media at the moment rattles you. It's for those people that love buying when other people aren't. It's for those people that love getting discounts short-term because there's a change, and people don't understand the change yet. It's for people that can stomach uncertainty because they know that they've got a 5-to-15-year plan, and they don't care what they're going to make in the next 12 months. And it's for people with mental and emotional stability that just love getting a bargain like myself. So, finally, finally, finally, the market has opened up for people like me, and you'll see I'm working on a property this week. I just bought another one. Um for the first time in probably 3 to 5 years, you'll see me buying some stuff in a big way again, and I'll be sharing that journey as I go. But, I love buying the best markets in the country for a discount. And if you knew what I knew, which is the 20 largest markets in the country and what each of them have individually done per year for the last 1 to 18 years or 30 years, and you could see what's coming in some of these undervalued markets around the country in the next 7 to 10, you'd be feeling just as excited as I am, too. So, if that feels like you, and you can confront your fears, and you like buying discounts, then I'd love to offer you a one-on-one strategy session. Simply jump over to www.pumpedonproperty.com, click that free strategy session button. We can talk about where you are right now, where you'd like to be longer term. I can educate you on what's going on in the 20 largest markets around the country right now. And then if you want to do it on your own, I'll give you all of our information and you can go and absolutely smash it on your own. Or if you'd potentially like to become one of the small number of clients that we take on as a business and that I work with personally one-on-one throughout the rest of this year, then I'm extremely excited to catch up with you. But either way, I truly wish you all the best on your journey to a life filled with financial freedom and choices through property. >> [singing and music] >> It was all a dream. Baby, I'm dreaming. >> [music] >> It was all a dream.

Description
Does the Federal Budget really help young Australians buy property, or simply change the rules of wealth-building for the next generation.

🏠 This podcast is brought to you by Coposit, where $10k means you can buy a brand-new home. To learn more about how it works and browse properties, visit: www.coposit.com.au

00:00:00 - Taxation and First Home Buyers
00:00:23 - Winners and Losers
00:02:14 - Capital Gains Tax
00:02:32 - National Debt
00:05:02 -  Taxation and Spending
00:05:33 - Capital Gains Tax Changes
00:06:54 - Investment Strategies
00:10:55 -  Negative Gearing
00:15:03 - Rent Increase Predictions
00:16:27 - Impact of Airbnb
00:19:42 -  Impact on Small Businesses
00:28:08 - Investment in Company
00:31:43 - Discussion on Renovating a Business
00:33:25 - Government Trust and Policy Changes
00:35:45 - Superannuation Taxing
00:36:42 - Possible Future Changes
Transcript discusses Australian government's proposed changes to capital gains tax (CGT) discount (from 50% to inflation-indexed minimum 30%) and negative gearing, affecting all asset classes (property, shares, crypto, small business). Key claims: policy aims to help 75,000 first home buyers over 10 years (7,500/year) but speakers argue it taxes all investments, not just residential; grandfathering until July 2027; opponents say it harms younger generation building wealth while protecting existing wealth-holders. Winners: developers, land holders, new-build buyers agents, grandfathered asset holders. Losers: startups, small businesses, younger investors. Borrowing capacity potentially double with negative gearing for new builds. Government claims rent will rise $2/week; speakers dispute. Context: $1 trillion national debt, NDIS cuts, $250 tax offset for 13 million Australians. Distrust of broken Labor promises on not touching CGT/negative gearing. Includes mention of company structures (30% flat tax) as potential strategy.deepseek-v4-flash
2026-05-16T07:23:52.835701+00:00		raw dim=384
summary dim=384

And there's going to be no negative gearing benefit in individual name for existing or in company. So there's no tax advantage there. So then what is the benefit of company? Well, you can um offset your uh your losses versus future gains. And the big one is that you don't need to distribute income annually. So in individual name in trust, you need to distribute that income to somebody inside an egg company. You you you might have $20,000 worth of profit and it's taxed at 30%. And then you can go and reinvest that profit and reinvest it. So you're capturing it in a lower tax environment, which means less tax leakage, which over time compounding means you can invest more and more and more. the you still got to get it out. Yeah. This is Jason He, the sixth voice in property, here to tell you that the Property Now podcast offers opinions on Australian property news forformational and entertainment purposes only and general in nature. It does not take into account your objectives, financial situation or needs. It does not constitute professional advice. Always seek expert guidance before making any property related decisions. Transcript Summary Filter Test

Question: can you summarise the impact of negative gearing, capital gains tax on the property market, how does impact the long terms trends of property prices and what type of properties are winners and losers

Threshold: 0.25 Filter top K: 5

Cosine similarity is computed directly between the question embedding and each stored transcript summary embedding. The current retrieval filter now applies the threshold to this cosine score. Chroma score is shown separately as 1 - distance for diagnosing distance-metric mismatches.

RankVideoTitleChannelCosine similarityChroma scorePasses thresholdSelected by current filterSummary
1Uc1yniFxg0oWhat the Budget shock means for property investors, rents and house pricesRask0.54700.0939yesyesPodcast discussion on Australian budget changes to negative gearing (restricted to new builds from July 2027, grandfathering for existing investors) and capital gains tax (minimum 30% rate). Key claims: changes will revalue all properties, reduce investor demand (40% of lending), smash borrowing capacity due to removal of negative gearing benefits from servicing calculations, increase rents, and shift strategies toward owner-occupier homes, share investing, or high-rent properties. Entities: Pete Went, Chris Bates, CBA, Treasury. Risks: oversupply of investor-heavy apartments, decline in new supply, marginalization of renters, and challenges for rentvesting and buyers agents.
23hk7nO_q0a8597 | Federal Budget 2026: Are YOU a Winner or Loser? (Property Edition)The Property Couch Podcast0.4834-0.0332yesyesThe podcast discusses the 2026 Australian federal budget's property changes, especially negative gearing (limited to new builds from 12 May 2026) and abolition of the 50% CGT discount (replaced with cost base indexation). Existing properties are grandfathered for negative gearing but CGT changes apply to post-valuation gains. Key claims: these measures act as a tax on lower-income earners, will increase rents, reduce rental supply in established areas, and penalize investors in shallow regional markets. Location's importance for capital growth is elevated to 90%. The principal place of residence remains tax-advantaged. Webinar on 26 May 2026 is announced. Criticism targets speculative trading and lack of investment philosophy among some buyers agents. Speakers: Bryce Holdway and Ben (co-hosts), with mentions of Jim Chalmers, Dan White, Evan Lucas, David Robinson.
3dn1tg0rYg7QWho Really Wins From Australia’s New Property Rules?Property Now0.4763-0.0475yesyesTranscript discusses Australian government's proposed changes to capital gains tax (CGT) discount (from 50% to inflation-indexed minimum 30%) and negative gearing, affecting all asset classes (property, shares, crypto, small business). Key claims: policy aims to help 75,000 first home buyers over 10 years (7,500/year) but speakers argue it taxes all investments, not just residential; grandfathering until July 2027; opponents say it harms younger generation building wealth while protecting existing wealth-holders. Winners: developers, land holders, new-build buyers agents, grandfathered asset holders. Losers: startups, small businesses, younger investors. Borrowing capacity potentially double with negative gearing for new builds. Government claims rent will rise $2/week; speakers dispute. Context: $1 trillion national debt, NDIS cuts, $250 tax offset for 13 million Australians. Distrust of broken Labor promises on not touching CGT/negative gearing. Includes mention of company structures (30% flat tax) as potential strategy.
4Y83fSCBTZDIHow will tax changes shift the housing market? | Insiders on BackgroundABC News In-depth0.4743-0.0515yesyesDiscussion of Australian budget's housing tax reforms: negative gearing limited to new builds; capital gains discount reduced; Treasury forecasts 75k more owner-occupiers, 35k fewer homes supplied, ~2% lower price growth, minimal rent impact ($2/week). Impact varies by market due to supply-demand imbalances. Opposition's alternative includes $5bn housing infrastructure fund and migration cuts. Older, wealthier Australians benefit most from existing tax concessions; rent-vesting strategy less viable for first home buyers. Key entities: Angus Taylor, Jim Chalmers, Treasury, Grattan Institute, Real Estate Buyers Agent Association.
5Q4gnTeHd1OMMay Budget Changes With Pizza & Property Featuring Jeremy Iannuzzelli, Simon Loo, & Morgan BushellWhat School Should Have Taught You0.4332-0.1336yesyesAustralian federal budget delivered May 12 by Treasurer Jim Chalmers. Key changes: negative gearing on established residential properties effectively removed for new purchases after 7:30pm May 12; CGT 50% discount replaced with indexation method from July 1, 2027; properties owned before budget grandfathered with split calculation; trusts face minimum 30% tax on distributions; new properties (additional dwellings) retain negative gearing and depreciation; companies and super funds favored structures; legislative process includes consultation, lower house, Senate, potential amendments before enactment. Impact on investors, borrowing power, shares, crypto, business sales. Panel: Todd (host), Jeremy (tax/structures), Simon (market), Morgan (borrowing). Key dates: May 12 (budget night), July 1, 2027 (effective date for CGT indexation). Domain terms: negative gearing, CGT discount, indexation method, grandfathering, new home definition, trust taxation, carried forward losses, borrowing power.
6ZdRYP1tfUU8Negative Gearing is GONE: 2026 Budget Winners, Losers + My Predictions for the Housing MarketPumped on Property0.3970-0.2060yesnoAustralian federal budget 2026-2027 proposes negative gearing restricted to new dwellings from 12 May 2026; CGT discount replaced with inflation indexation and 30% floor from 1 July 2027; $2B housing infrastructure package; faster planning measures. Speaker argues reforms are band-aids, not addressing 500k annual immigration or local red tape. Remains bullish on well-located residential real estate citing 8.2% annual growth over 50 years. Predicts rents will rise 7-12% as in NZ. Encourages buying in uncertainty for long-term gains. Mentions HTW, CoreLogic, CBA, Macquarie Bank, RBA.
7P2-rXTmb9hgFederal Budget Reaction with Economist Chris RichardsonMark Bouris0.3489-0.3023yesnoChris Richardson discusses the Australian federal budget 2026-27, focusing on its economic impact rather than individual tax effects. He critiques the 'intergenerational equity' narrative as political, arguing housing supply (not tax policy) is the core issue. Key claims: negative gearing and CGT changes will not help young buyers; older investors will outcompete for new housing. Richardson highlights stalled prosperity growth due to weak productivity, off-budget items (NBN, Snowy Hydro) obscuring fiscal reality, and a mismatch between immigration and housing supply. He calls for regulatory reform (e.g., national construction code) and genuine tax reform to boost disposable income. Overall, he views the budget as a reform attempt but insufficient to address Australia's low prosperity trajectory.
8SZFpO176aVcRBA Rate Hike: Will 5% Interest Rates Break Australian Property?Talk Property To Me Australia0.2836-0.4327yesnoTranscript discusses Australian cash rate predictions (Westpac forecasts two more hikes to ~5%, others predict pause), RBA Governor Michelle Bullock criticizes government spending and energy rebates as inflationary. Claims inflation is driven by government spending (180k new federal jobs, office upgrades) not consumer demand. Notes boomer spending up 14%, rate rises disproportionately affect first home buyers and families. Predicts double-digit growth in affordable markets, lower-end market to skyrocket due to investor activity. Recommends refinancing existing mortgages (average 0.5% above market) and highlights cash buyers unaffected. Entities: RBA, Westpac, CBA, NAB, ANZ, Michelle Bullock, negative gearing, capital gains tax, LVR, borrowing capacity.
9VMemhtlsoNkAgents Don't Do Standups: Building the Post-Engineer Engineering Org — Mike Spitz, PFFAI Engineer0.0704-0.8592nonoCase study at PFF (sports data company) Jan-Mar: two engineers using AI agents achieved 25x more deploys, 10x output, and customer satisfaction 8.6/10 (up from 7-7.5). Scrum replaced with huddles, spec/LDD flow, auto ticket creation, no sprints or standups. Agents handle code reviews on trivial matters and QA. Use feature flags, trunk-based development. Recommendations: pick best engineers, slow phased approach, experiment on non-critical systems, encode engineering patterns in composable skills, start with boring tasks. Highlights need to adapt process and culture for AI era.
10JT3OzDKrucUCombine Skills and MCP to Close the Context Gap — Pedro Rodrigues, SupabaseAI Engineer0.0188-0.9625nonoPedro from Supabase presents lessons from writing a Supabase agent skill, covering three core principles: don't duplicate documentation (point agents to live docs), put critical information (like RLS security checklist) directly in skill.md because agents skip reference files, and be opinionated about optimized workflows (e.g., direct DDL on dev/staging, then use advisor before creating migration file). Live evals on Braintrust (vs Claude Code Opus/Sonnet 4.6, Codex GPT 5.4/5.4 mini) show skills + MCP outperform MCP alone or baseline. Also discusses exposing docs via SSH for agent file-navigation, and challenges of skill distribution. Announces Supabase agent skill release.
VideoChunkTimeCharsText

spending in total we're getting relative to our ability to supply uh that spending. Again, I'd go back to that basic equation, too much money, chasing too little stuff. Best way to measure debt is not gross debt, it's net debt. If I've got a big housing loan, but I've got a bunch of money in the bank, I'm better off than somebody who's got the same loan um but no money in the bank. The net uh of our financial assets is important. So the Feds are close to a trillion dollars as you know uh in debt. Uh but net debt once you allow for the money they've got stashed away in various places is about uh 600 billion. Um, second thought is, uh, you know, well, uh, whether I've got a big housing loan, uh, and whether or not I've got money in the bank. What's also relevant is my wage, how much I'm earning. housing loan, uh, and whether or not I've got money in the bank. What's also relevant is my wage, how much I'm earning. Uh, and and the same is true for governments, right? And and and the distinction is a is a different one. Right? I I if you think one side of politics are genius and the other side are incompetent and evil, then you are a deep disappointment to your primary school maths teacher. All right? What generally happens after the budget is it will go through its consultation period where there'll be lots of different professionals and industry bodies that get involved just to really sit down with the Treasurer and actually go through the budget notes. What is it actually going to look like? What is the legislation? How do we draft the legislation? Once that's gone through its consultation period, it will then generally go through the legislation being drafted. It will generally go through the
Q4gnTeHd1OM155.823-116.4581160
will then generally go through the legislation being drafted. It will generally go through the lower house first, then up to the Senate to be debated. and constructed with those two engineers. It took them under two months. If you're going to do this before, we were estimating it's going to take four months. really delivering what the what the customers had been interested in. So, Scrum did not survive. So, we don't need to have all the old ceremonies that we had before. So, what did we have? It looks pretty basic. We basically had a We basically had huddles. So, those huddles were basically every other day. They'd be like half an hour maybe an hour. We would try to deploy to production as fast as possible in an MVP state and get as much feedback as possible also. And that development flow is we have a spec. I think most of us are probably fairly familiar around this, but we get the agent to go and interview us. We get feedback on that spec and then we make a

fairly familiar around this, but we get the agent to go and interview us. We get feedback on that spec and then we make a lightweight design document. That's done by That's done by the agent. So, it's not a cloud code It's not like a cloud code specific kind of thing. And then those LDDs they get distributed and we get feedback from all of the the engineers. And then at that stage we automatically create all we automatically create all the tickets and then the PRs after that. So, sprint planning we don't have sprint planning anymore cuz we don't need to have an hour going and basically estimating tickets cuz those estimations don't really make any difference. Something we're not doing at the moment, but we will probably have to do once the once the subsidization of tokens ends is we'll probably have to go and estimate the token expenditure to go see if we want to actually spend this amount of money on it. The daily standups we don't have to do

and estimate the token expenditure to go see if we want to actually spend this amount of money on it. The daily standups we don't have to do cuz all these tickets get auto updated. These things were obviously manageable to do before, but it's all been made a little bit easier. Sprint refinement we don't need to do cuz that happens in the spec and the LDD flow and then when we automatically create all of the tickets, we structure it in a manner so that the tickets are made so none of them are blocking each other and if there are ones that are blocking each other, that information is flagged up and highlighted. And retrospective this one might be a little controversial, but we rely on the customer satisfaction, the customer survey. flag when there is an issue to flag it immediately and to instead of hanging on till the end of the sprint to go and go and flag that issue where sometimes I think all of us have probably felt that our our feedback hasn't really been heard cuz it's been a sprint before. if there was a bug or a mistake it didn't really matter and then we moved it to the thing which gets 100 million page views after that. And I think the the type of engineer will there really strive is one who is curious. It's the engineer who if they haven't figured it out, they're going to spend a little bit of time just figuring out how something's been built. They'll be able to still be able to they'll be able to smash this easily. The old style of engineering which needs something really uh prescriptive as a spec, I think they're going to struggle. So, in the engineering side of things, you need verifiable verifiable verifiable deterministic tasks. I think a bunch of people spoken about this today. So, it ranges from everything from all the different types of tasks. But, there's also things which are specific for your product or your org. So, for us, it's feature flag cuz we do

the different types of tasks. But, there's also things which are specific for your product or your org. So, for us, it's feature flag cuz we do a trunk-based development. Um or it could be generating the interactive elements. Um generating the analytics for the interactive elements. Um Next thing is the agentic code review. So, with us, we didn't really like relying on agents to do code reviews for system design and what engineers would usually do code reviews for. We use agents to do the code reviews that engineers hate getting any feedback from. So, that's like the variable names, this doesn't fit the style or like those kind of opinionated manners is much easier to offload. Uh and then you can remove that whole kind of emotional aspect out of it. And then you just allow the engineers to focus on the big picture. Um we still need people involved. But, it's really heavy on the spec, really heavy on the LDD, the lightweight design document where you figure out how we're going to build this. And the thing I haven't touched on at the moment is the product. We're in an era where everyone's able to create anything in an hour. But, a lot of these tools have the brand feel and the
VMemhtlsoNk10690.64-773.56000000000011187
the moment is the product. We're in an era where everyone's able to create anything in an hour. But, a lot of these tools have the brand feel and the product feel of something that's been created by a cloud code. And so, if you really want to get the best out of it, you really need to make sure that the engineering team is spending time making sure it still feels like basically every other product from the company. So, how would I recommend attacking this? You should go see your engineering development life cycle like a like a kind of a like a kind of a factory. So, you should think you're in a factory. How do you break that up into the each small composable element? So, in a car factory, there's one thing which is building a building a building a a door, another one fitting a steering wheel. The exact same thing happens for engineering, right? So, you have the branch name, you've got uh creating feature flags with trunk-based development, you've got the if you build APIs with a specific software design a pattern, you should abstract that into a composable skill. The one thing I would flag is I'm not a big fan of um a consuming other people's skills if they have strong

a composable skill. The one thing I would flag is I'm not a big fan of um a consuming other people's skills if they have strong software kind of opinions that are in contrast to the today's engineering org cuz you're just going to kind of end up in issues. So, you just need to make sure that any skills that are getting composed are still kind of matching. Uh And yeah, this is just at the moment pretty much everything is fully autonomous from the spec, LDD, ticket from the PR. Uh we have a QA process also. So, what happens is um whenever we merge a PR, it automatically deploys onto staging. When the deployment on staging has happened, we will spin up a a QA agent which has a look at all of the tickets that have happened, has a look at the acceptance criteria, and it will go and then QA against that. And then if everything's a pass, I mean, great. And and then if everything hasn't, it will flag what those items are. And the bit we haven't gone and done at the moment, but I'm um I'm but I'm aiming in the next um a couple months is to then have an agent have a look at those tickets, find out where the acceptance criteria hasn't happened, and then automatically create the PRs.
VMemhtlsoNk12829.8-903.921167
have a look at those tickets, find out where the acceptance criteria hasn't happened, and then automatically create the PRs. And so, then you get into this flow where agents can basically uh self-heal. And the cool thing about that is it lets us do multiple things in a parallel cuz we're now in a position where we basically trust agents as well. Uh where do you still use people? I mean, I've experienced this, but they do like to use they they do like to use they like to use shortcuts. Um So, got to make sure the the the security side of things is behaving. A product feel, I spoke about that a little bit earlier. And then the scale and engineering complexity for task, the LDD is really meant to help on this. So, I think we've all experienced where it'll make a thousand lines of code or over-engineer something. If you're really prescriptive on that engineering document at the start, you can really uh prevent that from happening. So, what things should you go and aim for? You should start with boring repetitive tasks, ideally things engineers hate cuz you're going to get the most amount of buy-in from that. Remove as much redundant process as possible.
VMemhtlsoNk13894.4-965.9591194
engineers hate cuz you're going to get the most amount of buy-in from that. Remove as much redundant process as possible. And I know it can be strange when you've been doing something for like a two decades plus to just uh chuck it all out, but I would ask you, what is the purpose of this meeting? What's the purpose of this a process? Is it just cuz everyone else has been going and doing it before, or is it cuz it actually helps out? Uh make sure your team's a personal kind of engineering culture and the patterns are encoded in skills. So, if there are software design patterns that you really do, so for us, whenever we build an API, we focus on the service repository pattern. Uh make sure your guardrails are fully functional before you get into before you get into an autonomous flow. And you should start out with the best engineers. Things you should not do is to try and onboard everyone at the exact same at time. Uh I think a big reason why a lot of these things haven't really panned out is cuz we've given everyone a cloud code codex and given them a hackathon and like, "Sweet, we've done everything. codex and given them a hackathon and like, "Sweet, we've done everything. They should now be able to sort it all out." It's just not that easy.
