Y83fSCBTZDI00.64-72.321187
Well, if there's one word to define budget week this week, it's probably bold. The government finally showed some boldness on tax reform and its effort to reign in the spending on the NDIS. The opposition was arguably even bolder. Angus Taylor with his back to the wall went for broke with his promise to index tax thresholds to inflation, slash migration, and deny welfare payments to non-citizens. The competing visions set the battle lines for a furious debate that's going to run really for the rest of this parliamentary term. More immediately though, we're going to be looking at how the government's changes on negative gearing and capital gains tax in particular are likely to shift the property market. Will investors start moving away from existing properties and start turning up at auctions for newly built homes? Will first home buyers finally have a better shot? And what will all of this mean for house prices and rent? I'm keen to take a closer look at what all of this means for the property market. I'm David Spears on Naval Country at Parliament House in Canra. Welcome to Insiders on Background. Well, Melinda Jennison is the president of the Real Estate Buyers Agent
Y83fSCBTZDI161.039-132.2391195
House in Canra. Welcome to Insiders on Background. Well, Melinda Jennison is the president of the Real Estate Buyers Agent Association of Australia and Matt Ba is from the Graten Institute. Welcome to you both. Thanks for joining us. >> Thanks for having me. So we know um negative gearing rules have been grandfathered for existing investments. So there's no need for a fire sale I suppose of existing investments there. But as of this week, new investments can only be negatively geared if it's a newly built property, not an established home. And of course, the capital gains tax rules are becoming less generous as well. So Melinda, let me start with you. What's this going to mean for auctions, you know, as soon as this weekend and in coming weeks? What sort of immediate impact do you expect to see here? I think that the results will be mixed to be honest. Um we have already been seeing some of the auction clearance rates pull back in recent weeks as some of the information in relation to the budget has been leaked. Uh however, I think that the overall result will be dependent on who are the buyers in specific markets around the country. So those areas which are dominated by owner
Y83fSCBTZDI2122.799-193.921179
dependent on who are the buyers in specific markets around the country. So those areas which are dominated by owner occupiers are likely to see less impact than areas that are dominated by property investors. For the foreseeable future, I definitely think we will see property investors take a um side step. They will pause to reassess whether they divert or or change their um investment strategy to look at other options or alternatively they'll look at restructuring to um consider other tax effective ways to purchase. And so Melinda, just sticking with you, I if what you've painted there is what we're going to see, does that mean when you talk about owner owner occupied parts of the market perhaps not being immediately impacted, but where the investors are, what what parts of the market are they? Just give us a sense of what parts of the market we're talking about there. >> Look, I think in the affordable segment of the market, we've seen a concentration of investment activity in recent months. We've also seen first home buyers um competing for the same stock uh since the first home buyer guarantee scheme came into effect in October last year. So I think that we
Y83fSCBTZDI3182.4-250.2391191
home buyers um competing for the same stock uh since the first home buyer guarantee scheme came into effect in October last year. So I think that we will see some impact or or less competition in that segment of the market. Um >> so does that mean some of those properties coming down in price do you think in that in that part of the market? >> I don't think we're going to see price retraction immediately. Um and in fact I think that general statements when we're talking about Australia's property market as a whole need to be considered with caution. Um Australia is not a single property market and I think the supply and demand metrics at a local level are very dependent on um you know the number of properties that are available for sale and the buyer depth at a specific suburb level not even at a city or regional level. So Matt, do you agree with that sort of uh take on what we can expect to see in the immediate term as a result of these changes? >> Well, I think that's entirely right that when we look at the the things that are really driving the property market over the long term, it is that balance between supply and demand. And what we're really talking about here with
Y83fSCBTZDI4240.64-301.521177
really driving the property market over the long term, it is that balance between supply and demand. And what we're really talking about here with these tax changes is a slight shift in where that demand comes from. So, we're going to see somewhat fewer investors and maybe somewhat more homeowners, but ultimately there's a lot of homeowners out there. There's a lot of demand still there. And so, when you run that through the kind of models that the Treasury has used and the kind of estimates that we've done show a pretty minimal impact on prices. >> So, again, the follow-up to you is the same, Matt. What does that mean for different parts of the market, established homes, uh, apartments, different parts of the bigger the big cities? What What does that look like? Well, I think there's probably two impacts there. One is just as we've said, you know, this is something that advantages homeowners. They get to keep their big capital gains tax exemption, which is really the biggest tax exemption that there is now in in the property space now that we've we've retrenched on some of these other ones. And then I think there's a second impact, which is that the types of
Y83fSCBTZDI5293.68-352.721169
property space now that we've we've retrenched on some of these other ones. And then I think there's a second impact, which is that the types of properties that people might want to invest in might change with these capital gains tax changes. And that's because rather than trying to invest purely just to chase that that really attractive capital gains tax discount, that discount's now a little bit less generous and we might see some more investors who are looking more at how they can earn a return through the rental income of properties. That's that's certainly what the government seems to have intended. If you read kind of their their budget papers, they seem to be sort of supporting this idea that there'll be more investors perhaps who are looking at new housing supply certainly, but also higher density housing that traditionally has seen less of those capital gains and more of that rental income. >> So Matt, does that mean more activity uh more buyers, particularly investors, in the newly built uh at at the lower end of the market? >> I I think it's really hard to say which part of the market that they'll come in on. And I think the big thing to to
Y83fSCBTZDI6343.68-410.721185
of the market? >> I I think it's really hard to say which part of the market that they'll come in on. And I think the big thing to to remember here is just that the big things that are impacting the property market are really those those long-term supply demand dynamics which aren't really changing a lot because of this policy. Although there are there are other policies in the budget that matter there and also what interest rates are doing. You know that that is a huge impact on how much investors are wanting to get in the market and what kinds of price appreciation we'll will be likely to see. And Matt, you mentioned that the the greatest um tax advantage now is the the owner occupied, you know, family home that's still going to be uh capital gains taxfree. Does that mean we will see more um demand or or uh interest in those established family homes, the bigger bigger homes in in the suburbs? >> I I don't know that this changes that dynamic so much, but I think that that dynamic is still there, right? I think this for a long time in in Australian housing policy, the homeowner has kind of been the goal, the the person we want to support. Certainly, that's what a lot
Y83fSCBTZDI7402.639-467.441176
housing policy, the homeowner has kind of been the goal, the the person we want to support. Certainly, that's what a lot of politicians will will say. I think that forgets a little bit that there are a lot of other people out there who say are renting and who probably are not really impacted that much either way by these kind of tax changes. But when it comes to homeowners, certainly that's that's the goal of this policy. And I think we will likely see slight slight rise in home ownership as a result. >> Okay. Well, Melinda, we know Treasury in the budget have said that they're expecting about 75,000 more owner occupiers to enter the market over the decade. Very difficult to, I guess, model or forecast what's going to happen over a decade in the housing market, but do you think that number is is about right, a 75,000 increase in people getting into the market? >> It's really hard to predict how it's going to change um owner occupier behavior. owner occupiers are always looking in specific segments of the market based on affordability. And I think one of the bigger drivers of where people are able to buy is based on, you know, how affordable a specific market
Y83fSCBTZDI8458.24-526.081162
think one of the bigger drivers of where people are able to buy is based on, you know, how affordable a specific market is. And I guess some of the um changes to negative gearing also do impact first home buyers that cannot afford to purchase in the location that they live because ultimately some of those buyers in the past have leaned on rent vesting as a strategy as a way to build equity um temporarily. Um they sell out in the future, use that equity to purchase their own home in the location that they work near family. >> Does that become more popular? Yeah, because we hear that the stories of more and more younger people doing that, going with negatively geared property to help build up for their own >> uh home deposit. Are we talking about a big number that are doing that and has it has it been increasing? >> In recent years, we've definitely seen a trend towards first home buyers starting with a more affordable option, especially those that live in locations that they simply cannot afford to purchase their first home within. So, it's very hard obviously for first home buyers to build a deposit through savings alone. And so they do look at
Y83fSCBTZDI9515.599-585.5191183
purchase their first home within. So, it's very hard obviously for first home buyers to build a deposit through savings alone. And so they do look at other investment vehicles to fasttrack that wealth that wealth creation ultimately not not for any other purpose than than trying to purchase their own home in the future. So that's an unfortunate consequence of some of the tax changes that um have been proposed because we're no longer going to see that as a viable option for rent vesters. Matt, we know when it comes to supply, the Treasury um and you know, perhaps surprising for some to see this in the budget, the Treasury says these tax changes in and of themselves will lower supply by about 35,000 homes, but other measures in the budget, uh $2 billion for housing infrastructure and so on will more than make up for that with with more supply. But in and of themselves, the negative gearing and capital gains tax changes are going to, you know, slightly reduce supply. Do you agree with that uh forecast from Treasury? >> Well, I think it's just being honest, frankly, about what the impact of lower prices is in the housing market. You know, when developers are looking at
Y83fSCBTZDI10577.76-640.47999999999991183
frankly, about what the impact of lower prices is in the housing market. You know, when developers are looking at whether a project stacks up or whether a green field development stacks up, they've got to look at all of their costs, obviously, or the cost of the land, the cost of the construction, materials, and labor. But they're also looking at the price they can sell the the ultimate the home for at the end of it. And when home prices go down, that does mean that it it makes some of those projects a little bit harder to stack up. And so those kinds of estimates that Treasury's put out about say a 35,000 I think reduction in housing supply over a decade, that's relatively consistent with what we've previously estimated for the kind of tax changes that we recommended. And so that that doesn't strike me as as too surprising. I guess as you say, the big question is these other policies in the budget, including that local infrastructure fund, can they really get at the major barrier to to housing supply in our cities, which are these these planning barriers, these restrictions that states put particularly on apartments and town houses in established suburbs that have
Y83fSCBTZDI11631.519-705.921200
these these planning barriers, these restrictions that states put particularly on apartments and town houses in established suburbs that have made a lot of those suburbs far too expensive for particularly first home buyers. >> I want to ask you both about the impact on house prices. You've both kind of touched on this. Again, in the budget, it says we won't necessarily see house prices fall, but the growth will be about 2% lower as a result of this. Um, Molina, you touched on the fact that it's not one homogeneous market, the housing market. What impact are you expecting then on prices of houses of units generally? >> I think the impact as I mentioned previously will vary depending on which location um individuals are located within. So even in the last 12 month months we've seen such um different performance across capital city markets. Some have moved in excess of 20% while others have shifted um less than 5% at the same time in the same conditions. So supply is one of the big factors that influences property markets and there are some capital cities that are um facing a structural under supply with 30 to 40% um listing volumes rather listing volumes 30 to 40% below the long-term
Y83fSCBTZDI12694.88-767.761170
facing a structural under supply with 30 to 40% um listing volumes rather listing volumes 30 to 40% below the long-term average and other markets um we're seeing listing u listing volumes in in line with long-term supply. So obviously when you've got a structural under supply in markets like Perth, Brisbane, Adelaide, Darwin, you're going to see um that you in a standard um market where demand remains constant. You're going to see prices increase. Now of course these changes will impact on the demand side, but um as an example, I'm based in Brisbane and we have seen in the past for some properties um more than 10 or 15 offers on a single asset. Now, um, in the last few weeks in the leadup to the budget, we have seen the number of offers decrease as well as the buyer volume. However, there's still more than one buyer for every property. So, while we still have this structural imbalance between the number of properties available for sale and the number of buyers that are in the market, it's likely we're going to still see price pressure in those markets that are under supplied. >> Yeah. Y83fSCBTZDI201164.96-1233.521188
asset which is the building. Um that all flips and reverses with brand new properties. I think it's really important and I hope that by the end of this video, you've got clarity on what is actually being proposed, what still has to be legislated, and what it means for you and me as residential property investors in Australia in 2026 and beyond. Now, according to Herron Todd White, the Australian economic backdrop in terms of what's going on in the economy right now, at the time of this budget, needs to be factored into account when we're having this conversation today. housing supply. We just haven't built enough houses in the right locations in Australia for most of the last 12 years. A subdued and uneven economic environment. Now, according to CBA, the inflation remains elevated, which is driven by inflation in services, housing costs, and continued input price pressures like petrol, like energy, like food across the broader economy. Now, what this has resulted in is the Reserve Bank of Australia having to take more restrictive or reactive measures to the Australian economy, and that's why we're seeing interest rates going up at the moment. ZdRYP1tfUU83182.959-249.241181
seeing interest rates going up at the moment. Now, by doing this, it constrains spending, it contributes to weaker conditions economically both globally and domestically here in Australia, and it creates a feeling, at least inside me, of being just [ __ ] pissed off because I know the reason that we've hit this position is because the Australian government and global governments printed way too much money in COVID thinking it was going to be the end of the world and all of this is just a knock-on effect from poor policy five or six years ago. Now on top of this in the broader Australian economy, household consumption remains under pressure. So there's higher mortgage repayments, elevated living costs, and
ZdRYP1tfUU84238.36-303.841189
the broader Australian economy, household consumption remains under pressure. Now I think what the Reserve Bank of Australia and the Australian government would love to do is just put interest rates up to like 9% tomorrow and effectively completely cool off and create a mini recession in the Australian market. But that would cause hundreds of thousands of Australians to lose their home and that would be political suicide. So now they're taking this, well let's change some stuff in the budget, let's change some legislation, let's take some more tax from investors around the country, and let's push interest rates up. developing in Australia according to Michael Matusik is somewhere between 30 to 60% higher than it needs to be because of the take that the local governments, state governments, and national government are taking from developers and the building industry to bring on this new supply in Australia. services limiting our ability to get the right people and the right skilled people into the right jobs, hence bringing in 500,000 new people that are skilled from overseas per year. It's just creating an environment which we should have at this stage of the cycle where people are like, "What is actually going on? Are things going to be good tomorrow? sit out for a while." Now, if we get into the nuts and bolts according to HTW and CBA about the federal budget for 2026-2027, and the tax reform. So effectively, there's four things. There's the negative gearing reform, there's the capital gains tax changes, there's a $2 billion housing infrastructure package, which is putting more money on the fire, and then there's the faster planning and housing delivery measures, which effectively is just fast-tracking approval so that they can get more stuff to market sooner. Now, when we look at each of these four things and how they affect me and you as residential property investors in Australia both today with what we've already got and what we plan to do in the future. The first one I wanted to look at is the negative gearing reform. Now, in this 2016-2017 budget, they're introducing significant reforms. But remember right now they're just ideas. They have not
ZdRYP1tfUU88472.2-533.921166
2016-2017 budget, they're introducing significant reforms. But remember right now they're just ideas. They have not been legislated and they still have to pass through Parliament. Now, under the new framework, negative gearing deductions for residential property will primarily be restricted or focused on construction of new dwellings. So, effectively what they're saying is that we want to encourage more supply. So, we're going to take away the negative gearing benefits not for people that currently own property in the country, but effectively people that buy property after budget night, which is on the 12th of May this year. And what they're saying is we'll give you all of the benefits and even more if you're prepared to go out there and build something for us to create more supply as an investor. But if you're going to go out there and buy the established stock in Australia, then you're not going to get the current negative gearing benefits in the first year that you own the property that you would today. Now, the policy is designed to redirect investment demand towards new housing supply by reducing the tax incentives associated with the existing
ZdRYP1tfUU89525.08-592.441195
redirect investment demand towards new housing supply by reducing the tax incentives associated with the existing supply. So, effectively everything that's currently built in Australia right now is under one thing and everything that will be built in Australia in the future according to this reform is under a new position. Now, in terms of the policy rationale or the reason that they're doing this, they're effectively reducing the attractiveness of investing in established property in Australia and they're trying to redirect people, like I said a moment ago, towards new supply. Now, what they're hoping that over time is that more investors put their money into new stuff and it creates more supply across the entire country in the right markets at the right time. Now, the economic effect of this for investors like myself and yourself going forward could be lower after-tax returns for newly acquired established investment properties. So, if you buy something that's old, you're not going to get the negative gearing benefit in the first year, but I'll talk about what you will get in a moment. The other economic effect is that there's going to be likely changes to investment holding

you will get in a moment. Now, I've got no problem at all with high-quality greenfield sites close to Australia's metro markets or close to Australia's largest regional markets near the city or near near the beach. But, what you don't want is to be buying the absolute rubbish property in the absolute outskirts of Australia's largest cities or larger small towns and getting these tiny 100 to 300 square meter blocks with the property that's now going to be massively overpriced because all of these builders are going to be saturated with new work. Now, when we look at the second proposed change, it's around capital gains tax. Now, they are introducing significant reforms around
ZdRYP1tfUU811641.2-713.641167
with new work. Now, when we look at the second proposed change, it's around capital gains tax. Now, they are introducing significant reforms around the CGT tax, and what that actually looks like is in the past what we've had, the 50% capital gains tax will be removed from assets acquired after the 1st of July 2027 and replaced with what they're calling is an inflation index taxation model on capital gains. They're also saying a minimum 30% capital gains tax floor will be applied to relevant capital gains tax outcomes, reducing the extent to which gains can be taxed below this threshold. So, effectively what they're trying to do with this, guys, and they can sugarcoat it any way they want, is bring in more tax off Australian investors because we've all made money over the last 10 in in Australia, and create a more bloated, more confusing Australian taxation system for property investors with more red tape than ever. This is going to be absolutely ridiculous and there's going to be massive amounts of contention between the Australian government and accountants across the country as these things come into play. between the Australian government and accountants across the country as these things come into play. As with the negative gearing benefits for residential property investors, the negative gearing concessions will be restricted primarily to new constructed housing. Existing investment properties will be grandfathered under transitional agreements, while newly acquired established dwellings will lose access to negative gearing benefits from effectively the budget date moving forward. Newly built investment properties will retain more favorable tax treatment and from a policy perspective, the reforms are designed again to redirect investment towards new housing construction, reduce the demand for established housing and approve house and improve housing supply over time. So, what's interesting about this one is it's not actually going to affect every Australian in the same way and it's not going to affect all of us in the same way on the same year. some of the other major banks in the country where they're modeling this. Um and I've also been talking to my accountant about this this morning. Now, when we look at the economic impacts according to HTW around the capital gains tax changes, there's going to be a likely moderation in investment demand for established residential property. new construction across the country, and how the government actually rolls this out. to 9% and completely cool off the economy. growth, or over 10% per annum. So, when I'm saying it's going to double in 15 years time, it's not me just guessing or pulling a number out of the air.
